event_title: Prohibition began in the United States
description: One minute after midnight, the Volstead Act became law, making it illegal to possess alcohol in the United States.
The Polar Bear Club in Vancouver began its tradition of New Year's Day swims.
The annual Nieuwjaarsduik (New Year's Dive) tradition started in Scheveningen, the Netherlands, where thousands charge into the icy North Sea.
The 2nd of January is always a bank holiday in Scotland, extending the Hogmanay (Scottish New Year's Day) celebrations.

In America, January 2nd is designated as National Motivation And Inspiration Day, a day to help Americans become "the very best U that U can be", establish realistic annual goals and work meaningfully towards the achievement of those goals.
Marvin Stone patented the drinking straw. Initially, it was hand-rolled paper covered in paraffin. The process became mechanized in 1906, and the 'bendy straw' was invented 49 years later.

Thomas Edison demonstrated the kinetophone, a device that combined moving images and sounds in a public setting, paving the way for 'talkies'. Early challenges included syncing picture and sound, and the poor quality of the phonograph sound. A fire in 1914 destroyed Edison's masters, leading to the abandonment of the system until sound film was resurrected in the 1930s.

Pope Leo X excommunicated Martin Luther from the Catholic Church. Luther had complained about the sale of indulgences and perceived corruption within the Church. His excommunication inadvertently led to the formation of various Protestant churches. Despite being an outlaw, Luther survived, married, had children, and established his church.

The FTSE 100 share index, comprising the 100 top companies on the London Stock Exchange, began trading with an initial value of 1000. Its purpose was to measure the overall performance of stocks and shares in London, succeeding the FT 30 index.
Sir Isaac Newton, who would go on to make one of the most important scientific discoveries of all time, was born in a Lincolnshire hamlet.
The Burj Khalifa, the world's tallest skyscraper, was inaugurated in Dubai. It stands over 829.8 metres tall with 163 storeys and holds no fewer than seven world records.

event_title: Joma Shinji Japanese New Year archery ritual
description: The Joma Shinji Japanese New Year archery ritual, based on the Japanese belief that arrows have the power to banish evil, takes place at the Tsurugaoka Hachimangu Shrine in Kamakura. The ceremony dates back to between 1185 and 1333, when Kamakura was Japan's military capital. Archers dressed as samurai shoot at circular targets 27 metres away, with each hit thought to drive the devil away.

event_title: Harbin Ice and Snow Sculpture Festival begins
description: The Harbin Ice and Snow Sculpture Festival begins in the chilly north of China, illuminating a city in a blaze of light and ice. The festival features snow sculptures, ice lanterns, ice castles, skiing, figure skating, snow football, and everything from buildings to animals carved out of illuminated ice. Some ice buildings are as much as 50 metres high, and some snow sculptures are as long as two football pitches. The festival apparently began with local fishermen making ice lanterns for ice-fishing.

The British newspaper, the Daily Mail, went international – transoceanic – on this day in 1944. The Transatlantic Daily Mail, targeting American readers, lasted an impressive two years. Published weekly, it became the first-ever newspaper to cross the ocean. Since its establishment in 1896, the Daily Mail has had a chequered history, with its owner Viscount Rothermere praising Hitler in 1933 and supporting Britain's homegrown fascists.

event_title: Death of Alexander Sizonenko, world's tallest pro basketball player
description: On this day in 2012, the Soviet pro basketball player, Alexander Sizonenko, died in hospital in Saint Petersburg, aged 52. He was officially the world's tallest man in 1991 and the world's tallest ever pro basketball player. Sizonenko never stopped growing throughout his life, reaching over 2.5m tall when he died and wearing size 62 shoes. During his life, he took roles in films.

event_title: The anniversary of Fernand Petiot's death
description: Fernand Petiot was a legendary bartender credited with inventing the Bloody Mary. He served drinks to every US President from 1934 to 1972 (except Lyndon Johnson). Petiot began his bartending career in Paris at Harry's New York Bar, moved to the US in 1925, and bartended at the St Regis Hotel bar until his retirement in 1966. He passed away in Canton City, Ohio.

event_title: El Día de los Reyes (Three Kings Day)
description: In Mexico, Spain, and some Latin American countries, January 6th is known as 'El Día de los Reyes' (The Day of the Kings) or 'El Dia de Reyes' (Three Kings Day). It is the most important day of the festive period, when gifts are traditionally exchanged. The day is celebrated with Rosca de Reyes (King's Cake) in Mexico and Roscón in Spain, a round sweet cake-like bread shaped to signify a king's crown and baked with a small figurine inside representing baby Jesus.

A day to celebrate those lovable small collectable figurines with wobbling oversized heads compared to their bodies. They are called bobbleheads but are also known as nodders, wobblers, or wacky wobblers.

Eastern Orthodox Christmas Day is celebrated on January 7th by some churches following the Julian calendar, marking the birth of Jesus. Many Orthodox churches, including those in America, Albania, Bulgaria, Finland, Greece, and Romania, switched to the Revised Julian Calendar in the early 20th century, celebrating on December 25th. Ukraine recognized December 25th as Christmas Day in 2017, with the first celebration on that date occurring in 2023.
It is the birthday of shoe designer Christian Louboutin, known for his shoe artistry, trademark red-lacquered soles, and killer heels.

event_title: Anniversary of the H-Bomb's formal debut
description: On this day in 1953, President Truman announced that the U.S. had developed the H-bomb, or thermonuclear weapon, as part of his final State of the Union address. This bomb could reach temperatures of 50 million degrees Fahrenheit and inflict exponentially more destruction than the bomb dropped on Hiroshima.

The man who was to become "The King of Rock 'n' Roll" was born on this day in 1935 in a two-room house in Tupelo, Mississippi. His twin brother, Jessie Garon, was stillborn, leaving Elvis to grow up as an only child. During his childhood he was influenced by pop, country music, gospel and R&B, which he fused together to create a pioneering sound that propelled him to international stardom. He starred in his first movie, Love Me Tender, in 1956 and went on to star in over 30 more. It has been estimated that he has sold one billion records, more than anyone else in record-industry history. He supposedly died at his home in Graceland, aged just 42.

Born David Robert Jones this day in January 1947, David Bowie constantly reinvented himself. He was, in turn, a psychedelic folkster, glam rocker, electronica pioneer, actor and, well, icon. Bowie's unique, compelling style shaped looks and fashions in the 1970s as decisively as Madonna would a decade or so later. And the opening salvo of Space Oddity remains recognisable to folk of all ages. Bowie sadly died of cancer on 10th January 2016, just two days after his 69th birthday and the release of his twenty-fifth studio album Blackstar.

event_title: Anniversary of the Kegworth Air Crash
description: On this day in 1989, a Boeing 737 crash-landed on England's M1 motorway, after narrowly missing the village of Kegworth. Amazingly, given the plane split into three pieces and that the motorway is busy, only 46 people were killed - 80 people on the plane survived the impact, and drivers somehow managed to avoid the crash. One of the plane's two engines caught fire on the way out of Heathrow, forcing the crew to divert to East Midlands Airport for an emergency landing. In the confusion, the crew shut down the wrong engine, causing it to crash. One expert calculated the risk of dying on any given flight at one in seven million.

Today celebrates the humble apricot, a fruit cultivated in China and central Asia as far back as 2000 B.C. and later introduced to Greece by Alexander the Great. English and Spanish settlers brought apricots to North America in the seventeenth century. Today, most of the world's apricots are grown in the U.S.

James Sheridan and Horatio Bottomley launched a four-page pamphlet called the London Financial Guide, which a month later was renamed the Financial Times. Five years later, it adopted its iconic salmon-coloured hue.

Richard Nixon, the 37th US President, was born in small-town California. He achieved pioneering environmental and equality legislation, helped get the US out of Vietnam, and is remembered for the Watergate Scandal, which led to his resignation in 1974.

event_title: 3D printing of bones for medical repair
description: A research team released results showing that fake bones "grown" from ceramic powder in a 3D printer could be used to repair injuries. The ceramic scaffold was inserted into the body, allowing new bone to grow over it.

George Gibson Polley, known as the Human Fly, an American urban climber, was arrested on the 30th floor while attempting to climb the 57-floor Woolworth building in New York City. Over his career, Polley climbed over two thousand buildings.

Music legend David Bowie lost his 18-month battle with liver cancer, two days after his sixty-ninth birthday. Born David Robert Jones on January 8th 1947, he became one of the most influential musicians of the twentieth century. He began his career in the 1960s, releasing "Space Oddity" in 1969. In 1972, he introduced his alter ego Ziggy Stardust. Bowie was a singer-songwriter and actor, known for his role as Jareth in the 1986 film Labyrinth. He was awarded 10 platinum, 11 gold, and 8 silver album certifications, and released 11 number one albums. He was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 1996 and received the Grammy Lifetime Achievement Award in 2006. He declined a CBE in 2000 and a knighthood in 2003.

The London Underground, the world's first underground railway, made its debut as the Metropolitan Railway. It ran gas-lit wooden carriages hauled by steam locomotives from Paddington Station to Farringdon Street, stopping at Edgware Road, Baker Street, Portland Road, Gower Street, and King's Cross. 38,000 people rode on the first day, and by 1880, it was carrying 40 million passengers a year.

The British submarine HMS Tiptoe ran aground on a mudbank in the River Clyde. The submarine had been named by Winston Churchill. This was not its first incident; in 1955, Tiptoe hit a steamship in Tromsø Harbour, Norway, and in 1965, it had an incident with HMS Yarmouth off Portland. When it was retired in 1969, a ballerina danced on its deck in honour of its name.
Milk was first decanted into bottles and delivered in a more sanitary fashion, rather than being scooped from a large bucket.

Albert Hofmann, a Swiss chemist and the inventor of LSD, was born. He later synthesized LSD in 1938 and accidentally took the world's first acid trip in 1943. He became a passionate campaigner for the therapeutic use of LSD.

Possibly one of mankind's oldest sweet treats, marzipan is a confection made from finely ground almonds, sugar or honey and eggs. Its origins are debated, with claims from Persia and Europe, such as fifteenth-century Germany where bakers used ground almonds when flour was scarce.
Joe Frazier, one of the legends of boxing and a great heavyweight, was born in 1944. He inflicted Muhammad Ali's first defeat during the 'Fight of the Century' and won an Olympic boxing gold medal.

In 1967, 73-year-old Psychology Professor James Bedford became the first person to be cryogenically frozen in the hope of future revival. He is still cryopreserved in liquid nitrogen, and the anniversary is celebrated as Bedford Day in the cryonics community.
Howard Stern, the original shock jock, was born in 1954. He is known for his broadcasting career, controversial humor, and a brief candidacy for Governor of New York in 1994.
The Lincoln Near Earth Asteroid Research observatories in New Mexico discovered the asteroid 2004 AS1, which was initially predicted to have a high chance of hitting Earth.
Johnny Cash famously recorded the album At Folsom Prison in the prison itself.
Humphrey Bogart died at the age of 57 from cancer of the oesophagus.

event_title: Birth of Norman Keith Collins (Sailor Jerry)
description: Norman Keith Collins (1911-1973), better known as Sailor Jerry, the famous tattoo artist, was born.

A giant molasses tank exploded in Boston, flattening homes, shops and people, and flooding the neighborhood in a tide of molasses. More than 20 people died, and over 150 were injured. The 10 million litres of molasses formed a lethal, sticky and fast-moving tidal wave, reaching around eight metres high and moving at over 30 miles per hour.

event_title: Birth of Martin Luther King Jr.
description: Martin Luther King Jr. was born in Atlanta, Georgia.

Salvatore 'The Beast' Riina, one of the century's most-feared Mob bosses and killer of Judge Giovanni Falcone, was apprehended by carabinieri. He was later convicted of life imprisonment for over one hundred murders, including 17 in a single terrorist-style train bombing. His imprisonment led to a significant decline in the power and violence of the Palermo Mafia.
Snoop Doggy Dog (Calvin Broadus) released his song 'Gin & Juice'.

event_title: International Hot and Spicy Food Day
description: A day to celebrate hot spice. Chili peppers contain chemicals that aid blood circulation, reduce cholesterol and lower the risk of heart attack by dilating blood vessels and so reducing blood pressure.

Supermodel Kate Moss was born. She first appeared on the cover of British Vogue in 1993 and has since been on it over 30 times, also having campaigns with Gucci, Versace, Calvin Klein, Chanel, Dior and Burberry.

event_title: Ivan IV 'The Terrible' crowned 1st Tzar of Moscow
description: Ivan IV 'The Terrible' crowned himself 1st Tzar of Moscow. He was an insatiable reader, had eight wives, was a devout Christian, and a champion of the poor and oppressed. He transformed his country from a small medieval state into a wealthy empire.
A.A. Milne, the creator of Winnie the Pooh, Eeyore, and Piglet, was born on this day, which is now officially Winnie the Pooh Day.
James Cook became the first European to discover the Hawaiian Islands in 1778, observing an unknown archipelago rise out of the Pacific Ocean.

A day dedicated to celebrating popcorn, the classic cinema snack. Popcorn kernels discovered in 1948 in New Mexico were carbon-dated to be over 5,000 years old. Popcorn was used by Aztecs and Native Americans. The first commercial popcorn machine was created by Charles Cretors in 1885, and microwave popcorn bags appeared about 100 years later.

Janis Joplin, one of the first rock stars to form the 27 Club, was born in Texas. Her performances at Monterey, Woodstock, and aboard the Festival Express train made her a counter-culture icon. She was immortalized by Leonard Cohen who wrote Chelsea Hotel for her.

Journalist, politician, and freedom campaigner John Wilkes became an outlaw in the UK for publishing a pornographic and arguably libellous poem. He fled to Paris, but later returned to London and jail. He eventually recovered impressively, becoming Lord Mayor of London and a popular figure in America, continuing his campaign for religious and social freedom.

Edgar Allan Poe, the Massachusetts-born poet, was born on this day. Known for his gloomy works like 'The Raven', he was abandoned by his father, orphaned, and widowed. His poetry is loved worldwide, and an anonymous 'Poe Toaster' traditionally visits his grave annually.

Barack Obama made history by being the first president to fail to say the oath correctly during his inauguration, so he had to repeat it the following day, and wasn't officially sworn in as the 44th President until 21 January.
A day to celebrate the art of the one-liner, short, snappy statements that pack a punch, used for centuries to make people laugh or think.
A festival in The Dominican Republic honouring Altagracia, the patron saint, based on an interpretation of the Virgin Mary, associated with stories of her portrait and an abundant orange harvest.
Felix Kir, a French priest, led a resistance group during WWII against the German army stealing wine. He later became the Mayor of Dijon from 1945 until his death in 1968 at the age of 92.

Today is St Vincent's Day, honoring the patron saint of winemakers. St. Vincent, born in France, became Deacon of Saragossa in Aragon, Spain, and was martyred under the Roman Emperor Diocletian, tortured and thrown into the sea.
Today is the day to celebrate the deliciousness that is pie, whether sweet or savoury. It's thought that as many as 186 million pies are sold in US grocery stores annually.
The Hennepin Avenue Bridge started out as a toll bridge on this day, linking Hennepin Avenue with Nicollet Island in Minneapolis, Minnesota. Its official name is Father Louis Hennepin Bridge.
On this day, Kim Philby, a high-ranking operative in MI6 and a KGB informant, left his wife in Beirut and fled to Moscow.
The Gottfried Krueger Brewery in Richmond, Virginia, marketed the first canned beer, after almost three decades of solid experimentation to produce a can that would stand the pressure of carbonation.
It is the anniversary of the death of Winston Churchill, the great war leader, cigar smoker, tactician, politician, historian.
Robert Burns, Scotland's national poet and author of 'Auld Lang Syne', was born on this day.
Scots worldwide celebrate Burns Night with suppers, haggis, bagpipes, whisky toasts, and readings to honor Robert Burns.
A celebration of Irish Coffee, originating in America, though the specific reason for this date is unknown.

event_title: First Fleet arrives in Sydney Cove
description: The First Fleet of convict ships arrived at Sydney Cove, marking the anniversary celebrated as Australia Day.

event_title: Constitution of India comes into force
description: The Constitution of India came into force, making India a true republic and a parliamentary democracy, 20 years after India declared its independence.
Lotus launched 1-2-3, the first commercially successful spreadsheet application, transforming the worlds of work and business.

Today pays homage to chocolate cake. In 1828, Coenraad van Houten developed a method to extract fat from cacao, transforming chocolate into an affordable snack. Almost 60 years later, American cooks began adding chocolate to cake batter, leading to the chocolate cake we know today.

Designated as January 27th each year by the United Nations General Assembly in 2005, this day remembers and honours the millions of people persecuted and murdered by the Nazi regime and other persecuted groups during more recent genocides. It is a day of collective remembrance.

Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart was born on this day in 1756. By age six, he was composing and performing for European royalty. He composed a vast body of work including operas, concertos, quintets, and Requiems before his death at 35, influencing composers like Beethoven, Tchaikovsky, and Chopin.

On this day in 1910, Thomas Crapper, a London plumber and sanitary engineer, passed away. While he didn't invent the flush toilet, he patented several improvements to it. His T. Crapper & Co. manhole covers can still be seen around London.

event_title: Siege of Leningrad broken by the Soviet Army
description: On this day in 1944, the Soviet Army broke through the German blockade of Leningrad, which had lasted for 872 days since September 1, 1941. The city had suffered from cut land communication, air and artillery bombardment, and hundreds of thousands of deaths, with a harsh winter adding to the suffering.

The word 'serendipity' was first used on this day in 1754 in a letter written by Horace Walpole. Walpole was describing an unexpected discovery he had made by referring to a Persian fairy tale, The Three Princes of Serendip.
Jackson Pollock, the father of Abstract Expressionism, an American icon, was born on this day in 1912. He transformed art with his vast canvases layered in dripped, smeared, heavy paint.

event_title: The 'Great Rum Debate' in the House of Commons
description: On this day in 1970, the issue of the Royal Navy's daily rum allowance was raised in the House of Commons, becoming known as 'The Great Rum Debate'. The debate concluded that rum made sailors drunk and irresponsible, leading to the discontinuation of the daily ration on 31 July 1970.

Auguste Piccard, a Swiss-Belgian physicist, explorer, high altitude balloonist, and submersible designer, was born on this day in 1884. He flew 15km into the stratosphere, built the world's first passenger submarine, and invented the bathyscaphe.

Jane Austen published her book 'Pride and Prejudice' on this day in 1813. This rom-com before its time has lived in the English imagination ever since, influencing works like 'Bridget Jones' and being adapted many times.

event_title: C.E 'Elmer' Doolin acquires corn chip recipe rights
description: In 1932, C.E 'Elmer' Doolin, while travelling in San Antonio, USA, purchased a bag of fried corn chips. He enjoyed them so much that he pawned his mother's wedding ring to buy the rights to the recipe. He began selling them from the back of his Model T Ford before mass-producing them under the name of Frito Corn Chips.

event_title: Queen Victoria establishes the Victoria Cross
description: On this day in 1856, Queen Victoria established the Victoria Cross, which is the highest award that a British or Commonwealth serviceperson can receive. It is awarded for 'most conspicuous bravery, or some daring or pre-eminent act of valour or self-sacrifice, or extreme devotion to duty in the presence of the enemy.'
A day celebrating corn chips, one of the most iconic snack foods.
A day dedicated to celebrating curmudgeonliness, characterized by grumpy faces and sour comebacks.

Franklin Roosevelt, the 32nd U.S. President and leader of America during the Second World War, was born. A strapping athlete in his youth, he contracted a paralytic illness at 39 and was confined to a wheelchair for the rest of his life. He and his wife, Eleanor, had six children.

King Charles I made his last regal walk from St James's Palace to the Banqueting House in London, where he was executed on a scaffold. Despite a lifelong stammer, his bearing at the trial and conduct on the scaffold transformed him into a royal martyr.

This flaky pastry was created in Vienna or Budapest around 1683-1686. Bakers heard Turks attempting to invade the city by tunnelling, leading to the tunnels being blown up and the city saved. In celebration, bakers created crescent moon-shaped pastries to represent the symbol of the Ottoman Empire.

event_title: The Beatles played their last ever gig
description: The Beatles performed their last ever gig on the roof of Apple Records headquarters in Savile Row. This performance was filmed as the closing sequence of their biopic, Let It Be. Cracks in the band's relationships were showing, and though they released 'Abbey Road' later that year, they never performed again, with their split becoming permanent in 1970.
Large crowds gathered in London's Trafalgar and Parliament Squares to mark Brexit, as the UK officially left the European Union, becoming the first member state to do so.
Nauru, formerly known as Pleasant Island, gained its independence from a United Nations Trusteeship.

The River Thames finally froze solid enough for Londoners to enjoy their last-ever frost fair. Someone led an elephant across the river at Blackfriars to show the ice was safe to walk on; shopkeepers set up tents selling brandy, beer, wine and gin; someone set up an open fire, roasted a sheep on it and sold it as "Lapland mutton". When the ice melted a few days later, it was the end of an era. Victorian engineering changed the flow of the river, and the Thames would never freeze again.

William Painter, the inventor of the crown cap used to seal most beer and soda bottles, had three separate "Bottle Sealing Device" patent applications granted on this day. He subsequently established the "Crown Cork & Seal Company of Baltimore".

Groundhog Day is an old tradition celebrated in parts of North America, originating from central European legends about Candlemas. The tradition involves a groundhog predicting the end of winter based on whether it sees its shadow.
Charlie Chaplin, the renowned comedian, made his screen debut in the silent film 'Making a Living'. He quickly rose to international stardom within a year.
This day is dedicated to showing care for avian pals and is an opportunity to feed birds, especially with a seasonal chill in the air and their winter food stores running low.
A plane carrying rock 'n' roll pioneers Buddy Holly, Ritchie Valens, and J.P. Richardson crashed a few miles out of the airport, an event immortalized in Don McLean's 'American Pie'.

event_title: HMS Vanguard and Le Triomphant submarine collision
description: The British nuclear submarine HMS Vanguard collided with the French nuclear submarine Le Triomphant somewhere in the Atlantic, damaging the area where its nuclear missiles are stored.
Fernand Pete Petiot, who would come to fame as the inventor of the Bloody Mary cocktail, was born in Paris.
A day to celebrate stuffed mushrooms as a versatile bar snack.
A day dedicated to raising awareness about cancer, which kills around 9.7 million people worldwide annually, with deaths projected to rise to ~18.6 million by 2050.
Mark Zuckerberg launched thefacebook.com at Harvard, a site for classmates to connect. By 2005, it had over a million users, and by 2012, active users surpassed 1 billion.
Facundo Bacardi Massó and his brother Jose, along with their brother-in-law Schueg, established the Bacardi brand in a Santiago de Cuba distillery.
An unofficial celebration day for the hazelnut spread, Nutella.

Poet, author, artist, performer, and leading light of the Beat Generation, William S. Burroughs was born in St. Louis, Missouri, USA. He worked with and influenced artists from Allen Ginsberg to the Beatles, Andy Warhol to Patti Smith. His life included a tragic incident where he accidentally shot his wife.
King George VI died in his sleep at Sandringham Palace, having reigned for 15 years.
Elizabeth Windsor became Queen Elizabeth II at the age of 25, while in Treetops, Kenya, becoming the first British royal to become monarch while out of the country.
Queen Elizabeth II achieved her Diamond Jubilee.
Queen Elizabeth II celebrated her Sapphire Jubilee.
Queen Elizabeth II reached her Platinum Jubilee.
The British and French governments signed a pioneering agreement to jointly dig a tunnel under the sea.
The Channel Tunnel opened, 25 years after its scheduled completion, and was voted the Best Construction Achievement of the twentieth century.
Fernand Pete Petiot, who would come to fame as the inventor of the Bloody Mary, was born in Paris.

Charles John Huffman Dickens was born on this very day back in 1812. Despite describing himself as a "very small and not-over-particularly-taken-care-of boy," he went on to write 15 novels and became one of Britain's best authors.
On this day in 1900, Fernand Pete Petiot was born in Paris, to parents who owned a guesthouse - he would come to fame, correctly or otherwise, as the inventor.

event_title: Harry MacElhone purchases the New York Bar
description: Harry MacElhone bought the New York Bar on 5 Rue Daunou, Paris, where he had worked for over a decade. The bar, later renamed Harry's New York Bar, is said to be 'mainland Europe's oldest, still operating cocktail bar' and has remained in the same family for three generations.
A day to celebrate opera, a unique and captivating form of musical theatre known for its powerful vocal performances, elaborate costumes, and grand stage settings.

event_title: Astronauts return to Earth after 85 days in space
description: Astronauts Edward Gibson, Gerald Carr, and William Pogue returned to Earth after 85 days in space, proving that human beings can live in space despite the muscle-wasting effects of low gravity.
A Slovenian holiday commemorating the death of their national poet, France Prešeren, who led a tragic life largely due to unrequited love.

More than 73 million Americans - more than 40% of the population - tuned in to watch them perform live on the Ed Sullivan Show. Ed Sullivan, then a legendary host of a TV variety show, had been travelling through London when his plane was delayed by a gaggle of screaming girls. Wondering what all the fuss was about, he came across the Beatles. The Beatles' legendary manager Brian Epstein finagled his interest into three TV bookings and a signing with Capitol Records; by the time they finally made it onto the show, they were already number one.

Carmen Miranda was born in a northern Portuguese village. When she was still a baby, her parents emigrated to Brazil and settled in Rio de Janeiro where her father opened up a barbershop. Before Carmen Miranda was discovered as a singer, she worked in a tie shop and then in a boutique where she learned to make hats. By 1946 she was Hollywood's highest-paid entertainer and the highest female taxpayer in the United States. Miranda was nicknamed The Brazilian Bombshell, and was renowned for her sexy samba moves, her huge platforms and her enormous fruity hats.

Princess Margaret died after a life dominated by an awe-inspiring consumption of nicotine and alcohol. During and after her marriage to the photographer Anthony Armstrong-Jones she was an icon of the British 60s, hanging out with friends including Mick Jagger and Peter Sellers, and partying like a demon on the private island of Mustique.
Fernand Pete Petiot was born in Paris, to parents who owned a guesthouse - he would come to fame.
Mary Pickford first saw her name up in lights, publicising a silent movie called Hearts Adrift, marking her rise to stardom.

event_title: Deep Blue defeats Garry Kasparov in chess
description: International chess grandmaster Garry Kasparov lost the first-ever chess game between a human champion and a computer, named Deep Blue.

event_title: Margaret Thatcher became Britain's first female party leader
description: On this date in 1975, Maggie became the leader of the UK Conservative party and the first woman to head any British political party.
Whitney Houston was found dead in her bath at the Beverly Hills Hilton on 11th February 2012.
On this day in 1900, Fernand Pete Petiot was born in Paris.

A day to celebrate plum pudding, a dessert with roots stretching back hundreds of years to mediaeval England. Made from suet, dried fruit, breadcrumbs, sugar, and spices, it was originally a main course for Christmas dinner and evolved into a sweeter dessert, popularized by Queen Victoria.
The most valuable version of Edvard Munch's 'The Scream' was stolen from Norway's National Gallery in Oslo. The painting was later recaptured, and four men were convicted of the theft.

Spanish Conquistador Pedro de Valdiva founded the city of Santiago, Chile's capital, on Huelen Hill. The site was chosen for its climate and lush vegetation, and the settlement stabilized and grew despite initial conflicts with the Incas.

event_title: Black Sabbath released their self-titled debut album
description: Arguably the greatest metal band in history, Black Sabbath was formed in Birmingham in 1968 by Tony Iommi, Bill Ward, Geezer Butler and Ozzy Osbourne.

He made it big playing Bill Sikes in Oliver and went on to play Athos in The Three Musketeers before he famously died during a break in the filming of Gladiator when he was playing the part of Antonius Proximo.
Born in Paris, to parents who owned a guesthouse - he would come to fame, correctly or otherwise, as the inventor
Valentinus, a saint who lived during the third century in Italy, was imprisoned for performing weddings for young soldiers and for ministering to Christians. He was executed on this day.

event_title: Royal Court in England establishes Valentine's Day as a ritual of courtly love
description: Approximately 1,200 years after Valentinus's execution, the Royal Court in England transformed the saint's day into a ritual of courtly love, a tradition that has continued ever since.
Fernand Pete Petiot was born in Paris to parents who owned a guesthouse. He would later come to fame as the inventor of the Bloody Mary cocktail.

Canada adopted its iconic flag, the red and white maple leaf design, replacing an old red flag. The date is celebrated annually as National Flag of Canada Day. Officially called the National Flag of Canada, it is also known as the Maple Leaf and l'Unifolié.

event_title: Decimalisation Day in the UK and Ireland
description: The UK and Ireland decimalised their currencies. Before this date, pounds, shillings, and pence were used, with the pound made up of 240 pence. The decimalised pound is more compatible with computers and simpler to calculate change, bringing it in line with most other international currencies.

Galileo Galilei, a mathematician, physicist, and astronomer, was born. He is considered 'the father of modern science' for setting up a model of rational scientific thought and research. He believed that maths and physics should govern the interpretation of science, published arguments that the Earth revolved around the Sun, invented instruments like thermometers and telescopes, and discovered four of Jupiter's moons.
America celebrates National Gumdrop Day.
On this day in 1923, the archaeologist Howard Carter opened the tomb of King Tutankhamun, revealing a blaze of gold, jewels and leopard print accessories.
On this day in 1918, the Duchy of Lithuania proclaimed its independence, from Poland most immediately.

event_title: Fidel Castro sworn in as Prime Minister of Cuba
description: On this day in 1959, Fidel Castro was sworn in as Prime Minister of Cuba.
It is the birthday of Scottish author Iain Banks, a genius sci-fi and literary author.
World Cat Day is celebrated, a day for cat lovers.
London's Congestion Charge was introduced, requiring drivers to pay to drive into town.
Giacomo Puccini's opera Madame Butterfly had its world premiere at La Scala in Milan.

During the American Civil War, the Confederate combat submarine H. L. Hunley attacked and sank the U.S.S. Housatonic, marking the first successful submarine attack in military history. The Hunley also sank in the aftermath, taking its crew with it.

Fernand "Pete" Petiot was born in Paris. He came to fame as the inventor of the Bloody Mary cocktail and had a notable career in hospitality, working at establishments like Harry's New York Bar and the King Cole Bar. He was known for his sense of humour and rapport with customers.

A hotel owner in Menton, Côte d'azur, conceived the idea of a festival to celebrate the region's citrus harvest. This event, known as the Fête du Citron, started small but now attracts over 230,000 tourists and features large floats decorated with lemons.
The first episode of the BBC television series EastEnders was broadcast, marking the beginning of the long-running soap opera.

Betty Friedan published her influential book, The Feminine Mystique, which reached millions and became an icon of the feminist movement by arguing that many housewives were unhappy and sought more from life.
Enron's former CEO, Jeff Skilling, was charged with fraud and insider trading following the company's collapse, which cost shareholders billions and employees their jobs and pensions.

Tchaikovsky's ballet Swan Lake premiered at the Bolshoi Theatre in Moscow. The story was fashioned from Russian folk stories and tells the tale of Odette, a princess turned into a swan by an evil sorcerer's curse.
Mountbatten became the Indian Viceroy on this day back in 1947. He was the 24th and the last Viceroy of India, overseeing the creation of the independent states of India and Pakistan.

The Station nightclub in Rhode Island, New Jersey, was host to the fourth-worst nightclub disaster in the history of the nation, after fireworks set off by the band Great White set the place ablaze. The fire killed 100 people and injured more than 230.

event_title: The Smiths released their first album
description: A band named The Smiths released their first album, titled The Smiths, in 1984.
Ernest Raymond Beaumont-Gantt, who later became known as Don the Beachcomber, was born in Texas. He was a pioneering figure in the Tiki culture, opening Don's Beachcomber Caf\u00e9 in Hollywood.
Fernand Pete Petiot, known for his role in inventing the Bloody Mary, was born in Paris.
His old schoolteacher unmasked him as Dick Turpin.
Fernand Pete Petiot was born in Paris.
A day dedicated to celebrating bartenders, recognized as creative, hard-working individuals who satisfy customers' needs beyond just serving drinks.

Barnett Isaacs was born in 1852 in the Whitechapel slums of London. He became a successful entrepreneur, making a fortune in the South African diamond rush, and later sold his company to Cecil John Rhodes. He died dramatically in 1897, disappearing overboard while sailing back to England.

In 1572, Pope Gregory XIII issued a papal decree shortening the year by 10 minutes, 48 seconds, leading to the birth of the Gregorian calendar. This corrected the inaccuracies of the inherited Julian calendar by adding ten days to the current date and making all years exactly divisible by four a leap year.

In Romania, February 24 is celebrated as Dragobete, marking what is considered the first day of spring and honoring the pagan god of love. Traditions include young men and women heading out to gather spring flowers and engaging in a game of kiss-chase.

event_title: Cassius Clay became World Heavyweight Boxing Champion
description: On this night in 1964, Cassius Clay defeated Sonny Liston to become the World Heavyweight Boxing Champion. The following day, he announced he was changing his name to Cassius X, then a week later to Muhammad Ali. He became a hero of the US civil rights movement and was the first man to win the heavyweight title three times.
George Harrison, known as 'the quiet Beatle' and writer of 'Here Comes The Sun,' was born on this day. He was a member of The Beatles and died aged 58.

event_title: Grand Canyon designated a National Park
description: The Grand Canyon was designated a National Park by the US Congress.

event_title: The Britannic, Titanic's sister ship, was launched
description: The second and last of Titanic's sister ships, the Britannic, was launched.

New Orleans held its first Mardi Gras celebration. Held on Fat Tuesday, this carnival spread from Rome across Europe and eventually to the Americas, but nowhere celebrates Mardi Gras as flamboyantly as they do in New Orleans. French settlers brought the tradition of Mardi Gras to Louisiana at the end of the 17th century but the province's Spanish governors later banned the celebrations. However, after Louisiana became part of the United States, in 1803, the ban on wearing masks and partying in the streets was lifted. The New Orleans Mardi Gras was started in 1827 by a group of students who had experienced the carnival whilst studying in Paris. Today groups known as krewes build elaborate floats which are paraded over the two weeks leading up to Fat Tuesday. Riders on the floats toss 'throws', usually strands of beads, at bystanders.

Oscar Hammerstein patented the first cigar-rolling machine. The grandfather of lyricist Oscar Hammerstein II of Rogers & Hammerstein, while a young immigrant he worked sweeping in a cigar factory and progressed through the ranks to eventually edit the United States Tobacco Journal. He used the revenue he received from his patented machine to bankroll opera productions and build theatres.

Borley Rectory, officially "the most haunted house in England", burned down in a mysterious fire. A BBC investigation had found more than 2,000 examples of paranormal activity; elite society folk from George Bernard Shaw to the Governor of the Bank of England had attended seances at the house; a celebrity ghost hunter, Harry Price, had made a fortune from a book on the topic. It was, quelle surprise, all a hoax - and had been since the very first haunting. A local child remembers setting off bells mysteriously, helping the "spirits" to play a magic piano, watching the vicar put highly-flammable phosphorus on the skirting boards and dressing up as a headless monk for night-time strolls around the garden.

English spelling is famously and insanely difficult, and this day in 1953 the madness almost stopped, when a bill designed to simplify the dictionary made it through its second reading in parliament. The simple spelling would have been introduced for younger children, with the aim of making reading easier. They would then, in theory, have had to move onto harder spellings later. But... it never happened. The government, convinced that the bill would pass, agreed to back a research project, which never came through.

British physicist James Chadwick published a letter announcing the discovery of the neutron, a breakthrough that paved the way for nuclear fission and, ultimately, the atomic bomb. The neutron makes it possible to split an atom's nucleus, releasing energy through nuclear fission. During the Second World War, Chadwick worked with physicists in the United States on the Manhattan Project, which led to the development of the first atomic bombs. As we confront the prospect of an increasing number of nuclear-armed nations, we may question whether Chadwick's breakthrough is something to celebrate.

The 256th episode of MASH, a 2 ½-hour long movie entitled Goodbye, Farewell and Amen, was broadcast on this day in 1983 to 105.97 million viewers, becoming the most watched TV broadcast in American history for 27 years.

On this day in 1935, the synthetic fibre nylon, invented by chemist Wallace Carothers at the DuPont laboratory in Delaware, entered production. Made from coal, water, and air, it was developed as an alternative to silk and first used in toothbrushes, then stockings, and later for wartime applications.

event_title: Discovery of the Double Helix structure of DNA
description: On this day in 1953, Jim Watson and Francis Crick, with help from Rosamund Franklin, discovered the iconic Double Helix structure of DNA, cracking how genetic information is stored.

event_title: Wedding of Princess Mary and Lord Lascelles
description: On this day in 1922, Princess Mary, the third child and only daughter of George V and Queen Mary, married Lord Lascelles. The wedding was the first royal occasion where Lady Elizabeth Bowes-Lyon (the late Queen Mother) participated.
A day to celebrate coffee experts and the joy of coffee.
Icelanders were finally allowed to drink beer after almost 75 years of Prohibition.
A ban on all alcoholic drinks, voted for in 1908, took effect in Iceland.

event_title: Partial repeal of Prohibition in Iceland
description: Iceland partially repealed Prohibition, primarily due to trade agreements.
Commemorates the death of Saint David, the Patron Saint of Wales.
Saint David founded a Celtic monastic community at Glyn Rhosin (The Vale of Roses), now the site of St David's Cathedral.

Texans declared their independence from Mexico. This day also commemorates the Battle of the Alamo, where a small group of Texans fought to the death against Mexican forces in 1836, inspiring the rallying cry 'Remember the Alamo!'
Lou Reed, an iconic rock musician and co-founder of The Velvet Underground, was born in Brooklyn, New York. He later became a successful solo artist.
The cult film 'The Sound of Music' premiered in America. The movie follows the story of seven children being educated through song in rural Salzburg.
A day dedicated to celebrating the history, tradition, and craftsmanship of Irish whiskey.
Time, the world's largest-circulation weekly news magazine, was created in 1923. It made its mark with an innovative multimedia campaign.

event_title: Opening of the Barbican Arts Centre
description: The Queen opened a new arts centre in the City of London on a site devastated by Nazi bombing. The Barbican is now a Grade II listed building and an example of Brutalist architecture.

event_title: The Star-Spangled Banner adopted as US National Anthem
description: President Hoover signed a law adopting The Star-Spangled Banner as the official national anthem of the United States. The lyrics were written in 1814 by Francis Scott Key.
Turkish Airlines Flight 981 crashed near Paris, killing over 300 people. It was the deadliest aviation accident at the time, caused by a design flaw.
March 4th historically served as the date for the presidential inauguration in the United States until it was changed to January 20th in the 1930s.

The US Constitution, the first written constitution in world history, entered into American law on this day in 1789. It provided a model for over 160 countries and remains the oldest and shortest written constitution of any government.

event_title: Ernest Hemingway finished 'The Old Man and the Sea'
description: Ernest Hemingway completed writing his classic novella, 'The Old Man and the Sea', in Cuba in 1951. The book won the Pulitzer Prize and was referenced when Hemingway received the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1954.
March 4th is celebrated as National Grammar Day.

event_title: First nuclear power station in Antarctica
description: In 1962, the USA activated the first and only nuclear power station on Antarctica at its McMurdo Station. The plant was intended to reduce the need for shipping diesel fuel but was shut down a decade later due to leaks and cracks.
Absinthe went back on legal sale in the USA since its 1912 ban.

Cornwall's national holiday commemorating Piran, the patron saint of tin-miners, who allegedly discovered tin and smelting. Piran was supposedly exiled from Ireland, survived being thrown into the sea, and washed up on Perranporth Beach where he built a Christian oratory.
Riesling, a grape variety, celebrates its birthday.
A day to celebrate coffee experts.

Pharmacist Felix Hoffmann, an employee of Bayer AG, registered Aspirin with Berlin's patent office. Hoffmann's work involved synthesizing and stabilizing salicylic acid, the active ingredient from willow bark, which had been used as a painkiller for over 2000 years.

event_title: The Big Lebowski released in North America
description: The Coen Brothers' cult classic film, 'The Big Lebowski', was released in North America. The movie follows 'The Dude', a character reportedly based on film producer Jeff Dowd, through a story involving mistaken identity, kidnapping, nihilism, bowling, and a severed toe.

event_title: Sir Allen Stanford convicted of Ponzi scheme
description: Sir Allen Stanford, a financier and founder of Twenty20 cricket, was convicted of operating one of the largest Ponzi schemes ever. He defrauded investors of more than $7 billion by claiming safe investments while losing their money in risky ventures and using new deposits to pay off earlier 'investors'.

event_title: The world's first cornflakes were served
description: Doctor John Kellogg, a Seventh Day Adventist, served the world's first cornflakes to patients at his Michigan sanitarium, as part of his idea of a healthy diet.
International Women's Day was first celebrated, marking a day to celebrate women's social, political, economic and cultural achievements.

event_title: The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy Debuts
description: The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy, featuring Marvin the Paranoid Android and Vogon poetry, made its debut on BBC Radio 4.
Crosby Gaige, a founder of the American Wine & Food Society, author, theatre producer, and food personality, died in Peekskill, New York.

The first Barbie doll, named Barbara Millicent Roberts, was sold in 1959. Conceived by Ruth Handler, the doll was initially available as a blonde or brunette, wearing a black and white zebra striped swimsuit and signature topknot ponytail.

Amerigo Vespucci, the Italian explorer and navigator, was born in Florence, Italy, in 1454. He is credited with recognizing that the lands discovered by Columbus were a new continent, not Asia, and subsequently, the Americas were named after him.
Alexander Graham Bell made the world's first phone call, saying "Mr. Watson, come here! I want to see you."
Alexander Graham Bell made a transcontinental phone call from New York to Watson in San Francisco, repeating his immortal words.
The rings around the planet Uranus were discovered.
The United States of America officially doubled in size with a ceremony in St. Louis marking its ownership of the French territory, Louisiana.

event_title: Launch of England's first regular daily newspaper
description: Edward Mallet launched The Daily Courant, England's first regular daily newspaper, in a room above Fleet Street's White Hart pub in London. The paper was a single page, divided into two columns, and focused on objective foreign news without personal commentary.

Lithuania declared itself an independent nation, ending five decades of occupation by the Soviet Union. This followed a previous Act of Independence signed on 16th February 1918, which was short-lived due to Soviet re-occupation during World War II.

event_title: Great East Japan Earthquake and Tsunami
description: A 9.0 magnitude earthquake, the most powerful since records began, struck north-east Japan. This triggered a double tsunami, reaching up to 50 feet high, which swept 10km or more inland, killing over 20,000 people and inundating the Fukushima nuclear power plant, leading to one of the worst nuclear accidents in history.

The greatest manmade flood in British history occurred when the brand new Dyke Dam collapsed just before midnight, unleashing over two billion litres of water onto the Loxley Valley. The disaster killed more than 200 people and washed away hundreds of buildings. Workers had spotted a crack earlier, allowing some villagers to prepare for the disaster.

event_title: Tim Berners-Lee proposes the WorldWide Web
description: London-born computer scientist Tim Berners-Lee sent out a memo entitled "WorldWide Web: Proposal for a HyperText Project", outlining the future of the internet as a democratic space for knowledge sharing. This proposal is widely considered the web's birthday.

event_title: First female priests ordained in England
description: 32 women were ordained as priests in Bristol Cathedral, marking a significant milestone for equal rights in the Church of England after a long battle.
The musical Les Misérables opened on Broadway, where it achieved immense success, winning eight Tony Awards and becoming the third longest-running Broadway show in history with 6,680 performances.

event_title: First known written reference to Riesling grape variety
description: The anniversary of the first known written reference to Riesling, on an invoice dated 13th March 1435 discovered in the cellar log of a winery in Rüsselsheim. The document invoices Count John IV of Katzenelnbogen for six vines of Riesling planted in a local vineyard, referring to the variety as 'Riesslingen'.

event_title: First known attempt at manned rocket flight by F. Rodman Law
description: On 13 March 1913, F. Rodman Law made the first known attempt at a manned rocket flight, in a 13-metre (44-foot) craft he had designed and built himself. His rocket exploded on launch, but he walked away singed and bloody.

Joseph Priestley, clergyman, political theorist, and natural philosopher, was born. He was famed for 'discovering' oxygen and inventing soda water. He provided James Cook's crew with carbonated water for a long voyage.

The bystander effect, a phenomenon where the more witnesses are present as something bad happens, the longer each individual takes to do something about it, came into being after the brutal murder of Kitty Genovese in Queens, New York, on this day in 1964. Psychological researchers investigated the incident, leading to the concept.

A day to celebrate potato chips, also known as crisps. Legend attributes their invention in the 19th century to chef George Crum at a resort in Saratoga Springs, New York, who thinly sliced and fried potatoes for a complaining customer.

Albert Einstein, the father of modern physics and creator of the special theory of relativity, was born in Ulm, Germany. He was also involved in the atom bomb project, a decision he later regretted. After his death, his brain was removed and preserved for study.
A day celebrated due to the numerical representation of Pi (3.14).

Celebrated in Japan and much of South-eastern Asia, one month after Valentine's Day. It's a day for men to repay gifts received from girlfriends. It originated as a marketing initiative in 1978 by Japan's National Confectionery Industry Association.

event_title: Selfridges opened on London's Oxford Street
description: On this day in 1909, Selfridges opened on London's Oxford Street. It's still the second largest shop in the UK at 50,000 m2 (only beaten by Harrods).
International Ti' Punch Day was established in 2022 by Rhum Clément. In 2023, Ti' Punch Week was launched to extend the celebration.

The Roman festival of Bacchanalia, dedicated to Bacchus, was banned by the Romans in 186BC due to reports of murders, poisonings, and conspiracy. It was later revived in a much tamer form around 50BC.

event_title: Birth of Jurgis Bielinis, Lithuanian book smuggler
description: Jurgis Bielinis, a Lithuanian book smuggler who defied Russia's ban on the Lithuanian language and literature, was born on this day in 1846.
The very first FA Cup Final took place in London in 1872, where The Wanderers beat the Royal Engineers 1-0 at Kennington Oval in what is now the oldest football competition in the world.

A day to celebrate Saint Patrick, the patron saint of Ireland, who allegedly banished all snakes from Ireland after they disturbed him while he was fasting. He is also known for using the shamrock to teach about Christianity.

event_title: First recorded Saint Patrick's Day Parade
description: The first recorded Saint Patrick's Day Parade took place in Boston, America.
Saint Patrick, the patron saint of Ireland, was born in Wales (385 AD) or Scotland (387 AD). He was enslaved at 18, converted to Christianity, and later moved to France before returning to Ireland.
A day when American friends celebrate the sloppy joe, a sandwich made from ground beef, tomato sauce, and various seasonings, all piled onto a soft hamburger bun.

A day launched in 2015 by Ranjit S. Baxi to raise awareness around the importance of recycling, its impact on climate change, and the well-being of the planet. March 18th was chosen as it is also the birthday of the Bureau of International Recycling, and it has been recognised by the U.N. since 2018.

Fra Angelico, one of the true stars of early Italian Renaissance art, a humble, devout monk known as 'Blessed One', died in a Roman convent. He is beatified and is the official patron of Catholic artists.

event_title: Last woman burned at the stake in Britain
description: Catherine Murphy was burned at the stake for forging coins, a crime that counted as high treason, over 230 years ago. Her male conspirators were killed by hanging; as a woman, she had to be burned at the stake.
March 19 is observed as National Chocolate Caramel Day, celebrating the combination of chocolate and caramel.
Gambling was legalised in Nevada, paving the way for the state's future as a casino mecca.
The Sydney Harbour Bridge, affectionately known as 'the Coathanger', opened for public use.
Pope Francis was inaugurated, following the resignation of his predecessor, Pope Benedict XVI.

event_title: Spring Equinox in the Northern Hemisphere
description: The Spring Equinox marks the beginning of spring in the Northern Hemisphere, a time when the daffodils are out and the days get longer.

The spring equinox brings with it Eoster, or Ostara, a day for pagans in the northern hemisphere to celebrate a dawn goddess called Eostre, and the renewed life that comes to earth with spring. Eoster is also the origin of 'Easter'.
A day for those who believe in little green men, greys, UFOs, or alien abductions to celebrate.
On this day in 1848, King Ludwig I of Bavaria abdicated. His mistress, Lola Montez, wielded considerable power and drove liberal reforms that made both unpopular, leading to his abdication.
On this day in 2006, 29-year-old Jack Dorsey sent the world's first tweet, "inviting coworkers", on Twitter, a new service he had founded.

event_title: The Beatles' first evening session at The Cavern Club
description: On this day in 1961, The Beatles performed their first evening session at Liverpool's Cavern Club, where they were later discovered by Brian Epstein. They played a total of 292 sets there until August 1963.
On this day in 1963, Alcatraz, the iconic prison known for housing the hardest-to-handle prisoners, ceased operations. It was famous for no known successful escapes.
A day to reflect on and act upon the fact that almost a billion human beings live without access to clean drinking water, despite water being the most abundant compound on Earth.

The UK's famous political sex scandal began building to its peak when John Profumo, Secretary of State for War, denied a relationship with teenage model Christine Keeler, who was also involved with a Russian spy. He later resigned, and Stephen Ward, who introduced them, committed suicide.
Chef Auguste Escoffier created Melba toast at London's Savoy Hotel for the Australian opera singer Dame Nellie Melba, who requested something easy to digest.
The initials 'O.K.' were first published in The Boston Morning Post as an abbreviation for 'oll korrect,' common American slang for 'all correct' at the time.
Akira Kurosawa, influential Japanese film director, was born in Tokyo.
Scientists Stanley Pons and Martin Fleischmann held a press conference claiming to have produced energy by fusing atoms at room temperature, a discovery that was later largely discredited.
Today has been pronounced "National Cocktail Day" in America, and by extension for the rest of us, it is simply Cocktail Day.
Helen David was born in Port Huron, Michigan, on this day in 1915. She worked at her bar, the Brass Rail, for almost 70 years, from 1937 until just before she died in 2006.

event_title: Elvis Presley joined the US army
description: On this day in 1958, Elvis Presley joined the US army as private #53 310 761. He did basic training at Fort Hood, Texas, and was assigned to the 3rd Armoured Division, deployed to Friedberg, Germany.

event_title: Day of Remembrance for Truth and Justice (Día de la Memoria por la Verdad y la Justicia)
description: Today is a public holiday in Argentina, known as Día de la Memoria por la Verdad y la Justicia, to commemorate the victims of the Dirty War. It's the anniversary of the 1976 coup d'état that toppled a government and initiated a period of state terrorism against political dissidents.

On this day in 1989, the Exxon-Valdez oil tanker hit a reef off the Alaskan coast, spilling over 40 million litres of oil. This became one of the worst environmental disasters of all time, killing numerous seabirds, sea otters, seals, and killer whales.

First celebrated on March 25, 2026, World Hanky Panky Day is a tribute to Ada Coleman, who created the Hanky Panky cocktail in the early 1900s. She was the first female head bartender at The Savoy's American Bar, serving a 23-year tenure from 1903 until her retirement in early 1926.

event_title: Beginning of Greek War of Independence
description: On March 25, 1821, the Greek War of Independence against the Ottoman Empire began. This historic event led to Greece gaining independence, formally declared in 1830 with the Treaty of Constantinople, marking the beginning of the modern Greek state.

event_title: Lady Day and Medieval New Year
description: Traditionally, March 25th was celebrated as 'Lady Day' (Feast of the Annunciation of the Blessed Virgin Mary) and marked the New Year in medieval times. This practice changed in 1752 with the adoption of the Gregorian calendar, shifting New Year's Day to January 1st.

According to legend, the city of Venice was founded on March 25, 421, at noon, with the dedication of its first church, San Giacomo di Rialto. Historians suggest its original population were Roman refugees fleeing invasions.
Ludwig van Beethoven, a musical genius who started his career as a child piano prodigy and overcame deafness, died on this day. He produced some of the most enduring classical music ever.

event_title: London Stock Exchange admits first female members
description: After years of campaigning, the London Stock Exchange finally admitted its first 'lady' members, ending almost two centuries as a club for British-born men. These women were not allowed to trade on the floor.

event_title: UK Road Traffic Act passed, introducing driving tests and speed limits
description: The Road Traffic Act passed into law, introducing driving tests (which became compulsory in June 1935) and speed limits (30mph in built-up areas) to a skeptical public. This effort aimed to reduce road deaths, and also brought in the first pedestrian crossings.

American friends celebrate Spanish Paella Day on March 27th. It's a delicious dish, known around the world for its bright, bold flavours and beautiful presentation. Legend has it that paella was first invented by farmers who would cook a communal meal over an open fire in the fields using whatever ingredients they had on hand. Over time, different variations emerged, with each region of Spain putting their own unique spin on the recipe.
Quentin Tarantino, the acclaimed director known for films like Pulp Fiction, Reservoir Dogs, Kill Bill, True Romance, and Django Unchained, celebrates his birthday.

Michael Jackson, also known as The Beer Hunter, was born on this day in 1942. He was a trailblazer in the beer world, publishing his 'World Guide to Beer' in 1977, which set the foundation for the global theory of beer styles and helped spark interest in beer as a varied and interesting liquid, spreading the craft-brewing movement.

event_title: Europe's First Artificial Reef Created (HMS Scylla)
description: On this day in 2004, HMS Scylla, a navy frigate, was deliberately sunk off the coast of Cornwall. After being carefully cleaned to remove pollutants, HMS Scylla became Europe's first artificial reef, now attracting divers and scientists who have found over 230 species living there.
Confectioner Josef Keller (1887–1981) invented Schwarzwälder Kirschtorte, the German name for a Black Forest Gâteau, at Café Agner in Bad Godesberg.
Henri Fabre's hydravion, Le Canard, the world's first functional seaplane, lifted from a lagoon outside Marseilles and flew almost 600 metres.
World Piano Day falls on the 88th day of the year, March 29, because classical pianos have 88 keys.
The first-ever London Marathon took place, inspired by the New York Marathon, with 6,747 runners competing in the English capital.
Physical Review Letters published an article showing that teleportation was theoretically possible, albeit only of quantum particles.
Scientists in California achieved the world's first known example of teleportation, sending a photon three feet without crossing any physical medium.

event_title: Robert Falcon Scott's last diary entry
description: Polar explorer Robert Falcon Scott, snowbound in a tent without fuel, food, or water, wrote his last diary entry, expressing his dire situation and the impending end, only 11 miles from a depot.

event_title: Doctor's Day founded in the US
description: Doctor's Day was founded in the US in the Winder area of Georgia by Eudora Brown Almond to show appreciation for physicians.
Dr. Crawford W. Long used the first ether anaesthesia, marking the reason March 30th was chosen for Doctor's Day.
America purchased Alaska from Russia for $7.2 million, a deal orchestrated by US Secretary of State William H. Seward.

On March 31, 1889, the Eiffel Tower was inaugurated in Paris. It was built as an entrance arch for the World Fair to mark the centennial of the French Revolution and was the tallest building in the world until 1930. Initially considered an eyesore, it is now France's most iconic structure.

On March 31, 1912, workmen in Belfast finished fitting out the RMS Titanic, the largest and most luxurious ocean liner in the world. It was considered "virtually unsinkable" due to its watertight compartments and featured lavish interiors including a gym, a grand staircase, and opulent suites.

On March 31, 1848, Niagara Falls ceased to flow for two whole days. An ice dam formed at the edge of Lake Erie, preventing water from reaching the falls, causing panic and allowing people to walk on the dried-up riverbed. The flow resumed with a surge of water after the ice dam broke.
On March 31, 1999, the Wachowski siblings released 'The Matrix,' a groundbreaking film known for its philosophical questions, slow-motion action sequences, stellar effects, and influential outfits.
April Fool's Day is observed, a tradition of hoaxes and pranks until noon.
In 1957, the BBC Panorama TV report broadcast a famous April Fool's hoax about a Swiss spaghetti harvest.
A famous American April Fool's hoax involved a baseball pitcher who could supposedly throw a ball at 168mph after training in a Tibetan monastery.
In 1984, soul singer Marvin Gaye was shot dead by his father, Marvin Gay (Sr.), after a physical altercation.
In 1973, Value-Added Tax (V.A.T.) was introduced in Britain, replacing the older purchase tax.
In 1933, Prohibition began to ease in the US, making beer with 3.2% alcohol legal again at midnight.

event_title: National Peanut Butter and Jelly Day
description: April 2nd is officially "National Peanut Butter and Jelly Day". The average American will have eaten over 2,000 peanut butter and jelly sandwiches by the time they graduate from high school. "Jelly" is the American name for jam/conserve and is popularly spread with peanut butter as a sandwich filling. During World War II, peanut butter and jelly were part of American soldiers' rations.

Hans Christian Andersen, creator of The Little Match Girl, The Little Mermaid, The Princess and the Pea, and The Snow Queen (which inspired Disney's Frozen), was born in Odense, Denmark. He based The Ugly Duckling on his own experiences. Besides fairy tales, he also wrote poems, plays, novels, and travelogues.

Alec Guinness, one of the best-known British actors of the twentieth century, was born this day in 1914. Best known for Obi-Wan Kenobi in Star Wars, he won an Oscar for his role in The Bridge on the River Kwai and was nominated for a screenwriting Oscar. He also appeared in Lawrence of Arabia, Doctor Zhivago, and A Passage to India.

On this day in 1913, the suffragette leader, Emmeline Pankhurst, was sentenced to three years penal servitude for inciting her followers to bomb a house being built for the Prime Minister. The bombing was part of a campaign of non-lethal direct action in pursuit of votes for women. She lived to see women get the vote in Britain after World War I.
At midnight on this day in 1933, Prohibition began to ease away in the United States, as beer with a noticeable quantity of alcohol (3.2%) became legal again.

event_title: Creation of the Ice Cream Sundae
description: On April 03, 1892, the Ice Cream Sundae was created when a drugstore owner and church treasurer in upstate New York treated his parson to a dish of vanilla ice-cream fancied up with some cherry syrup and a glacé cherry.

On April 03, 1974, the worst tornado outbreak in US history broke out. Within 18 hours, 148 tornadoes killed 330 people and injured thousands more, damaging more than 2500 miles of the United States. This event is known as the Super Outbreak.

On April 03, 1933, Douglas Douglas-Hamilton flew a biplane over Mount Everest, as part of the world's highest formation flight. It was the first flight over Everest, a mountain that had never been summited.

event_title: New Beer's Eve and Easing of Prohibition
description: At midnight on April 03, 1933, Prohibition began to ease away in the United States, as beer with a noticeable quantity of alcohol (3.2%) became legal again.

People from around the world appreciate the many benefits of carrots. While the origins of International Carrot Day are unclear, it is believed to have started in Sweden in the early 2000s as a way to promote healthy eating habits.
This day is celebrated by a group of dedicated fans of rats, who recommend 'bearing gifts and treats to unsuspecting rats', hosting 'Ratfests' and, generally, making 'rats very happy'.
Tardar Sauce, AKA Grumpy Cat, made her way into this world. Her unique expression made her an internet megastar with a brand value estimated at over $50million.
At midnight, Prohibition began to ease away, as beer with a noticeable quantity of alcohol (3.2%) became legal again.
Spencer Bonaventure Tracy, one of Hollywood's greatest male lead actors, known for his naturalistic acting style, was born in Milwaukee, Wisconsin.
The anniversary of the death of Charlton Heston, famous for roles in Ben Hur, The Ten Commandments, and Planet of the Apes, and known for his pro-gun activism and long marriage.
The UK tax year ends on this day, marking the start of the tax year wrap-up for businesses.

The Qingming Festival, a Chinese spring festival, is observed for remembrance of the dead, ancestor worship, visiting graves, and leaving offerings. It is also a time for picnicking, singing, dancing, and kite-flying.
Native American princess Matoaka, known as Pocahontas, married English planter John Rolfe in Jamestown, becoming Rebecca Rolfe. This was the first interracial marriage known in American history.
At midnight, Prohibition began to ease away in the US, allowing beer with 3.2% alcohol to become legal.

event_title: Prohibition began to ease with legal 3.2% beer
description: At midnight on April 6, 1933, beer with a 3.2% alcohol content became legal in 21 states, marking the beginning of the end of Prohibition. President Roosevelt passed the Cullen-Harrison Act, leading to over 1.5 million barrels of beer sold within the first 24 hours.

event_title: Athens hosted the first modern Summer Olympics
description: On April 6, 1896, Athens, Greece, hosted the first international Summer Olympics of modern times. The games ran for ten days, included nine different sports, and only men were allowed to compete.
The World Health Organization (WHO) designated every 7th April from 1950 onwards as World Health Day, a time to focus on issues of global health.
The groundbreaking stop-motion film 'King Kong' premiered in cinemas, becoming a popular icon and offering escapism from the economic misery of the Depression.
At midnight on this day, Prohibition began to ease away in the United States, making 3.2% alcohol beer legal.

The Japanese, using the standard Western calendar, celebrate Buddha's birthday on April 8th. At Hana-Matsuri, or Flower Festival, shrines are decorated with flowers to commemorate the birth of Siddhartha Gautama, who would later become the Buddha, more than two thousand years ago. Worshippers sprinkle baby Buddhas with sweet tea.

Pablo Picasso, one of the most influential artists of the 20th century and co-creator of Cubism, died in his French chateau at the age of 91, leaving a fortune over $50 million. He produced more than 6,000 paintings and over 25,000 works in total during his lifetime.

An electrician discovered the body of Kurt Cobain, Nirvana's frontman and a major grunge movement figure, at his home in Seattle, Washington. He had died three days prior, joining the '27 Club', after struggling with depression and heroin addiction.

event_title: Prohibition began to ease in the USA
description: At midnight on this day, Prohibition began to ease away in the USA, allowing beer with a noticeable quantity of alcohol (3.2%) to become legal again.
National Gin and Tonic Day is celebrated in the USA on this day, distinct from International Gin and Tonic Day on October 19th.

event_title: Finnish Language Day and Death of Mikael Agricola
description: April 9th is Finnish Language Day, celebrating the language first written down by Bishop Mikael Agricola, who died on this day in 1557.
In 1927, actress Mae West was arrested and imprisoned for eight days for her play 'Sex,' which she wrote and produced, dealing with the life of a prostitute.

event_title: Prohibition Eases with New Beer's Eve
description: At midnight on this day in 1933, Prohibition began to ease in the USA, allowing beer with 3.2% alcohol to become legal.
Botanist and herbalist Thomas Johnson displayed a bunch of bananas in his shop on Snow Hill near London's modern-day Smithfield Market, marking their first appearance in the UK.
Walter Hunt invented the "Dress-Pin", now known as the safety pin, in his New York City workshop.
The luxury cruise liner Titanic set sail from Southampton, headed for New York on her maiden voyage.

event_title: First-ever Arbor Day established in the US
description: Julius Sterling Morton persuaded Americans to plant as many as a million trees on the first-ever Arbor Day.

event_title: Prohibition began to ease in the US
description: Beer with a noticeable quantity of alcohol (3.2%) became legal again, marking the beginning of the easing of Prohibition.
April 11th is observed as World Parkinson's Day to spread awareness about Parkinson's, a progressive neurological condition where nerve cells producing dopamine start to die.
Napoleon Bonaparte, the military genius who became emperor of France, abdicated and went into exile on the island of Elba.
The British government agreed that the Straits Settlement, the colony soon to be known as Singapore, could rule itself, setting it on the path to independence.

event_title: Prohibition began to ease in the US
description: At midnight, Prohibition began to ease away in the United States, as beer with a noticeable quantity of alcohol (3.2%) became legal again.

event_title: First edition of Tatler magazine launched
description: The English publisher Richard Steele launched the first version of a society gossip-and-manners magazine, Tatler. Published under his pen name Isaac Bickerstaff, Esq, it featured gossip from London coffee shops and esteemed contributors like Samuel Johnson.

event_title: First edition of New Statesman magazine launched
description: The first edition of the New Statesman magazine rolled off the presses. Established by influential socialists and backed by George Bernard Shaw, its aim was to permeate influential classes with socialist ideas.
Thailand celebrates Songkran, their New Year, with a countrywide water fight. Cambodians and Laos also celebrate New Year today. Originally a ceremony for symbolic cleansing with holy water.

event_title: Captain Richard Phillips rescued by Navy SEAL Team Six
description: Captain Richard Phillips, who was taken hostage by Somali pirates after his ship Maersk Alabama was captured, was rescued by Navy SEAL Team Six.

event_title: Prohibition began to ease (New Beer's Eve)
description: At midnight, Prohibition began to ease away, making beer with 3.2% alcohol legal again.
Since 1916, Australia, New Zealand, and some Pacific island nations have commemorated ANZAC (Australian and New Zealand Army Corps) Day.
The first book by Ian Fleming, 'Casino Royale', was published, introducing the character James Bond.

event_title: Sidney Poitier wins Best Actor Oscar
description: Sidney Poitier won the Best Actor statuette for his performance in 'Lilies of the Field', becoming the first black person to win an Oscar in a leading role.
At midnight, Prohibition began to ease away, as beer with 3.2% alcohol became legal.

On April 14, 1865, President Abraham Lincoln was shot by actor John Wilkes Booth while attending the theatre. Lincoln's life was cut short, but he saved the United States from a permanent split and brought an end to slavery.

On April 14, 2010, the Eyjafjallajökull volcano in Iceland erupted, sending a plume of ash up to 30,000 feet and causing widespread flight disruption across Europe, the most significant since World War II. London's Heathrow Airport was shut down, and many travelers were stranded.

On April 14, 1999, Sydney was hit by one of Australia's most expensive natural disasters: a hailstorm. The storm dumped around 500,000 tonnes of hailstones, some up to 9cm across, damaging approximately 70,000 vehicles and 24,000 houses, causing over AU$2 Billion in damages.
At midnight on April 14, 1933, Prohibition began to ease in the United States, allowing the sale of beer with 3.2% alcohol content.

World Art Day celebrates what is an integral part of every culture all over the world and shows appreciation for the greats of the art world, whilst hoping to inspire the inner artist in everyone. April 15th was chosen to mark World Art Day to honour Leonardo da Vinci's birthday and was declared in 2012 at the General Assembly of the International Association of Art, which is also supported by UNESCO.

Early this day in 1912, the RMS Titanic passenger liner sank beneath the sub-zero waters of the Atlantic and spiralled almost 4,000 metres (12,500 feet) down to the silty, pitch-black sea floor. More than 1500 people would die, either sinking with the ship or freezing to death in the icy waters around her. Of all the tales of heroism from that night - John Jacob Astor, who sent his pregnant young wife to the lifeboats but stayed behind himself, to the Captain who went down with his ship, to John Edward Hart, who went down into the depths of the ship to rescue Third Class passengers, it's the band that stands out. To prevent panic among the hundreds facing certain death, and a potentially lethal storming of the lifeboats, they stood on the deck and played calming, jaunty tunes. Their bravery and composure, despite knowing they would go down with the ship, most likely saved hundreds of lives.

Leonardo di ser Piero Da Vinci, arguably the single most talented person ever to have lived. Artist, architect, scientist, mathematician, writer, inventor and mapmaker, Leonardo was the illegitimate son of a peasant woman, born in Vinci, Tuscany, on this day in 1452. Many centuries before such things were achievable, Leonardo had the idea for a helicopter, a tank, a form of solar power and a submarine. He pioneered anatomy, researched physics and was one of the most talented painters and sculptors of his era. He was also not only left-handed but a vegetarian, and almost certainly gay into the bargain.

event_title: Prohibition began to ease (New Beer's Eve)
description: At midnight on this day in 1933, Prohibition began to ease away, as beer with a noticeable quantity of alcohol -- a whopping 3.2% -- became, as if by magic, legal.

Charles Spencer Chaplin, Jr, one of film's first superstars, who made his name as the bowler-hatted, cane-wielding Tramp of the silent era, was born in London. He overcame a brutal childhood to become a star, producer, political activist and father of 11 children.

event_title: The Rolling Stones Released their First Album
