description: Decca Records released an eponymously named debut album by the then-obscure British band, The Rolling Stones. Over the years, the Stones have sold more than a quarter of a billion albums and became the first band to top the chart across six different decades.
Swiss chemist Albert Hofmann inadvertently discovered Lysergic Acid Diethylamide (LSD) in his lab. Hofmann's discovery helped shape the revolution of the 1960s and he lived to the age of 102.
At midnight, Prohibition began to ease away in the United States, as beer with a noticeable quantity of alcohol (3.2%) became legal.
International Kummel Day was declared on April 17, 2019, following a proposal by Henry Jeffrey.
John Pierpont Morgan, one of the greatest businessmen of America's Gilded Age, was born on this day.
April 17 is World Haemophilia Day, chosen in honour of Frank Schnabel, the founder of the World Federation of Hemophilia, whose birthday it is.
At midnight on this day, Prohibition began to ease away, as beer with 3.2% alcohol became legal again.
The 'International Day for Monuments and Sites', also known as 'World Heritage Day', is celebrated to recognize exceptional cultural sites designated World Heritage Sites.
Lucrezia Borgia, daughter of a cardinal who would later become Pope, was born in Subiaco, near Rome. She was a notorious woman of her era and a famous patron of the arts.

At 5:12 am, an earthquake, probably registering 8 on the Richter scale, struck San Francisco due to its location on the San Andreas fault. The city crumbled, and a subsequent fire left it in rubble, with a death toll running into the thousands.

event_title: New Beer's Eve / Easing of Prohibition
description: At midnight, Prohibition began to ease away as beer with 3.2% alcohol became legal again.
National Amaretto Day originated in the USA where it is celebrated annually on the 19th of April, and like so many things born in America, the day has been adopted by folk in other countries.
In 1965, an engineer named Gordon Moore formulated the first draft of his famous theory, that computing power would double every year. He later revised his prediction to every two years.

Today is National Garlic Day, a chance to pay homage to the vampire-terrorising, Black Death-beating bulb. The Ancient Egyptians fed it to workers to give them strength; it was used as a medicine in ancient India and China, as well as in medieval and Renaissance Europe; and, of course, it's well known to combat all forms of evil. Studies also suggest garlic can be genuinely good for your health.

On this day in 1839, when European powers signed the Treaty of London, Belgium formally moved from being part of the United Kingdom of the Netherlands to nationhood, under the name the Kingdom of Belgium.

event_title: Prohibition Began to Ease (New Beer's Eve)
description: At midnight on this day in 1933, Prohibition began to ease away, as beer with a noticeable quantity of alcohol -- a whopping 3.2% -- became legal again.
Arguably the first-ever film star, Mary Pickford made her screen debut on this day.

The date 4/20 became a holiday for stoners, originating with high school kids in California who used '420' as a code to arrange meetings to smoke cannabis. The Grateful Dead later popularized the code, leading to a counter-cultural movement and festivals campaigning for marijuana legalization.
At midnight, Prohibition began to ease away, making beer with 3.2% alcohol legal again.
Since 1916, Australia, New Zealand, and some Pacific island nations have commemorated this day as Anzac (Australian and New Zealand Army Corps) Day.
Romulus and Remus founded the city of Rome, with Romulus naming it after himself after killing his sibling in a dispute.

event_title: Introduction of the £1 coin in Britain
description: The £1 coin was introduced in Britain, which was met with public outrage.

event_title: Prohibition began to ease in the USA
description: Beer with 3.2% alcohol content became legal again at midnight, marking the beginning of the easing of Prohibition.
Since 1916, Australia, New Zealand, and some Pacific island nations have commemorated this day as Anzac (Australian and New Zealand Army Corps) Day.

Earth Day is probably the biggest event in the green calendar. More than a billion people, in 190 countries, take part each year. It began in 1970 and helped produce the Clean Air Act, Clean Water Act and Endangered Species Acts.
The 22nd of April is also "National Jelly Bean Day", a celebration of multicoloured bean-shaped sugar candies in America. Since the 1930s, they have been linked to Easter due to their egg-like shape.
At midnight on this day in 1933, Prohibition began to ease away, as beer with a noticeable quantity of alcohol (3.2%) became legal again.
Henry Jeffrey of The Guardian and Master of Malt's blog proposed that kummel be dedicated a day of its own at an event with other drinks hacks.
Since 1916, both Australia and New Zealand, not to mention some Pacific island nations, have commemorated this day as Anzac (Australian and New Zealand Army Corps) Day.
Saint George is the patron saint of England, Catalonia, Beirut, and Georgia. He was a Roman soldier born in Syria, and is said to have killed his famous dragon in Libya.

event_title: William Shakespeare's Birthday and Death Day
description: William Shakespeare, England's greatest poet and playwright, is celebrated on this day as his birthday, though he was baptized on 26 April 1564. He also died on this day, aged 52, after producing as many as 37 plays and over 150 poems.

event_title: New Beer's Eve - Easing of Prohibition
description: At midnight on this day in 1933, Prohibition began to ease away in the United States, allowing beer with 3.2% alcohol to become legal.

On this day in 1913, New York City's 60-storey Woolworth Building opened. At 241.4 m (792 ft) high, it was the world's tallest building at the time. It was designed as the B. F. W. Woolworth Company's new corporate headquarters.

event_title: First cloned dog, Snuppy, was born
description: Snuppy, the world's first cloned dog, was born on this day in 2005. A black Afghan hound, he was created in Korea by a team of scientists and later became a father himself.

On this day in 1993, the Bishopsgate Bombing was carried out by the IRA in the City of London. A tonne of fertiliser exploded, killing one person, injuring forty, and causing hundreds of millions of pounds worth of damage, leading to the creation of the 'Ring of Steel'.

event_title: Prohibition began to ease (New Beer's Eve)
description: At midnight on this day in 1933, Prohibition began to ease in the United States, allowing beer with 3.2% alcohol to become legal.
Since 1916, Australia, New Zealand, and some Pacific island nations have commemorated this day as ANZAC (Australian and New Zealand Army Corps) Day.

Australia and New Zealand, along with some Pacific island nations, began commemorating April 25 as Anzac (Australian and New Zealand Army Corps) Day, remembering the men and women who lost their lives fighting at Gallipoli during WWI.

The first Australian and New Zealand Army Corps forces landed at Gallipoli, marking the start of the horrifically bloody Gallipoli campaign, a misguided attempt by Winston Churchill to conquer a strategic peninsula in Turkey.

event_title: Watson and Crick Publish DNA Double Helix Structure
description: James Watson and Francis Crick published an academic paper describing the double helix that creates the molecular structure of DNA, solving one of the biggest questions in biology and opening the doors for genetics.
At midnight, Prohibition began to ease away as beer with a noticeable quantity of alcohol (3.2%) became legal.

Observed annually, World Intellectual Property Day was established by the World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO) in 2000 to raise awareness of intellectual property. The date coincides with the establishment of WIPO in 1970. Intellectual property rights originated with The British Statute of Anne in 1710, initially covering books and written works.

event_title: The Man from Snowy River poem published
description: Banjo Patterson's epic poem 'The Man from Snowy River' was first published in The Bulletin on this day in 1890. The poem, set northwest of Canberra, captured a distinct 'Australian-ness' and has since inspired movies, a stage musical, a TV show, and a 1971 Slim Dusty recording.

This day commemorates the Richter Scale, developed in 1935 by seismologists Charles Francis Richter and Beno Gutenberg. The scale records the magnitude of earthquakes, with a 5.0 magnitude earthquake having a shaking amplitude 10 times greater than a 4.0. Earthquakes over 7.0 are considered 'major', and those over 8.0 can cause widespread destruction.

This day celebrates pretzels. In 2002, President George W Bush choked on a pretzel, leading to a fall and injury. According to historians/urban myth, the pretzel is the oldest snack food, first made in 610AD by a monk in southern France or northern Italy. He formed the dough into a shape resembling a child's arms folded in prayer, giving them as rewards to children who learned their prayers. The name 'pretzel' is said to derive from 'pretiola', Latin for 'little reward'.
At midnight on this day in 1933, Prohibition began to ease in the United States, making beer with 3.2% alcohol legal again.

King's Day (Koningsdag) in the Netherlands has been a national holiday and tradition since 1890. The country turns orange to celebrate, with festivities, 'oranjegekte' (orange madness), canal boat parties, and 'vrijmarkt' (free market) where people sell unwanted possessions.
First established as World Design Day in 1995 and named International Design Day since 2020, this day commemorates creativity and how design impacts the world around us.

event_title: Founding of the International Council of Design (ICoD)
description: April 27th marks the anniversary of the founding of the International Council of Design (ICoD) in 1963, which is the world's largest representative of professional designer entities.

event_title: New Beer's Eve - Easing of Prohibition
description: At midnight on this day in 1933, Prohibition began to ease away in the US, as beer with a noticeable quantity of alcohol (3.2%) became legal again.
Since 1916, Australia, New Zealand, and some Pacific island nations have commemorated this day as Anzac (Australian and New Zealand Army Corps) Day.

Apple launched its iTunes Store, allowing users to purchase and download music. It was the first legal digital catalogue of music to include tracks from the five major record labels, starting the digital music revolution.
Nelle Harper Lee, author of 'To Kill a Mockingbird', was born. Her book, published in 1960, became an immediate bestseller and won the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction in 1961.
Fletcher Christian and his shipmates mutinied against Captain William Bligh, casting him and 18 crew members adrift. Bligh, in a feat of incredible navigation, made his way to the island of Timor.

event_title: Prohibition began to ease (New Beer's Eve)
description: At midnight, Prohibition began to ease away in the USA, as beer with a 3.2% alcohol content became legal again.
Since 1916, Australia and New Zealand, along with some Pacific island nations, have commemorated this day as Anzac (Australian and New Zealand Army Corps) Day.

event_title: Peter Mark Roget signed off the preface to the first thesaurus
description: A British physician named Peter Mark Roget signed off the preface to the first thesaurus of the modern era, his compendium of synonyms saving the day for writers and crossword-puzzle fans ever since.

event_title: Peter Mark Roget started work on his thesaurus
description: An obsessive-compulsive workaholic and list-maker, Roget started work on his masterpiece in 1805 and would put it through 28 more editions before his death in 1869.

event_title: Prohibition began to ease away in the USA
description: At midnight on this day, Prohibition began to ease away, as beer with a noticeable quantity of alcohol (3.2%) became legal again.
Since 1916, both Australia and New Zealand, not to mention some Pacific island nations, have commemorated this day as Anzac (Australian and New Zealand Army Corps) Day.

event_title: Louisiana became the 18th state of the United States
description: On this day, Louisiana was admitted as the 18th state of the United States. Named after French king Louis XIV, the territory was part of the Louisiana Purchase from Napoleon in 1803, a deal covering over 800,000 square miles.

The film 'Crocodile Dundee', starring Paul Hogan as Mick Dundee, was released in Australia. It became Australia's highest-grossing locally-produced picture and received award nominations. The film was inspired by Rod Ansell, who survived 56 days stranded in a remote area.
A bomb exploded in the Admiral Duncan pub in Soho, London, killing three people and injuring almost 80. The attack was carried out by Neo-Nazi David Copeland, who was sentenced to 50 years.

In 1963, the Bristol Omnibus Company implemented a policy banning the employment of 'coloureds' as bus crews. Four young black men initiated the Bristol bus boycott, leading to protests. The 'colour bar' policy was ended on August 28, 1963.

event_title: New Beer's Eve / Easing of Prohibition
description: At midnight on this day, Prohibition began to ease away in the United States, making beer with 3.2% alcohol legal.

The ancient pagan festival of May Day is celebrated throughout the northern hemisphere. It is about welcoming spring, chasing away evil spirits, and in Germany, relief that Witches' Night (Walpurgisnacht) is over. Communities raise maypoles and hold festivities. The earliest celebrations can be traced back to the Floralia, the festival of Flora, the Roman goddess of flowers.

Labour Day is an annual holiday in many countries, often falling on May 1st. It celebrates the achievements of workers and has its origins in the labour union movement's campaign to establish the eight-hour day (eight hours for work, eight hours for recreation, and eight hours for rest).

Beltane is the Celtic fire and fertility festival that marks the beginning of summer, just as Samhain (October 31) marks the beginning of winter. In olden times, it marked the astronomical midpoint between the spring equinox and the summer solstice. Today, most pagans celebrate with activities such as bonfires, gathering morning dew, or collecting hawthorn branches.

IBM officially released 'the world's smallest movie' – a stop-motion animation about a little boy and his atom buddy, imaginatively entitled 'A Boy and His Atom'. Scientists made it by individually manipulating individual atoms, with every single frame shot on the atomic scale, and every atom in the picture being a real, bona fide atom.

May 2nd is celebrated as International Harry Potter Day, marking the date of the Battle of Hogwarts where Harry Potter defeated Lord Voldemort. In 2012, UK Prime Minister David Cameron announced May 2nd as an international day of celebration for J.K. Rowling's literary contributions.

event_title: Austin Powers: International Man of Mystery released
description: The film 'Austin Powers: International Man of Mystery', starring Mike Myers, was released, introducing the 'Shagadelic' icon Austin Powers.
Poland celebrates Flag Day, part of a three-day national celebration that includes International Workers' Day on May 1st and Constitution Day on May 3rd.
A robotic bee, Robobee, half the size of a paper clip, made its first public flight at Harvard University.

The United Nations General Assembly (UNESCO) established World Press Freedom Day to maintain awareness of the importance of freedom of the press and to remind governments of their duty to respect and uphold the right to freedom of expression. It also marks the anniversary of the Declaration of Windhoek, a statement of free-press principles put together by African newspaper journalists in 1991.

event_title: India's first feature-length film, Raja Harishchandra, premiered
description: On this day in 1913, the Coronation Cinematograph and Variety Hall in Bombay opened its doors to movie-goers, showing India's first ever feature-length film, Raja Harishchandra.
Star Wars fans around the world celebrate Star Wars Day, playing on the phrase 'May the Fourth Be with You'.

event_title: Margaret Thatcher became British Prime Minister
description: Margaret Thatcher took office as British prime minister.

event_title: London Evening News 'May the Fourth Be with You' advertisement
description: An advertisement in the London Evening News read 'May the Fourth Be with You, Maggie. Congratulations.' on the day Margaret Thatcher took office.

event_title: German news channel mistranslated George Lucas
description: A German news channel mistranslated George Lucas's 'May the Force be with you!' into 'We are with you on May the Fourth' in 2005.
The Mexican army achieved victory over invading French forces at the Battle of Puebla.

event_title: Mexican-Americans adopt Battle of Puebla as symbol of pride
description: Mexican-Americans fighting in the US Civil War adopted the Battle of Puebla and its date as a symbol of pride.

King Charles III was crowned at Westminster Abbey. Born on November 14th 1948, Charles Philip Arthur George is the eldest son of the late Queen Elizabeth II and Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh. He held the title of Prince of Wales from 1958 and has been an active member of the British Royal Family, involved in charitable and environmental causes.

Sir Roger Bannister set a world record for the mile, becoming the first man to run it in under four minutes, covering the distance in 3 minutes 59.4 seconds. Bannister was a medical student who trained in his lunchbreak and at weekends, and later became a pioneering neurologist, academic, and President of the Sports Council of Great Britain.

Waltzing Matilda, a famous Australian ballad written by Banjo Paterson, was performed for the first time in a Queensland pub. The song tells the dark tale of a travelling bushman who drowns himself to avoid being caught for poaching a sheep, and has become an icon of Australia.

event_title: First successful use of the Heart-Lung Machine
description: Doctor John Heysham Gibbon successfully used his heart-lung machine for the first time in surgery, removing the heart from an 18-year-old girl and connecting her to the machine, allowing her to live. Gibbon spent almost twenty years designing the machine, which functioned as both heart and lungs, though he later abandoned his research.
Somebody in America declared May 7th to be National Cosmopolitan Day.

event_title: National Homebrew Day officially recognized by Congress
description: Congress officially recognized National Homebrew Day, which is now celebrated annually on this date. The day was first created in 1979 by the American Homebrewers Association.
The extraordinary dome of Istanbul's monumental Hagia Sophia collapsed during an earthquake.

George Washington, who had been sworn in as the first-ever president of the newly independent United States a week earlier, attended a ball held in his honour, setting a standard for future inaugural balls.
Native American warrior and general Pontiac rose up against British and French forces, starting a war best known as Pontiac's War.
Coca-Cola, one of the biggest and most well-known drinks brands in the world, was created by Dr. John Pemberton.
Henry Dunant, who created the Red Cross, was born.

event_title: World Red Cross and Red Crescent Day
description: A day to celebrate the Red Cross and Red Crescent, which provide assistance in times of conflict around the world.
UK's Prime Minister, Sir Winston Churchill, announced the end of WWII in Europe after Nazi Germany's unconditional surrender the day before, leading to spontaneous celebrations.
America boasts over 1,200 national days celebrating all manner of things... and as the name suggests, this one remembers dearly departed socks and their surviving twins.

The ninth of May has also been declared today "National Moscato Day". Despite this "National Day" celebration originating in America, Moscato is Italian wine from the Piedmont region. Made from the Muscat grape, Moscato is often enjoyed as an aperitif as it's slightly sweet, low in alcohol and very quaffable.
A day celebrating shrimp, one of the world's most favored seafoods, with Americans consuming over 4lbs annually. There are over 2,000 varieties, and they are enjoyed globally in countless dishes.

Nelson Mandela was inaugurated as South Africa's first black president, following his election by the country's first-ever democratic government. This marked the culmination of his journey and his efforts to dismantle apartheid.
Alfred the Great called together all the people of Somerset to raise arms against the Viking invasion.
The Spanish painter Salvador Dalí was born. He was a leader of the Surrealist movement and later moved on to his classical period, leaving behind a monumental volume of work in various media.
Edward Lear, who took a rhyme form usually used for utter filth and sanitised it for Victorian audiences in his 1846 Book of Nonsense, was born.
The Balance and Columbian Repository, an upstate New York newspaper, published the first known definition of the word 'cock-tail' in response to a reader's query.
Giuseppe Cipriani first opened Harry's Bar in Venice, one of the world's great classic bars.

event_title: Louis XIV enthroned as King of France
description: Louis XIV was crowned King of France at the age of four. He built the Palace of Versailles, established France as a pre-eminent European power, and held the record for the longest reign in a major European country (72 years and 110 days).

event_title: Launch of Skylab, the first U.S. Space Station
description: Skylab, the United States' first space station, was launched as a response to the Soviet space station Salyut 1. It aimed to mark the initial steps towards outer space travel.
Someone, somewhere, quite randomly we're sure, decided that today is National Chocolate Chip Day.
The city of Las Vegas was born on this day in 1905.
Tenzing Norgay, who would go on to become one of the world's great climbers and complete the first ascent of Everest with Edmund Hillary, was born in a remote Tibetan valley.

event_title: Carthusian Monks Receive Élixir Végétal Recipe
description: The Carthusian Monks of Chartreuse were given a manuscript containing the recipe for the Élixir Végétal, which they still produce today.
To celebrate 400 years since the Carthusian Monks received the Élixir Végétal recipe, the first Chartreuse Day was established on May 16th.

event_title: Operation Chastise (The Dam Busters Raid) Begins
description: On the evening of May 16th, Operation Chastise commenced, with a British Royal Air Force squadron setting out for the Ruhr valley to destroy three German dams using 'bouncing bombs'. Two large dams were destroyed, but over a third of the 133 men involved died. Squadron leader Guy Gibson was awarded the Victoria Cross.
Guy Gibson, the squadron leader of the Dam Busters raid, was killed in 1944 after insisting on returning to flying, against the wishes of his superiors.

event_title: National Piercing Day Established in USA
description: Since 2007, May 16th has been recognized as National Piercing Day in the USA.
The practice of ear piercing dates back at least 5,000 years.
Folk have been piercing noses for more than half the time ear piercing has existed.
Records of piercing more intimate body parts exist as far back as Ancient Rome.
The exceedingly ouchworthy piercing known as a 'Prince Albert' has been known since the 1970s in San Francisco.
A day celebrating Pinot Grigio (or Pinot Gris), a grape variety identified with gently acidic refreshing white wines.
Marks the signing of the Constitution of Norway in 1814 and its declaration as an independent nation. Norwegians celebrate their nationhood on this day.

event_title: US Supreme Court bans school segregation
description: The Supreme Court decided that an apartheid school system was unconstitutional, allowing African-American children to attend the same well-funded schools as white children.

event_title: First legal same-sex marriage in Massachusetts
description: Tanya McCloskey and Marcia Kadish became the first couple in the USA to legally marry as wife and wife in Massachusetts.

Probably created by a fed-up and overloaded homemaker, No Dirty Dishes Day is a day dedicated to keeping our sinks clear of dirty plates, pots and pans. But it represents more than just having a clean and tidy sink area and encourages us all to take a break from our daily chores, to marvel at our gleaming basins and to relax and have some fun instead.

Established in 1977, this annual day "helps raise awareness of how important museums are in the development of society." Around the world, some 37,000 museums across more than 150 countries will be celebrating International Museum Day with special activities and exhibitions.

event_title: Jackie Cochran broke the Sound Barrier
description: On this day in 1953, Cochran set one of her 200 aviation records, piloting an F-86 fighter to become the first woman to break the sound barrier. 11 years later, at the age of almost 60, she would fly faster than twice the speed of sound. During World War II, she came close to bringing women pilots into the U.S. Air Force, decades ahead of her time: after the war, she was made a Lieutenant Colonel in the U.S. Air Force Reserve.

When Polish soldiers raised the flag of Poland over Monte Cassino it was the culmination of a struggle that had lasted four months. Monte Cassino was a critical conflict in World War II - some estimate it left a quarter of a million men dead or wounded. Allied forces - Americans, Britons, New Zealanders, Canadians, Indians, Algerians, Moroccans, Tunisians, Nepalis and Poles - made four attempts to take the mountain to open the way to Rome. Along the way they caused one of the worst cultural disasters of all World War Two - they bombed a monastery that was over 1400 years old.
Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis, former First Lady of the United States and one of the most famous women in the world during the 1960s, died on this day.

Vietnam's national hero, Ho Chi Minh, was born on this day. He began his revolutionary career in the hospitality industry, served years in prison for his political philosophy, and worked for Vietnamese independence.

event_title: World Bee Day proclaimed by UN
description: The UN proclaimed 20th May World Bee Day in December 2017. May was chosen due to bees in the northern hemisphere developing profusely ahead of a productive summer while in the southern hemisphere hives are being harvested. Slovenia lobbied the United Nations for three years before the UN Member States unanimously approved Slovenia's proposal. World Bee Day is intended to raise awareness of the importance of bees and beekeeping and publicise the importance of bees as pollinators.
Anton Janša (1734–1773), a famous Slovenian beekeeper regarded as the pioneer of modern beekeeping, was born on this day.

event_title: Levi Strauss and Jacob Davis patented riveted trousers
description: In 1873, Levi Strauss and his business partner, Jacob Davis, received a patent from the USA patent office giving them exclusive rights to strengthen working men's trousers with metal rivets. This act transformed hard-wearing denim trousers into the items we know today as jeans.
In 2013, a category five tornado swept through the Oklahoma City suburb of Moore, killing more than 20 people, including children in their classroom, and injuring almost 400.

event_title: Tennessee repealed the ban on manufacturing alcoholic beverages
description: More than three years after the 21st Amendment ended Prohibition, Tennessee repealed the ban on manufacturing alcoholic beverages, thereby giving control of alcohol to the states rather than making it a federal issue.
Queen Victoria performed the official opening of the Manchester Ship Canal, which was, at the time, an amazing 58km long and the largest river navigation canal in the world.
Observed annually on May 22nd, National Craft Distillery Day celebrates small-batch, independently owned distilleries and the artisanal spirits they make.
Around the world today, thousands of black-clad nihilists will be upping the make-up and partying like it's 1985. Goth culture originated in England during the early 1980s as an offshoot of punk.

A day dedicated to celebrating and promoting the protection of turtles and tortoises, from sea turtles and terrapins to pet tortoises and their giant cousins. Established in 1990 by American Tortoise Rescue, today is a day for bringing awareness to the disappearing habitat of these beloved reptiles and aims to encourage us all to do what we can to help them both in the wild and in captivity.

event_title: Henry VIII annulled his marriage to Catherine of Aragon
description: Henry VIII got rid of Catherine of Aragon by getting his marriage to her annulled.

event_title: Bonnie and Clyde were gunned down
description: Bonnie and Clyde's three-year crime spree ended in a hail of bullets when Louisiana law officers ambushed them.
Robert Moog was born. This engineering prodigy, who founded his first company when he was just 19, changed the shape of music by creating the Moog synthesiser.
Surgeons stuck a ten-year-old boy's arm back on - the first time a limb reattachment had been successfully concluded.
The legendary hotelier César Ritz opened the London Ritz, on the site of an old coaching inn, not far from the Savoy.
The iconic Brooklyn Bridge opened, becoming the longest suspension bridge in the world, the first made using steel cable wire, and the first made using underwater explosives.
Fans of the late Douglas Adams, creator of The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy, celebrate his work by carrying towels.
Great classical actor, gay rights activist, and Hollywood star Ian McKellen was born.
Stephanie Difford, the daughter of the discerning drinker behind this website, was born.
In Australia, May 26 is National Sorry Day, a nationwide apology to the Stolen Generations, mixed-race Aboriginal children forcibly removed from their families from the late 1800s to the 1970s.

event_title: Kevin Rudd's Formal Apology to Indigenous Australians
description: Kevin Rudd, the then Prime Minister of Australia, delivered a formal apology to Indigenous Australians.
The company that would become BP struck oil in a remote corner of what was then Persia, a discovery that profoundly shaped modern history.
Thomas Cook, the world's oldest travel firm, went private after being bought by the state in 1948.
New York City's Chrysler Building was completed, becoming the tallest man-made structure at the time.
California's iconic Golden Gate Bridge opened, linking San Francisco and Marin County.
The French colonial authorities of Guadeloupe abolished slavery in the colony and its dependent islands, including Saint-Martin.

This national day originates from the days before refrigeration when butchers ate leftover or less popular cuts on Fridays to prevent spoilage. Smoking meats like brisket was also a way to preserve them and reduce waste. Brisket is a tough cut of beef or veal, best slow-cooked or barbecued. Black's Barbecue in Lockhart, Texas, became the first restaurant in the 1950s to have a barbecue menu entirely devoted to brisket.

James Bond's creator Ian Fleming was born on this day in 1908. He worked in Naval Intelligence during World War II, running commando operations. He published his first Bond novel, Casino Royale, in 1953, and retired to his Jamaican estate Goldeneye to write. A serious film adaptation of Casino Royale was released in 2006, following a 1967 spoof version.

John Christie opened the Glyndebourne Opera House on this day in 1934, bringing opera to England. He built a theatre and hired talented opera performers, many escaping early Nazi Germany. The institution puts on 120 performances a year, reaching around 150,000 people, and its education program conducts over 230 events annually.
Edmund Hillary, a New Zealander, and the Nepalese Sherpa Tenzing Norgay became the first climbers known to have reached the summit of Mount Everest.
Today is the birthday of King Charles II, the son of the King Charles who was executed, known for hiding up an oak tree.

event_title: Oak Apple Day / Royal Oak Day
description: For many years after Charles II became king, May 29 was an English public holiday, known as Royal Oak Day or Oak Apple Day, celebrating his return to the throne.

Two chemists discovered a new element, Krypton, a mysterious substance that glowed yellowish-green when pumped into an induction coil and heated. They named it Krypton, from the Greek word meaning 'hidden', due to its elusive nature.

This day celebrates the Italian sweet treat, the macaroon. Its recipe can be traced back to an Italian monastery in the 8th or 9th century, brought to France in 1533, and later popularized by the 'Macaroon Sisters' during the French Revolution. It also became popular in the Jewish community for Passover and is now a classic American cookie.

Walt Whitman, an American poet and proponent of the temperance movement, was born. His controversial poetry collection 'Leaves of Grass', published privately in 1855, was considered obscene for its overt sexuality but was an epic celebration of modern American life.

The World Health Organization designated this day as No Tobacco Day to raise awareness about the dangers of tobacco. Tobacco killed 100 million people in the last century, and approximately 5 million suffer tobacco-related deaths annually.
This day marks the birthday of Peppa Pig, an insanely popular pink pig character who is the frontline of a merchandising juggernaut worth hundreds of millions in the UK alone.

Norma Jeane Mortenson, later known as screen superstar and model Marilyn Monroe, was born on this day in 1926. She became a 1950s sex symbol and film star, known for movies like 'Gentlemen Prefer Blondes' and 'Some Like It Hot'.
The Heimlich Manoeuvre, a technique to save someone from choking, made its debut on this day in 1974. It was developed by chest surgeon Henry Heimlich.

César Ritz and August Escoffier opened the doors of the Paris Ritz on the Place Vendôme to a glittering reception on this day in 1898. It became a famous hotel, hosting figures like Coco Chanel and Ernest Hemingway.

British movie star Leslie Howard, known for 'Gone with the Wind', died on this day in 1943 when his civilian plane was shot down off Portugal by German fighters. Theories suggest he was targeted as a propagandist or for a secret mission.
Alexander Bell transmitted the first sound between two receivers, an accidental discovery during work on their harmonic telegraph.

event_title: Marconi received the first wireless patent
description: Guglielmo Marconi, known as the creator of Radio, received the first wireless patent.

event_title: The Beatles' Sgt Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band album released in the US
description: The Beatles' eighth album, 'Sgt Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band', was released in the US, providing identical track listings on both the US and UK versions for the first time.

event_title: Sir Paul McCartney received the Library of Congress Gershwin Prize
description: Sir Paul McCartney received the Library of Congress Gershwin Prize at the White House, an award celebrating musicians who have impacted the music industry.
Queen Elizabeth II was crowned, with over 8,000 guests attending and the ceremony broadcast in over 40 languages to a global audience of 40 million.
Italy held a referendum to determine its form of government, voting out the king and becoming a republic, leading to the exile of male members of the royal House of Savoy.
Astronaut Ed White became the first American to walk in space, floating out of his capsule attached by a 7.6-meter (25-foot) cord and maneuvering with a gas gun for 23 minutes.
The Post newspaper broke the story that White House lawyer John Dean had told investigators that President Nixon had discussed covering up the Watergate break-in with him 'at least 35 times'.
Slobodan Milosevic agreed to make peace in Kosovo amidst NATO air strikes, bringing the wars in the former Yugoslavia to their closing stages.
America has designated June 04 as National Cognac Day.
June 04 has been designated National Hug Your Cat Day, an opportunity to give back to all the cats in the world.

event_title: Emily Davison's protest at Epsom Derby
description: Emily Wilding Davison stepped onto the racecourse in front of King George V's horse at Britain's Epsom Derby to draw attention to the women's suffrage cause. She was hit by the horse and sustained injuries that resulted in her death four days later.
In 1972, the United Nations first decided the environment was a topic worth discussing and acting upon, holding a five-day conference dedicated to the subject.
Allied soldiers landed on the French beaches of Normandy, marking the largest seaborne invasion in history and a crucial turning point in World War II in Europe.
Entrepreneur Richard Hollingswood debuted the first drive-in movie theater in Camden, New Jersey.

event_title: Colony of Queensland Splits from New South Wales
description: The colony of Queen's Land split off from the colony of New South Wales, forming a separate entity that would later become part of the Commonwealth of Australia.

event_title: Gustav Eriksson Elected King of Sweden
description: Gustav Eriksson of the Vasa noble family was elected king, marking the end of the Danish-ruled Kalmar Union and the beginning of Swedish independence, which laid the foundation for modern Sweden. This day is now celebrated as Sweden's National Day.
The author, wit, and journalist Dorothy Parker passed away on this day in 1967.
Alan Turing, who established computer science, helped win the Second World War, and set a benchmark for artificial intelligence, died on this day in 1954 at the age of 41.

Oceans cover over 70% of the earth's surface, with 95% remaining unexplored. Marine species are dying off faster than they have in 55 million years, and 80% of pollutants in the oceans derive from human activity. Plastic is found in the stomachs of various creatures, and global seafood fondness leads to over 12 million tonnes of marine life taken by China alone annually, with predictions of fish stock collapse before the second half of this century.

The oldest film production company in America, Universal Studios, was established today in 1912 as the Universal Film Manufacturing Company, by German-Jewish immigrant Carl Laemmle. He was motivated by irritation at Thomas Edison's practices. Universal survives today in a much-changed form, including theme parks and film production/distribution arms.

At 9am on this day, the Laki volcano in Iceland erupted, causing an epic disaster. It ejected 600 sq. km. of lava, but the killer was the gases: fluorine gas fell as hydrofluoric acid, and a toxic cloud of sulphur dioxide and sulphuric acid drifted south. This killed plants, livestock (wiping out 20% of Iceland's population), and brought savage winters globally, killing at least 20,000 Britons. The toxic fog in England made the sun appear pale and blood-red.

event_title:
Day of La Rioja (D
00eda de La Rioja)
description:
The 9th of June is an annual public holiday in the autonomous community of La Rioja in northeast Spain, celebrating the anniversary of King Juan Carlos I's signature of the Statute of Autonomy of La Rioja in 1982. La Rioja became a self-administrated province in 1833, originally under the name Logro
00f1o, and was renamed La Rioja in 1980.

event_title: George Mallory and Andrew Irvine disappeared on Mount Everest
description: During the third British attempt to summit Everest, climbers George Mallory and Andrew Irvine were lost for good. One of them had been seen the day before on the summit ridge. Mallory's body was found in 1999, but Irvine's body nor his camera have been recovered.

Journalist Bíró László József and his brother, Hungarian Jews who fled to Argentina, filed a patent for their rolling ball pen and formed Biro Pens of Argentina. Their pen was later licensed for production in the UK for the Royal Air Force.

Doctor Bob Holbrook Smith, a surgeon from Akron, Ohio, took his last drink. He, along with Bill W, co-founded Alcoholics Anonymous, a group that conceptualized alcoholism as an addiction or disease and developed the Twelve Step treatment.
In America, this day is designated as National Cognac Day.
This day is celebrated as International Cachaça Day.
In America, this day is celebrated as National Bourbon Day.

event_title: National Corn On The Cob Day
description: A national day celebrating corn on the cob, a staple part of many BBQs and picnics, which has been celebrated for over 150 years. Corn or maize was a key crop for Native American tribes and remains a significant agricultural product in America.

event_title: Troy was sacked and burned by the Greeks
description: According to calculations by Eratosthenes, a Greek scholar, on this day in 1184 BC, Troy was sacked and burned by the Greeks. After a ten-year siege, the Greeks used the stratagem of the Trojan Horse to infiltrate the city, leading to its destruction.
Richard Strauss, the renowned German composer known for works like 'Also sprach Zarathustra' and operas such as 'Der Rosenkavalier' and 'Salome', was born in Munich, Germany, on this day in 1864.

A revolt occurred on this day in 1744, remembering a period when the Portuguese, then the colonial power in Brazil, outlawed both the making and selling of cachaça, leading to uprisings and revolution.

Three bank robbers, Frank Lee Morris, Clarence Anglin, and John Anglin, disappeared from Alcatraz, considered the USA's most impregnable prison. They crafted dummy faces, cut through their cells, climbed through a ventilation duct, and made a raft to sail away, never to be seen again.
This day is celebrated as Dia dos Namorados, Brazil's equivalent to Valentine's Day.
This day is celebrated as International Cachaça Day, a celebration that seems to have started in America.

The sewing machine is one of man's greatest inventions and without one you probably wouldn't be wearing what you've got on today. Sewing is making a comeback and the sewing machine, once a standard item in most households, is too.

event_title: Pioneer 10 leaves the inner solar system
description: Pioneer 10 - arguably the most successful spacecraft of all time - left the inner solar system, passing Pluto, and headed on its way towards the star Aldebaran, which it should reach, all being well, in approximately two million years. Launched in 1973, Pioneer was only supposed to last for 21 months. But as late as 2003 it was still sending back signals from the far unknown. In case any aliens stumble across it, Pioneer carries plaques with a picture of a man, a picture of a woman and a map of Earth's location in space. Pioneer's long-term mission? To find the heliopause, the place where the solar wind, a band of gases expelled by the sun and travelling at a million miles per hour, meets deep space.

event_title: U.S. Supreme Court issued Miranda vs. Arizona decision
description: The U.S. Supreme Court issued its landmark Miranda vs. Arizona decision, ruling that criminal suspects must be informed of their constitutional rights prior to questioning by police. 'You have the right to remain silent. Anything you say may be taken down as evidence and used against you in a court of law...'
Today is celebrated as National Bourbon Day in America and numerous countries worldwide.
Legend has it that bourbon was first produced by Reverend Elijah Craig, who either warmed oak staves or charred a barrel, leading to a greatly improved whiskey.
Sandpaper was patented by Isaac Fischer, Jr. of Springfield, Vermont.
The UN has decreed June 14 as World Blood Donor Day.
The Magna Carta was sealed, with ale mentioned in clause 35, which specified a single measure for wine, ale, and corn throughout the kingdom.
National Beer Day Britain was established by beer sommelier and writer, Jane Peyton, and is celebrated annually on June 15th.
The first series of the sitcom 'The Black Adder' aired on British television.

The day chronicled in James Joyce's 1922 novel Ulysses, following the experiences of three Dubliners over a single day. The annual commemoration of Bloomsday was first referenced by Joyce in a letter on June 27, 1924.

A new form of entertainment, the roller coaster, took off at New York's Coney Island. Known as the Switchback Railway, it was powered by gravity and reached speeds of 10 kph (6 mph), based on Marcus Thompson's patented invention.

Rachel Carson published an article in the New Yorker entitled 'Silent Spring', an extract from her upcoming book. The article dealt with the harm that pesticides were doing to plants, birds, fish, and humans, and the book is widely credited with kicking off the environmental movement worldwide.

event_title: Statue of Liberty's arrival in New York City's harbour
description: On this day in 1885, the Statue of Liberty appeared in New York City's harbour for the first time, on board a French naval frigate. For her transatlantic journey, the 151-foot statue, already a year old, had been disassembled and divided between more than 200 packing cases.
Today is Iceland's National Day, commemorating Iceland's independence from Denmark in 1944.
Hanaya Yohei created what is now known as sushi by placing pieces of seafood on hand-pressed vinegared rice.

event_title: Anniversary of the Battle of Waterloo
description: An allied army led by the Duke of Wellington and Von Blücher defeated Napoleon's armies near Waterloo, Belgium.
The silent action film 'The Iron Man' was released in cinemas.
Sally Ride became the first American woman to travel into space.
Union Army General Gordon Granger announced freedom from slavery in Texas, marking the anniversary known as Juneteenth.
Celebrations of Juneteenth began, initially with church-led community gatherings in Texas.

event_title: Juneteenth Becomes Official State Holiday in Texas
description: Texas became the first state to adopt Juneteenth as an official state holiday.
Albert Stevens Crockett, known as the 'dean of correspondents' and a connoisseur who preserved pre-Prohibition cocktails, was born.

event_title: National Vanilla Milkshake Day and the History of Milkshakes
description: On June 20, National Vanilla Milkshake Day is observed. Historically, the first milkshake recipes found in 1885 were alcoholic, containing cream, eggs, and whisky. By the 1900s, they evolved into sweet drinks with fruit syrups or chocolate, and eventually ice cream. The invention of the blender led to the smooth milkshake, which became popular in diners and ice cream parlours. Vanilla, the focus of the day, is native to Mexico and comes from the only fruit-bearing orchid.

June 20 (or 21st/22nd) marks the Summer Solstice, the longest day of the year with the most hours of sunlight. Known as 'solstitium' meaning 'sun stands still', it occurs when the Earth's axis tilts closest to the sun. This day is celebrated as Midsummer's night, with traditions including gatherings at Stonehenge to witness the sunrise and significant celebrations in Scandinavia. In the Southern Hemisphere, the Winter Solstice, the shortest day, is celebrated around this time.

event_title: First Train Crossing of the Channel Tunnel
description: On June 20, 1993, the very first train crossed between France and England via the Channel Tunnel. The concept of a Channel crossing dates back to 1751, with early excavations in the 1880s being halted by the British Army due to invasion concerns.
A day to celebrate smoothies, which originated from the health food movement of the 1960s and 1970s when electric blenders became common.

Greenlanders celebrate their national day, also known as Ullortuneq, chosen because it is the longest and lightest day of the year. Celebrations include displaying the Greenlandic flag, wearing traditional costumes, singing, folk dances, kayaking, and enjoying Greenlandic food.
Designated in America as a day to celebrate Cognac, though the reason for its designation is unknown.
The Empire Windrush arrived at Tilbury Docks in Essex, England, releasing its 1,027 passengers, marking a significant moment for post-WWII migration to the UK.
The Home Office destroyed all landing cards belonging to Windrush arrivals, which later made it difficult for them to prove their legal status.

event_title: Theresa May's apology for Windrush Scandal
description: Then Prime Minister, Theresa May, issued a public apology for the Windrush Scandal, which saw legal UK citizens wrongly classified as illegal immigrants.
The Empire Windrush made its last voyage in March 1954, catching fire and sinking in the Mediterranean Sea, with the death of crew members.
A national monument commemorating the Windrush Generation, designed by artist Basil Watson, was officially unveiled in London's Waterloo station.
Bilbo Baggins, from J. R. R. Tolkien's The Hobbit, returned home to Bag End in Shire Reckoning after the Quest of Erebor, only to learn he had been declared dead.
John Herbert Dillinger, one of America's most notorious gangsters, was born in Indianapolis, Indiana.

event_title: John Dillinger named Public Enemy Number One
description: John Dillinger was informally named America's first Public Enemy Number One, 31 years after his birth.
John Dillinger started his first prison sentence, during which a physical examination revealed he had gonorrhoea.
John Dillinger was shot and killed by FBI agents outside the Biograph Theatre in Chicago, Illinois, after being informed on by Ana Cumpănaș.
Ana Cumpănaș (Anna Sage), who informed on John Dillinger, was deported to Timișoara, Romania, despite FBI agents agreeing to help her avoid deportation.
The International Olympic Committee was founded in Paris on the initiative of Baron Pierre de Coubertin.
Mathematician, codebreaker, computing pioneer, and the father of artificial intelligence, Alan Turing was born in Maida Vale, West London.

Quebec National Holiday in Montreal and other areas of French Canada takes place on the same day as the feast day of the Nativity of St John the Baptist, patron saint of French Canadians. It's a public holiday, and celebrations take in solemn ceremonies as well as live music concerts, sporting events, parades, fireworks and more.

This day in 1314 was the second and decisive day of the Battle of Bannockburn. The actual battle marked the culminating moment of the First Scottish War of Independence when a Scots army defeated an English army almost three times its size.

On this day in 1997, the US Air Force released a report on the Roswell UFO rumours, entitled "Roswell Report: Case Closed". The Air Force report found that the "alien bodies" were air force personnel, killed or disfigured by high-altitude accidents, or crash test dummies dropped from high-altitude balloons. The spacecraft were high altitude balloons and the UFOs space probes.
A designated day celebrated on June 25, though its origins are unclear.

One of Britain's worst serial killers, John Christie, was sentenced to death for one of the murders he had committed. His case helped transform British justice and led to the abolition of capital punishment, partly because a man named Timothy Evans had already been hanged for a murder Christie committed.
Designated by friends in America and celebrated on June 25, though the reason for this designation is unknown.

event_title: International Cachaça Day and Dia dos Namorados
description: Celebrated on June 25. Dia dos Namorados is Brazil's equivalent of Valentine's Day.
Celebrated in America as National Bourbon Day, this celebration has travelled and is now widely observed.
The Ming dynasty Emperor Hongzhi invented the toothbrush, combining bristles from a pig with a handle made of bone or bamboo.

event_title: First-ever use of a Universal Product Code (UPC) barcode
description: Clyde Dawson bought a packet of Wrigley's Juicy Fruit chewing gum at a supermarket in Troy, Ohio, which was scanned by cashier Sharon Buchanan, marking the first use of a UPC barcode.
Violette Szabo, a World War II secret agent of immense bravery, was born in Paris. She later died in Ravensbrück concentration camp in 1945.
June 27th is International Pineapple Day, celebrated particularly by those in the hospitality industry.

event_title: First reference to 'pineapple' for the fruit
description: The spiky fruit, resembling a pine cone, was first referenced as 'pineapple' in 1664.

event_title: King Charles II posed with a pineapple
description: King Charles II of England posed for a portrait receiving a pineapple as a gift, symbolizing his royal privilege due to the fruit's rarity.
The world's first automatic teller machine (ATM) was installed at Barclays Bank in Enfield, London. The first customer was actor Reg Varney, who used a cheque to extract money.
Joseph Smith, the founder of Mormonism, was martyred when a mob attacked the jail at Carthage, Illinois, and shot him.

Adolphe Sax patented the saxophone, a new musical instrument he created. Sax learned to make instruments from his father and sought to create an instrument softer than brass and more penetrating than woodwind, combining a reed-based mouthpiece with the brassy quality of brass instruments.

Archduke Franz Ferdinand, heir to the Austro-Hungarian empire, was assassinated in Sarajevo. After escaping an initial bomb attack, he was shot by Gavrilo Princip while visiting his injured entourage in the hospital. This event is considered the first step on the road to worldwide war.

event_title: Establishment of the Mission of San Francisco of Assisi
description: Spanish colonists first established the Mission of San Francisco of Assisi.
After eight years of construction, London's Tower Bridge was opened by the Prince of Wales, the future King Edward VII.

event_title: Double-decker bus incident at Tower Bridge
description: A double-decker bus had to leap the gap when Tower Bridge started to lift mid-crossing.
An aircraft dared to fly under Tower Bridge's spans.
An aircraft dared to fly under Tower Bridge's spans.

event_title: President Bill Clinton's motorcade incident at Tower Bridge
description: President Bill Clinton's motorcade was separated from his police escort when Tower Bridge lifted.

The British North America Act united the previously three separate colonies of the United Canadas, Nova Scotia, and New Brunswick into Canada, a single dominion within the British Empire, establishing Canada Day.
60 competitors set off on the first Tour de France, a gruelling race comprising six stages. The event was won by Maurice Garin.
The world's most widely circulated magazine, The Watchtower, a missionary publication produced by the Jehovah's Witnesses, launched. It churns out over 62 million copies in over 303 languages.

event_title: Harold 'Kim' Philby admits being a Soviet double agent
description: Former British Foreign Office official, Harold 'Kim' Philby, admitted he had been a Soviet double agent, the mysterious 'Third Man' who had protected Guy Burgess and Donald Maclean. His career almost certainly led to the death of many agents.

event_title: Doctor's Day in India / Birthday and day of death of Dr. B. C. Roy
description: India celebrates Doctor's Day, which is the birthday and day of death of India's most celebrated doctor, Dr. B. C. Roy, who was also a politician and ally of Gandhi.

Nobel Prize-winning author Ernest Hemingway died on this day in 1961. He began his career as a journalist and novelist, moving to Paris in the 1920s where he associated with other 'lost generation' figures. His successful novels led him to Key West, where he engaged in big game fishing and writing. Despite his achievements, his later life was marked by heavy drinking, a plane crash, and a battle with depression, leading him to take his own life just before his 62nd birthday.

A day to raise awareness of, and to fight against, the global catastrophe that is single-use plastic pollution. Plastic bags were introduced in the mid-1960s. In 1997, sailor and researcher Charles Moore discovered the Great Pacific Garbage Patch. In 2019, single-use plastic particles were found in the Mariana Trench.
15-year-old Midshipman Robert Pitcairn, on board the British sloop HMS Swallow, sighted the islands which were to take his name.

event_title: Bounty Mutineers Settle on Pitcairn Island
description: Nine mutineers from Captain Bligh's HMS Bounty and their Tahitian companions settled on Pitcairn Island and set fire to the stolen ship.

event_title: Signing of the Declaration of Independence
description: The Declaration of Independence was signed, marking America's Independence Day, two days after the United States declared its independence from Britain.
The Appletini cocktail was created in West Hollywood, California.

The National Health Service (NHS), a comprehensive health service funded by taxation and available to all UK residents, came into effect. Conceived as part of the post-WWII rebuilding, it was shaped by the 1942 Beveridge Report and established by The National Health Service Act, which received royal assent in November 1946.

The bikini, named by vehicle engineer Louis Réard, made its debut. Réard's daring design, created for a competition for the world's smallest swimsuit, was modeled by nude dancer Micheline Bernardini at Paris' Piscine Molitor swimming pool.
World Chocolate Day is celebrated globally every year on this day.
Dive Bar Day was established in the USA by Diageo's Seagram 7 Whiskey brand to celebrate dive bars across America.

An American military surveillance balloon crashed at a ranch near Roswell, New Mexico. The incident later became the center of theories alleging an extraterrestrial spaceship was involved, fueled by the military's initial cover-up.

Fashion Day was inaugurated as an occasion to celebrate and express individual uniqueness and style through clothing. It is a celebration of everyday fashion and a public rebellion against fashion trends and global fashion houses.

It is the birthday of actor Tom Hanks, known for movies such as Forrest Gump, Saving Private Ryan, Cast Away, and the Toy Story series. In 2014, he was recorded as the highest-grossing actor in film history, and Asteroid 12818 Tomhanks was named after him.

event_title: Biggest Wave Ever in Lituya Bay
description: The biggest wave ever known, a megatsunami over 500m in height, swept through Lituya Bay, Alaska. This event was caused by an 8.3 magnitude earthquake that dumped 30 million cubic metres of rock and ice into the narrow bay, taking out trees high up the mountainsides.
One minute after midnight on July 10, 1973, The Bahamas flag was raised for the first time over government buildings where the Union Jack once flew.

event_title: British Navy ended rum ration (Black Tot Day)
description: On July 10, 1970, the British Navy retired one of its longest-standing traditions – the midday rum ration, known as a tot.
Donald Bradman scored an extraordinary 309 runs against England during the 1930 Ashes cricket series. This remains the highest score by a batsman in one day, eventually reaching 334 runs.
The British Navy retired one of its longest-standing traditions, the midday rum ration, known as a tot.
Mick Jagger, Keith Richards, Brian Jones, Ian Stewart and Dick Taylor, billed as The Rollin' Stones, played their first gig at the Marquee Club on London's Oxford Street.
The British Navy retired one of its longest-standing traditions " the midday rum ration, known as a tot.

event_title: The Hollywood Sign (originally 'Hollywoodland') was formally dedicated
description: California realtors established a company and set up an enormous, illuminated advertising sign on Mount Lee, formally dedicated on this day.

event_title: Actress committed suicide by jumping from the Hollywood Sign
description: During the 1930s, a failed actress committed suicide by jumping from the Hollywood Sign.

event_title: The Hollywood Sign was restored by the Hollywood Chamber of Commerce
description: The sign was in disrepair and missing the 'H'; the Hollywood Chamber of Commerce restored it, regaining the 'H' but losing the 'land'.

event_title: The Hollywood Sign was amended to 'Hollyweed'
description: Cannabis campaigners amended the sign to read 'Hollyweed'.

event_title: The Hollywood Sign was demolished and replaced
description: The Hollywood Sign was demolished and replaced with the structure seen today.

event_title: Black Tot Day: British Navy retired the midday rum ration
description: The British Navy retired one of its longest-standing traditions – the midday rum ration, known as a tot.

The anguished French took matters into their own hands and stormed the Bastille, kick-starting the French revolution. While the Bastille had only seven old men rather than a hoard of angry political prisoners, it was still the symbol of revolt that the country needed.
An expedition, led by Aleksei Chirikov and Vitus Bering, became the first Europeans to reach Alaska.

event_title: First edition of Punch magazine published
description: Founders Ebenezer Landells and Henry Mayhew published the first edition of Punch magazine.

event_title: Same-sex marriage legalized in the UK
description: Same-sex marriage became British law.
Hunter S. Thompson, the father of Gonzo journalism, sports writer, novelist, and author of Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas, was born.
The British Navy retired one of its longest-standing traditions, the midday rum ration, known as a tot.
Bars worldwide celebrate National Daiquiri Day, honoring the classic cocktail. The creation of the Daiquiri is often credited to mining engineer Jennings Cox.
Britain recorded its hottest day with a record temperature of 40.3 C at Coningsby in Lincolnshire, surpassing previous records.
The British Navy retired one of its longest-standing traditions, the midday rum ration, known as a tot.

The Apollo 11 mission achieved the first crewed moon landing. Buzz Aldrin and Neil Armstrong were the first people to walk on the moon, with Armstrong uttering his famous words, "one small step for [a] man, one giant leap for mankind".
Space Exploration Day (AKA National Moon Day) was officially proclaimed a holiday in the United States by President Ronald Reagan.

event_title: Día del Amigo (Friend's Day) in Argentina
description: Día del Amigo, a Latin American celebration of friendship, is observed in Argentina. The founder chose this day in 1969, inspired by the world uniting in friendship while watching the moon landing.
Ernest Hemingway, the noted writer, was born.
Mr and Mrs Spicer sighted the Loch Ness Monster, sparking national interest when they saw "a most extraordinary form of animal" cross the road and enter Loch Ness.
This day (22/7) is recognized as a close approximation to the irrational number Pi, representing three and one-sevenths.
This day commemorates St. Mary Magdalene, who was historically misidentified as a prostitute but is recorded as one of Jesus' disciples, known for her wisdom.

National Vanilla Ice Cream Day is celebrated. Vanilla ice cream was introduced to the USA in the 1780s, with Thomas Jefferson bringing a recipe back from France. A copy of his recipe is preserved in the US Library of Congress. In 1841, a new technique for hand-pollinating vanilla was discovered, allowing for its commercial production.

Gorgeous Grandma Day, an American invention, is celebrated as a day to call grandmas or any older woman. It honors women over fifty, sixty, or seventy who believe they have their whole life ahead of them.

Haile Selassie, African statesman, global icon, and the last emperor of Ethiopia, was born on this day in 1892. His title before becoming emperor was Ras Tafari, which forms the heart of Rastafarianism.
Today is National Tequila Day in the USA, and by extension Tequila Day in other agave-loving countries.

Simón Bolívar, the liberator of much of South America, was born. Venezuela and Ecuador have a holiday in his memory. He is a sacred figure in Venezuela and Bolivia, known as El Libertador, who drove out numerically superior Spanish forces from much of what is today Latin America.

event_title: Admiral Nelson loses arm and battle
description: Rear Admiral Horatio Nelson lost the Battle of Santa Cruz de Tenerife and his arm during a British attempt to land and attack the Spanish port. His arm was amputated and thrown overboard.

event_title: Harry Patch, last WWI trench soldier, passes away
description: Harry Patch, the last soldier known to have fought in the trenches of World War I, passed away at the age of 111. He was traumatized by the war and only spoke about it after turning 100.

event_title: Birth of Louise Joy Brown, the first IVF baby
description: Louise Joy Brown was born, becoming the first baby ever conceived via in vitro fertilization (IVF). Dr. Robert Edwards, who developed IVF, won the Nobel Prize for medicine in 2010 for his work.

Angelenos woke up believing they were under chemical attack due to thick, choking smog. It took almost a decade to realize cars were to blame, and another twenty years for pollution standards for automobiles to be introduced.

María Eva Duarte de Perón, known as Evita, died aged 33. She rose from B-movie acting to become First Lady of Argentina, potentially influencing women's right to vote and establishing a foundation to reduce poverty. Her body was mummified and later buried in a secure tomb in Buenos Aires.

event_title: Origin of Sleepy Head Day (Seven Sleepers of Ephesus)
description: A group of Christian saints in Ephesus retired to a cave to escape Roman persecution and slept there for two centuries, forming the historical basis for Sleepy Head Day.
The armistice agreement ending the Korean War was signed, concluding almost two years of negotiations between the feuding sides.
General José de San Martín proclaimed Peru's independence from the Spanish empire.

event_title: Fingerprints brought back into use for identification
description: William Herschel, a British magistrate in colonial India, brought fingerprints back into use as an alternative to signatures for documents and attempted to use them for identification in prisons.
Austria-Hungary declared war on Serbia, following the assassination of Franz Ferdinand, marking the beginning of World War I.

Today is observed as National Lipstick Day. The history of lipstick dates back millennia, with Mesopotamians mixing crushed gemstones and Cleopatra using crushed bugs. Its popularity grew in 16th century England with Queen Elizabeth I, and commercial production began in 1884 when French cosmetics brand Guerlain started manufacturing it.

A man in a baseball cap walked into the Carlton InterContinental in Cannes and minutes later walked out with over $100 million worth of jewels. This remains one of the biggest jewellery heists of all time, and the diamonds have never been recovered.

President Dwight Eisenhower signed into law the National Aeronautics and Space Act, creating NASA, the National Aeronautics and Space Agency. This agency was formed in response to Soviet space achievements like the Sputnik launch.
One of the greatest painters of all time, Vincent Van Gogh, tragically shot himself. Despite his paintings now selling for tens of millions, he sold only one during his lifetime.

National Cheesecake Day is celebrated. The dessert is thought to have been conceived by the Ancient Greeks, first appearing in a book by physician Aegimus, and fed to Greek athletes during the Olympics in 776BC. The recipe has since gone global, adapted to local tastes, and remains popular in the United States.

The paperback revolution began with the publication of ten Penguin books, inspired by Allen Lane's frustration with the lack of cheap, good fiction. The original colour-coded books, costing six pence, included works by Ernest Hemingway, Dorothy L. Sayers, and Agatha Christie. The concept was an instant success, leading to Penguin becoming a standalone publisher in 1936 and selling three million paperbacks within a year.

Henry Ford, who would set the foundations of modern car culture and automated factory production, was born. A natural engineer, he repaired watches in his teens, outlined plans for a gasoline-powered horseless carriage before 30, and founded the Ford Motor Company, which launched the affordable Model T.

The Winton Motor Carriage Company placed the world's first car advertisement in Scientific American, suggesting readers 'dispense with a horse'. The ad was successful, leading to sales of over 20 vehicles, including one to James Ward Packard, who later founded his own car company. Winton's motor carriage cost $1000 and reached 20mph.

event_title: British Navy retired the midday rum ration
description: On this day in 1970, the British Navy retired one of its longest-standing traditions – the midday rum ration, known as a tot. By the time of its abolition, the tot had been reduced to a mere 70 millilitres, but even that came to be considered too much for men who might spend the afternoon managing weapons and complicated machinery.

On this day in 1944, Antoine de Saint-Exupéry, author of The Little Prince and a pioneer aviator, disappeared while flying his ninth reconnaissance mission from an airbase on Corsica in an unarmed P-38. The remains of his plane were not discovered until more than 50 years later after a fisherman found his silver identity bracelet off the coast of Marseille.
A recent celebration created in 2023 on the back of the surge in popularity of the spritz cocktail style from the Twenty-Teens.

event_title: Publication of A Game of Thrones
description: George R. R Martin published A Game of Thrones, the first instalment of his epic fantasy series A Song of Ice and Fire.
Representatives of Switzerland's different cantons met and joined forces against Austrian occupiers, marking the beginnings of Swiss independence.
Polish fighters in Warsaw rose up against the occupying Nazi forces.
Henry Charles Ramos, generally known as Carl, was born in Indiana.
Irish actor Peter O'Toole was born in Connemara, Ireland. He was the most-nominated lead actor never to win an Academy Award. He died on 14th December 2013.
African-American, gay writer James Baldwin was born. He was an inspiration to writers, published novels like Giovanni's Room, and campaigned for Civil Rights.
Henry Charles Ramos, known as Carl, was born in Indiana. He is arguably New Orleans's most famous bartender.
America celebrates National Watermelon Day annually on this day.

event_title: USS Nautilus navigates under the North Pole
description: Captain Anderson and the crew of the USS Nautilus submarine navigated under the North Pole, discovering that the Arctic Sea stretched more than 2 ½ miles below the ice floes and traveling between Alaska and Iceland.

event_title: Sir Edward Grey's 'lamps are going out' remark
description: British Foreign Secretary Sir Edward Grey remarked, 'The lamps are going out all over Europe. We shall not see them lit again in our lifetime.'

event_title: Germany declares war on France and invades Belgium
description: Germany declared war on France and invaded Belgium, effectively forcing Britain's entry into the First World War.
Henry Charles Ramos, a famous bartender, was born in Indiana.

This day originated in America as a celebration of Californian wine, with the first vineyards established there in the 1850s. The industrious Romans are also thanked for ideas for civilised fun, including drinking chilled white wine in the summer. English white wines have also seen significant improvement in recent years, winning awards at international competitions.

event_title: Dom Pérignon allegedly invents sparkling champagne
description: The Benedictine monk Dom Pérignon allegedly invented sparkling champagne at his abbey in Hautvillers, France. He pioneered a number of champagne innovations, from corks and thicker glass to the successful use of red grapes. Some historians suggest sparkling wine was actually invented and first bottled by the British.

The world's most expensive hamburger, the Cultured Beef Burger, made its debut. It was grown in a laboratory at Maastrict University at a cost of $330,000, funded by Sergei Brin, and crafted from stem cells taken from a cow.
Former President of the USA, Barack Obama, was born in Hawaii to a Kenyan father and an American mother.
Henry Charles Ramos, arguably New Orleans's most famous bartender, was born in Indiana.

National Oyster Day celebrates oysters, a food source with a long history dating back to the age of dinosaurs. Once a plentiful and cheap food, they became a delicacy due to overharvesting and pollution. New York was a major producer during the 18th and 19th centuries' Golden Age of Oysters, and the town of Oyster Bay was named for them in 1639.

Richard Burton, the celebrated British actor and love of Elizabeth Taylor's life, who was one of the most-nominated actors never to have won an Oscar, died on this day in 1984. He rose from an impoverished Welsh village to global celebrity.

event_title: First-ever nuclear test ban treaty agreed
description: At the height of the Cold War in 1963, the United States, the USSR, and Britain agreed to the first-ever nuclear test ban treaty. The Moscow treaty banned all nuclear testing in the air, water, or outer space, though it allowed underground tests. This historic move towards world peace was prompted by growing concerns over radiation fallout and its health impacts, and it was later signed by over a hundred other nations.
Henry Charles Ramos, arguably New Orleans's most famous bartender, was born on this day in 1856 in Indiana.
After 300 years of British rule, the Union Jack was ceremonially lowered and the new Jamaican flag proudly hoisted aloft as Jamaica declared itself an independent state.
Theatrical impresario Richard D'Oyly Carte opened the doors of his brand new hotel, The Savoy, in the heart of theatreland.
Henry Charles Ramos, a famous New Orleans bartender, was born in Indiana.
Henry Charles Ramos, generally known as Carl, was born in 1856 in Indiana.
The United States of America introduced its very own currency, the US Dollar.
Henry Charles Ramos, a famous bartender, was born in Indiana.

A gang of 15 robbers hijacked an overnight mail train, escaping with over £2 million in 1960s money. Twelve robbers were caught and sentenced, but three were never apprehended. The train driver suffered life-changing injuries.

American president Richard Nixon resigned, bowing to media pressure after covering up a break-in at the Democratic National Committee headquarters in Watergate. He was implicated by tape-recorded phone calls and meetings where he planned to block the investigation.
Singapore gained independence from Malaysia.
Henry Charles Ramos, also known as Carl, was born in Indiana.
Dr J.B. Siegert & Sons medicinal liquor company, which had been marketing bitters, reached a milestone.
World Lion Day was first celebrated on this day in 2013, aiming to raise awareness and build support for the conservation of endangered big cats.

August 10 is officially 'Spoil Your Dog Day', a day to pamper dogs who have been loyal companions, hunting allies, protectors, herders, and now assist the blind, aid patient recovery, and work with police and military.

Portuguese explorer Ferdinand Magellan set out from Seville, Spain, in command of five Spanish ships on a mission to circumnavigate the globe. He was later killed during the Battle of Mactan in the Philippines, but his expedition completed its mission.
Ecuador broke away from the confederation of Greater Colombia, which it had joined after gaining freedom from the Spanish, celebrating its independence on this day.
Henry Charles Ramos, a famous New Orleans bartender, was born on this day in 1856 in Indiana.

event_title: Mountain Day (Yama no Hi) in Japan
description: A public holiday in Japan to appreciate mountains, formally entered into law in 2016. The date was chosen because 'eight' in kanji resembles a mountain and '11' apparently resembles two trees.

event_title: First civilian prisoners arrived at Alcatraz
description: The first civilian prisoners classified 'most dangerous' arrived at the new high-security penitentiary on Alcatraz Island. Alcatraz had been used as a military prison since 1868.
The Mayan Long Count calendar, which predicted the world's end on December 21, 2012, began on this day.

Peter Cushing, known for his roles as Governor Tarkin in Star Wars and characters like Baron Frankenstein and Van Helsing in Hammer Horror films, passed away. He was also a committed vegetarian and birdwatcher.
Henry Charles Ramos, also known as Carl, a famous New Orleans bartender, was born in Indiana.
The day when the shooting season opens in the United Kingdom, sending the gentry to the moors in quest of grouse.
Erwin Schrödinger, the Austrian physicist known for the Schrödinger's Cat experiment, was born.
Henry Charles Ramos, arguably New Orleans's most famous bartender, was born in Indiana.

The earliest documentation of prosecco dates back to 1754, in the poem titled Il Roccolo Ditirambo by Aureliano Acanti. "And now I would like to wet my mouth with that prosecco with its apple bouquet."

Prosecco's modern history begins in Italy during the 19th century when it gained attention, at a time when Antonio Carpen
es writings detailed the sparkling wine's diffusion and production.

event_title: Stainless Steel created by Harry Brearley
description: An English metallurgist named Harry Brearley created a new steel alloy, with a lot of chromium and not much carbon. It was resistant to everything from nitric acid to lemon juice and vinegar, and so Brearley, rather unimaginatively, named his creation "rustless steel". The cutlery-maker who embraced Brearley's discovery christened it "stainless steel".

event_title: Northeast America and Canada Power Blackout
description: A massive power blackout, thought to have started by a power plant in Ohio shutting down, affected over 50 million people across the eastern United States and Canada, crippling major cities like New York and Ottawa. It was the worst power cut in US history, causing widespread chaos including traffic gridlock, railway stoppages, and suspended flights.

Ilich Ramírez Sánchez, the Marxist-Leninist terrorist known as 'Carlos the Jackal', was captured in Sudan. He was one of the world's most wanted men, responsible for assassinations, a bombing campaign, and hostage-taking. He was later sentenced to two life sentences.

In China and beyond, the Hungry Ghost Festival is celebrated. According to Buddhism, ancestral spirits return from the grave to visit the living. Traditions include making offerings of food and burning fake banknotes. In Malaysia and Singapore, the day also features live shows, traditional music, and dances.
A celebratory day to enjoy white wine and, if possible, celebrate one's own nation's white wine.
Henry Charles Ramos, arguably New Orleans's most famous bartender, was born in Indiana.
A day celebrating Prosecco, one of the most sold sparkling wines in both the USA and UK.
National Whiskey Sour Day is celebrated in America.

A celebratory day for the lemon meringue pie, a popular dessert with hotly contested origins. Some believe the French began adding meringue to pies in the 18th century, while others credit American pastry shop owner Elizabeth Goodfellow with its invention.
