Half a million people gathered at a farm in upstate New York for the Woodstock music festival, billed as "an Aquarian exposition: 3 days of peace and music." Artists like The Who, Janis Joplin, Joan Baez, Sly & the Family Stone, Jimi Hendrix, and the Grateful Dead performed.

The Allies celebrated victory over Japan, following the Japanese government's unconditional surrender announcement on August 14, 1945, which ended the Second World War. The 15th of August 1945 coincided with the state opening of the British Parliament, marked by public celebrations in the UK, while Japan reeled from the devastation of Hiroshima and Nagasaki.
Henry Charles Ramos, arguably New Orleans's most famous bartender, was born in Indiana.
August 16th is celebrated as National Rum Day in the United States and International Rum Day globally.
Henry Charles Ramos, arguably New Orleans's most famous bartender, was born in Indiana.
On this day in 1921, Dr J.B. Siegert & Sons medicinal liquor company, which had been marketing bitters out of first Venezuela then Trinidad, marked a significant event.

Balloonist John Wise attempted the first ever mail delivery by air, taking to the skies in Lafayette, Indiana, and aiming for New York with a bag of 123 letters. He landed 30 miles away after a five-hour flight, having dropped the mail ahead of him on a homemade parachute. Wise continued flying, researching, and writing on ballooning until he disappeared over Lake Michigan at age 71.
Mae West, known for her bawdy double entendres and as a vaudeville star, was born on this day in Brooklyn, New York. She later moved to Hollywood and frequently came up against censors.

Indonesia declared its independence on this day. It took more than four years of fighting before the Dutch, who had ruled most of Indonesia's islands before the war, accepted the country was free. Today, over 200 million Indonesians celebrate their independence across more than 17,000 islands.

event_title: Debut of 'The Private Life of Henry VIII'
description: The film 'The Private Life of Henry VIII' debuted in British cinemas. It became the first British film to achieve international success and the first British film to ever be nominated for Best Film at the Academy Awards. Charles Laughton, who played King Henry VIII, won an Oscar for Best Actor.
Henry Charles Ramos, arguably New Orleans's most famous bartender, was born on this day in Indiana.

event_title: Initial observation leading to the discovery of Helium
description: During a total solar eclipse, French astronomer Pierre Jules César Janssen observed an unusual color band in the sun's spectrum, concluding the presence of an unknown element. Later that year, British astronomer Norman Lockyer proposed the name helium for this element.
The American explorer, soldier, and governor Meriwether Lewis, famous for leading the Lewis and Clark Expedition to map much of the western United States, was born.

An opportunity to celebrate the craft, science and history of this incredible art form. Photography came out of late 1830s France when Joseph Nicéphore Niépce managed to 'fix' an image that was captured with a camera. Louis Daguerre, Niépce's associate, went on to develop the daguerreotype process, which was announced by the French Academy of Sciences before the French government acquired the patent for his innovation, and proceeded to hand it to the world as a free gift with no copyright.

Celebrated since 1939 when President Roosevelt decided that Orville Wright's birthday would be National Aviation Day. Orville Wright, with his brother Wilbur, is generally credited with inventing the aeroplane.
Henry Charles Ramos, arguably New Orleans's most famous bartender, made his appearance on this day in 1856 in Indiana.
British doctor Sir Ronald Ross discovered the ability for female mosquitoes to transmit malaria between humans. World Mosquito Day, observed annually on August 20th, commemorates his discovery.
Hungarians celebrate the foundation of their country, also known as St Stephen's Day.
King Stephen, who founded the Hungarian state, was canonized by Pope Gregory VII, becoming St. Stephen.
John Milton's publisher, Samuel Simmons, registered his poem 'Paradise Lost' with the British Stationer's Office.
Henry Charles Ramos, a famous New Orleans bartender, was born in Indiana.
On this day, Hawaii, a group of breathtaking volcanic islands, became the 50th US state, now affectionately known as the 'Aloha State'.

event_title: Mona Lisa stolen from the Louvre
description: Vincenzo Peruggia stole the Mona Lisa from Paris's Louvre museum, an event that made the artwork globally famous.
William James 'Count' Basie, one of jazz music's all-time greats, was born in Red Bank, New Jersey.

The father of photojournalism, Henri Cartier-Bresson was born in Chanteloup-en-Brie, France. His belief that "in photography, the smallest thing can be a great subject. The little human detail can become a leitmotiv" shaped photography for decades, helping found the now-commonplace reportage style.

In India and other Hindu cultures, this day marks the birth of Krishna, the eighth incarnation of the great god Vishnu. Highlights of Krishna's career include lifting mountains, taming giant snakes, fighting demons, and stealing butter.

The British TV institution, Match of the Day, premiered with black and white coverage of a game between Liverpool and Arsenal. It remains the BBC TV's principal football programme and is recognized by Guinness World Records as the longest-running football TV programme in the world.
Americans celebrate National Sponge Cake Day on August 23rd each year.

Rudolph Valentino, the first great heartthrob of an era and star of The Sheikh, died of appendicitis at age 31. His funeral drew 100,000 people, and he was also a talented actor, dancer, and boxer who led an actors' strike, published poetry, and recorded songs.
Henry Charles Ramos, known as Carl and arguably New Orleans's most famous bartender, was born in Indiana.
Patrick Grant chose August 24, 1997, to honor his girlfriend's dad and release his album 'Fields Amaze', marking the establishment of International Strange Music Day.

event_title: International Strange Music Day becomes international
description: By 2002, International Strange Music Day had grown to an international level, being celebrated by several venues and artists.

event_title: British troops burn the White House
description: British troops rolled into an abandoned White House, ate the President's food, and then set the place on fire during the War of 1812.
Henry Charles Ramos, New Orleans's famous bartender, was born in Indiana.

event_title: Dr J.B. Siegert & Sons company milestone
description: On this day in 1921, Dr J.B. Siegert & Sons medicinal liquor company, which marketed Angostura Bitters, reached a significant milestone (referred to as its 'birthday').
Henry Charles Ramos, arguably New Orleans's most famous bartender, was born in Indiana.
A milestone occurred for Angostura Bitters, a product of Dr J.B. Siegert & Sons medicinal liquor company, which had been marketing bitters out of first Venezuela then Trinidad.

A teenage chemistry prodigy, William Perkin, accidentally invented mauveine, the first synthetic dye, which he patented on this day. This discovery transformed science by enabling predictable and lasting fabric dyes and inspiring further chemical developments.
Henry Charles Ramos, also known as Carl, a famous bartender, was born in Indiana.

event_title: Dr J.B. Siegert & Sons medicinal liquor company milestone
description: On this day in 1921, Dr J.B. Siegert & Sons medicinal liquor company, known for marketing Angostura Bitters, reached a significant milestone.
The Mariner 2 spacecraft took off from Cape Canaveral, becoming Earth's first interplanetary mission to Venus.
The magma chamber below the volcanic island of Krakatau exploded, causing tidal waves and killing at least 36,000 people. The noise was audible over at least a third of the globe.
Henry Charles Ramos, arguably New Orleans's most famous bartender, was born in Indiana.

Harry Johnson, one of the pioneers of bartending and known as the "Dean of Drink", was born on this day in 1845 in Königsberg, German Prussia. He went on to run bars in Chicago, Philadelphia, and New York and published his "Bartender's Manual" in 1882.

Today is sacred to Saint Augustine of Hippo, the patron saint of beer. Famous for his prayer "God grant me chastity and self-control, but not yet", he abandoned a high life for celibacy, preaching, and the priesthood in his 30s.
A celebratory day to enjoy and celebrate one's own nation's white wine.
Henry Charles Ramos, arguably New Orleans's most famous bartender, was born on this day in 1856 in Indiana.
A day celebrating Prosecco, one of the most sold sparkling wines in both the USA and UK.
National Whiskey Sour Day in America, an occasion to revisit this classic cocktail.
On this day in 1921, Dr J.B. Siegert & Sons medicinal liquor company, which marketed bitters, had a significant milestone or 'birthday'.
A day dedicated to celebrating lemon juice, a versatile ingredient known for its zingy freshness and as a source of vitamin C.
British physician James Lind discovered that adding citrus juice to the diets of seamen could treat and prevent scurvy.

event_title: Introduction of lemons to the New World
description: Italian explorer Christopher Columbus introduced lemon seeds to the New World, planting them in Hispaniola.

event_title: Lemons become a sign of wealth
description: In the 1800s, the well-heeled grew lemon trees outside their homes to display their affluence.
Harry Craddock, the famous bartender and author of the Savoy Cocktail Book, was born in Stroud, England.
Dr J.B. Siegert & Sons medicinal liquor company, which had been marketing bitters for almost a century, reconstituted itself as Angostura Bitters Limited.
Victor Jules Bergeron is credited for creating the Mai Tai cocktail at his Trader Vic's restaurant in Oakland, near San Francisco.

event_title: Mai Tai Day Proclaimed in Oakland
description: According to a signed proclamation by Rebecca Kaplan, Council Member of the City of Oakland, Mai Tai Day should fall on August 30th.
Henry Charles Ramos, arguably New Orleans's most famous bartender, was born in Indiana.
Trinidad and Tobago gained independence from the United Kingdom.

event_title: Death of Diana, Princess of Wales
description: Diana, Princess of Wales, Dodi Fayed, and their chauffeur, Henri Paul, died in a car crash in Paris.
Nazi SS and Gestapo troops, disguised in Polish uniforms, staged a false flag attack on a radio station in Gleiwitz to justify Hitler's invasion of Poland, marking the start of World War II.
English sculptor and artist Henry Moore, known for his monumental semi-abstract sculptures, died.
Henry Charles Ramos, a famous New Orleans bartender, was born in Indiana.

Rosolio Day, celebrated annually on September 1st, was created by Italicus Rosolio di Bergamotto to honour the brand's 8th anniversary. It is dedicated to celebrating the history of rosolio, an Italian liqueur originally made in convents and monasteries as a medicinal tonic.

Today is Ginger Cat Appreciation Day. Founded by American software developer turned animal rights activist Chris Roy, it honours 'Doobert', the stray ginger cat Roy adopted, who inspired him to create the Dooberert app for animal welfare.

This unofficial national day celebrates Chicken Boy, a 6.7-meter (22-foot) tall fibreglass statue on Route 66, described as 'the Statue of Liberty of Los Angeles'. Named after a 1960s restaurant, it received a Governor's Historic Preservation Award in 2010 from Arnold Schwarzenegger.

World Coconut Day commemorates the foundation of the Asian Pacific Coconut Community (APCC), an intergovernmental organisation that oversees production and promotes coconuts. The APCC was originally formed as the Asian Coconut Community on 9th September 1969, and its name was changed to encompass countries of the Pacific.

On September 02, 1666, a fire started in a bakery in Pudding Lane, London. The fire raged for three days, destroying 80% of London, including 13,200 houses, 87 churches and St Paul's Cathedral. It also helped put an end to the bubonic plague.

American architect Louis H. Sullivan, known as the 'father of modern skyscrapers', was born. He designed and built more than 100 towering buildings across the United States in the late nineteenth century.

event_title: Richard I crowned King of England
description: Richard I, known as Richard the Lionheart, was crowned king of England in Westminster Abbey. He spent no more than about six months in the country he ruled for a decade.
Sergey Brin and Larry Page incorporated Google, taking their college research project to the next business level.
The Bullring's current incarnation, a shopping centre in Birmingham, opened on this day.

event_title: Tsar Peter the Great introduced a beard tax
description: Tsar Peter the Great introduced a beard tax, ostensibly to impose a more modern way of living on his population at a time when most Western European men went clean-shaven, but in practice, the primary goal was revenue. Anyone who chose to dodge the barbers and preserve his beard had to prove he'd paid the tax by carrying a token.
The Mayflower embarked from Plymouth, England, on an epic voyage that would terminate in Plymouth, Massachusetts, without its partner ship, the Speedwell, which had proven unseaworthy.
Brazil declared its independence.
UNESCO calls our minds to focus on literacy.

Tens of thousands of Chinese couples got married on 9 September 2009. The date 9/9 (jiu-jiu) sounds like 'long-long' in Chinese, associating it with longevity and eternality, making it an especially auspicious date for weddings.

event_title: Chrysanthemum Day (Kiku no Sekku) in Japan
description: September 9th is Chrysanthemum Day (Kiku no Sekku) in Japan. This celebration dates back to 910 and was originally observed on the 9th day of the 9th lunar month. The chrysanthemum is a long-standing symbol of the Emperor of Japan.

Golf legend Arnold Palmer was born in Latrobe, Pennsylvania. He became one of the most successful sportsmen of all time, winning over 90 championships, including seven majors and four Masters. He died on September 26, 2016, aged 87.

event_title: American Express credit card launched in Britain
description: The BBC announced the arrival of the American Express credit card in Britain, available to members for an annual fee of three pounds and twelve shillings. The Bank of England allowed this on condition that folk wouldn't spend more than £75 on any single item abroad. This move was expected to give a powerful boost to the idea of credit cards in Britain.

Saudi terrorists affiliated with Osama Bin Laden and al Qaeda hijacked four planes on a suicide mission. Two flew into the World Trade Center, one into the Pentagon and one crashed into a field in Shanksville, Pennsylvania. Nearly 3,000 people were killed.

Georgi Markov died of blood poisoning, four days after being stabbed by an umbrella on London's Waterloo Bridge. Markov, a Bulgarian defector, was a writer and broadcaster who made critical broadcasts about the communist regime. The assailant was later revealed as Francesco Gullino.

The Battle of Stirling Bridge was a significant victory for the Scots leader William Wallace against English knights. It demonstrated that heavily armoured knights were vulnerable, changing military perceptions across Europe.

event_title: Invention of the modern chocolate milkshake
description: Ivar 'Pop' Coulson, a Walgreens employee in Chicago, invented the modern chocolate milkshake by adding two scoops of ice cream to an old-fashioned chocolate malted milk.

event_title: Leo Szilard conceives the idea of a nuclear chain reaction
description: While at a traffic light in London, physicist Leo Szilard realized that the atom could be split, releasing immense amounts of energy, leading to the concept of a nuclear chain reaction.
Marcel Ravidat, an 18-year-old mechanic, discovered the Lascaux Caves in Montignac, France, revealing ancient human habitation with remarkably lifelike cave paintings.

event_title: Dia Nacional da Cachaça (National Cachaça Day)
description: September 13th is celebrated as Dia Nacional da Cachaça (National Cachaça Day) in Brazil, and by extension also in other countries. This should not be confused with International Cachaça Day on June 12th.

Norwegian-British author Roald Dahl, known for stories like Charlie and the Chocolate Factory, James and the Giant Peach, Matilda, and The Witches, was born in Llandaff, Cardiff, on this day in 1916. He also piloted fighters during World War II and nursed his first wife through a long recovery from brain injury.
The illustrator Charles Dana Gibson, known for his 'Gibson Girls' sketches, was born in Roxbury, Massachusetts.
Teacher Terry Shay introduced his classroom to Peter H. Reynolds' book 'The Dot', leading to the concept of International Dot Day.
Mexico celebrates its true independence, a day of parades, marches, singing and music, distinguishing it from Cinco de Mayo.

event_title: Culmination of the War for Mexican Independence
description: The War for Mexican Independence culminated in the ejection of the Spanish from what was then known as New Spain.

event_title: Miguel Hidalgo y Costilla's Grito de Delores
description: Miguel Hidalgo y Costilla, a local priest, began the Mexican revolution from a pulpit with his famous speech of the Grito de Delores.
International Country Music Day, a celebration of country music, was established in the 1950s.
The 'King of Country Music' Hank Williams, one of the most significant and influential country music artists of the 20th century, was born.

event_title: Jim Morrison and The Doors banned from The Ed Sullivan Show
description: Jim Morrison and The Doors performed on The Ed Sullivan Show. During the performance, Jim Morrison defied producers' requests to change the lyrics of 'Light My Fire', leading to the band's ban from the show for good.
Jimi Hendrix, rock icon and electric guitarist, died aged 27 in a Notting Hill, London flat. He most likely choked on his vomit in his sleep after taking a lover's sleeping pills.

Stunningly beautiful Greta Garbo was born in Sweden. She left school at 14, modeled, won a scholarship to drama school, and became one of the silent era's biggest stars, later finding success in talkies. She made her last film in 1941, aged 36, and became reclusive.
Professor Scott E. Fahlman suggested that people could use the sign :-) for "joking" and :-( for "serious" on bulletin boards.
Chubby Checker's 1960s cover of Hank Ballard's song "The Twist" made No. 1 in the US charts.
Chubby Checker's song 'The Twist' topped American magazine Billboard's list of the most popular singles to have appeared in its Hot 100 since the list's debut in 1958.
A day to celebrate the original spirits-based mixed drink, the punch, particularly the Rum Punch.
A day celebrating paella, a Spanish rice dish whose birthplace is Valencia in the mid-19th century. The annual World Paella Day Cup is held today in Valencia.

event_title: DNA's role in heredity confirmed by Hershey and Chase
description: Scientists Alfred Hershey and Martha Chase published a report confirming DNA holds hereditary data after they proved that DNA, not proteins, held the secret to life and carried genes, by experimenting on viruses that infect bacteria.

event_title: Billie Jean King wins 'The Battle of the Sexes' tennis match
description: Top women's tennis player Billie Jean King beat retired male No. 1 player Bobby Riggs 6-4, 6-3, 6-3, in a match billed as The Battle of the Sexes, watched by over 50 million worldwide.
Today is observed as the International Day of Peace, an occasion to work towards peace in the world and for warring parties to put down their weapons.
Paloma Difford, the Managing Director, celebrates her birthday.
The International Day of Peace was established by the United Nations General Assembly.

event_title: International Day of Peace Designated Day of Non-Violence
description: Two decades after its establishment, the International Day of Peace was designated a day of non-violence and cease-fire by a unanimous vote of the General Assembly.

event_title: Cultural Reference to 'The 21st Night of September'
description: The date is famously referenced in the lyrics of Earth, Wind & Fire's song 'September', released in 1978, which reached No. 1 on the US Billboard Hot R&B Songs chart, No. 8 on the US Billboard Hot 100, and No. 3 on the UK Singles Chart.
J.R.R. Tolkien's novel 'The Hobbit' was released on this day, which would later have a significant impact on English literature.

White chocolate, technically not considered chocolate due to the absence of cocoa solids, was first introduced to the world in 1936 when Swiss confectioner Nestlé launched the Galak, better known as 'Milkybar'.
A day to encourage people to opt out of using vehicles and instead use public transport, cycle, or walk. In some cities, roads are closed to powered traffic.

event_title: Anniversary of the last Salem hangings
description: The mass hysteria of the Salem witch trials resulted in the execution of 20 people, most of them women, with the last eight of them being hanged on this day.

Bi-Visibility Day, also known as International Celebrate Bisexuality Day, was established by Wendy Curry, Michael Page and Gigi Raven Wilbur and first recognised in 1999. Since then, on 23rd September, events take place globally to raise awareness and call for positive change for the bisexual community.

Victoria Martin, the first female presidential candidate in the US, was born on this day in 1838. She ran for president as a representative of the Equal Rights Party at age 34. She was also the first woman to start a weekly newspaper and the first to run a Wall Street brokers' firm.
Jeff Rubin founded National Punctuation Day to promote the correct usage of punctuation.
This day celebrates the Cherries Jubilee, a dessert Auguste Escoffier prepared to mark Queen Victoria's Jubilee.
F. Scott Fitzgerald, author of 'The Great Gatsby', was born.
Soichiro Honda incorporated his company, Honda Motor Company, in Japan.
Established in 2017, Sourest Day is a celebration of all things sour – not so much sour-faced people, but rather tangy, zesty treats in the form of candy and desserts.
National One-Hit Wonder Day was established in 1990 by the American music journalist Steven Rosen, celebrating songs that achieved widespread popularity for a brief period.
Concorde slashed the transatlantic air-crossing record, making the journey between Washington and Paris in just over three and a half hours and averaging an impressive 1,535 kph (954 mph).
The UN celebrates World Tourism Day.
Marked annually on September 28th, National Drink Beer Day is a celebratory day that finds broad appeal as beer is the world's No. 1 alcoholic beverage.
World Rabies Day aims to spread awareness and advice on how to prevent this deadly disease, which causes thousands of deaths every year.

event_title: First edition of Radio Times published
description: The first-ever edition of the Radio Times magazine, featuring the slogan 'The Official Organ of the B.B.C.', was published.
The Hungarian–American icon, Zsa Zsa Gabor, was jailed after slapping a police officer who objected to her driving her Rolls without a licence but with an open flask of Jack Daniel's.
An annual observance dedicated to raising awareness about heart disease and stroke, which collectively kill more than 17 million people a year.

On September 30, 1955, the iconic 1950s actor James Dean died at the age of 24 in a car collision. He was driving his new Porsche Spyder to a race meeting when an oncoming car veered into his lane. Dean was pronounced dead on arrival at the hospital, four years into a meteoric career with roles in 'Rebel without a Cause', 'Giant', and 'East of Eden'.

On September 30, 1912, six men, part of Scott's ill-fated South Pole expedition, walked out of Antarctica after surviving an entire winter in an ice cave. They had been left in January 1912 and were unable to be picked up by their ship due to pack ice. They spent almost seven months in a snow cave, emerging to hunt, before abandoning their shelter and walking for five weeks across Antarctica. Upon arrival, they learned of Scott's death.

event_title: Neville Chamberlain's 'Peace for our time' speech
description: On September 30, 1938, British Prime Minister Neville Chamberlain returned from Munich, declaring an agreement with German Chancellor Herr Hitler as symbolic of a desire to avoid war. This statement, famously known as 'Peace for our time,' was quickly undermined as Hitler marched into Czechoslovakia's Sudetenland the next morning, leading to war between Britain and Germany within 12 months. Chamberlain resigned a year later and died by the end of 1940.

World Sake Day marks the beginning of the annual rice harvest, when sake production begins in Japan. Sake Day originated in 1978, when the Japan Sake and Shochu Makers Association designated October 1 as a day of sake celebration.
The International Coffee Organization (ICO) launched International Coffee Day, observed annually to celebrate the coffee bean and honor those involved in its production.

event_title: The New York Plaza Hotel's Birthday
description: New York's Plaza Hotel opened on this day in 1907. The 19-storey building featured 1,650 crystal chandeliers and became a New York icon, appearing in many movies.

Twickenham Stadium, in southwest London, opened on this day in 1909 with a match between the Harlequins and Richmond rugby union clubs. Constructed on land previously used to grow cabbage, it has been affectionately known as "the Cabbage Patch" ever since. Its agricultural leanings came back to the fore when it was used during World War I to graze cattle, horses and sheep. Owned and operated by the Rugby Football Union (RFU), it is currently the third-largest rugby union stadium in the world and is the home ground of the English rugby union team. Its inauguration capacity of 20,000 spectators has grown to 82,000.
After negotiations and a Unification Treaty, the German Democratic Republic (GDR) was incorporated into the Federal Republic of Germany (FRG).
The 4th of October is celebrated as National Vodka Day in America, and by extension, International Vodka Day worldwide.
The 4th of October is celebrated as National Taco Day in America.

event_title: Soviet Union launched Sputnik 1, marking the start of the Space Age
description: The Space Age officially began when the Soviet Union launched Sputnik 1, the first human-created device to enter space. This artificial satellite, about the size of a beachball, was the forerunner of thousands of satellites and the opening gambit in the race that would lead to men walking on the moon.
World Teachers Day is celebrated, a chance to celebrate the role this often-maligned profession plays in all our lives.
The Beatles released their very first single: Love Me Do. It reached 17 in the UK charts.
World Sake Day marks the beginning of the annual rice harvest, when sake production begins in Japan, running all the way through to April.
Mad Hatter Day is celebrated on October 6th, derived from the '10/6' price tag on the Mad Hatter's hat in Alice's Adventures in Wonderland, which in America is interpreted as October 6th.

Charles-Édouard Jeanneret-Gris, better known as Le Corbusier, one of the founding fathers of modern architecture, was born. He was known for his contributions to modern architecture, painting, and a lifelong feud with Salvador Dali.
World Sake Day marks the beginning of the annual rice harvest and the start of sake production in Japan, which continues through April.
An American "National Day" marked annually on October 7th, National Frapp
International Octopus Day is observed to raise awareness about octopuses, remarkable creatures with blue blood, three hearts, and highly developed intelligence, which are at risk from overfishing.
The nation of Croatia declared its independence from the former Yugoslavia in 1991, following a long and bloody war.
In 2008, then Chancellor Alistair Darling announced a £500 billion bailout of the British banking sector by the UK government to prevent its collapse, as banks were refusing to lend to each other.
Bolivian paramilitaries, working with the CIA, executed Che Guevara.
260 million cubic metres of trees, rock and soil roared into the Vajont Dam reservoir, causing a 250m wave that wiped out five villages and at least 2,000 people.
Marks the beginning of the annual rice harvest, when sake production begins in Japan.

World Porridge Day is an international event to raise funds for Mary's Meals, a charity that aids starving children in developing countries, providing nutritious mugs of maize porridge as part of its daily school feeding programme.

The town of Lake Havasu City in Arizona debuted a new bridge - London Bridge. Arizonan entrepreneur Robert P. McCulloch had bought the original London Bridge, shipped its individually numbered stones to Arizona, and rebuilt it over a local canal, a project which took four years to complete.
World Sake Day marks the beginning of the annual rice harvest, when sake production begins in Japan, running all the way through to April.

The world's first cocktail festival, London Cocktail Week, began on this day in 2010. Conceived by Simon Difford and organized by Hannah Sharman-Cox, the festival ran from October 11th to 17th, establishing a model for similar events globally.

Victor 'Trader Vic' Bergeron, a renowned restaurateur and entrepreneur, passed away on this day in 1984 at the age of 81. He founded Hinky Dink's in 1934 and was also known as a sculptor, painter, jewellery designer, and cook.
World Sake Day is celebrated annually on this day, marking the beginning of the rice harvest and sake production season in Japan, which continues through April.

The first Oktoberfest, celebrating the wedding of the crown prince of Bavaria (who became King Louis I) and Princess Therese von Sachsen-Hildburghausen, was held in Munich, Germany. City officials invited members of the public to celebrate the marriage on fields outside the city walls, with a horse race as the main attraction. This annual gathering is the origin of the Oktoberfest tradition.

event_title: Christopher Columbus arrived in the Americas / National Day in Spain
description: Christopher Columbus arrived in the Americas, claiming them for his Spanish employers. This day is celebrated as Spain's national day, Fiesta Nacional de España, and is a chance for Spaniards to celebrate their common Hispanic heritage.
World Sake Day marks the beginning of the annual rice harvest in Japan, when sake production begins and runs through to April.

event_title: Greenwich Mean Time (GMT) became the global benchmark for time
description: On this day in 1884, after a lengthy conference convened by the United States, Greenwich Mean Time (GMT), or the time in London's Greenwich observatory, became the benchmark for time around the world. This resolved issues with navigation caused by nations using different zero degrees longitude points on maps. Noon in Greenwich subsequently set the time zones for the rest of the world, and the line in Greenwich marked the division between east and west.

event_title: Mrs Thatcher's government lost a lawsuit to prevent the UK publication of Spycatcher
description: In 1988, Mrs Thatcher's government lost a lawsuit attempting to prevent the publication of 'Spycatcher' in the UK. The book, memoirs of a disgruntled ex-MI5 man, had already been published in America and Australia. Its publication significantly impacted MI5, transforming it into a more professional and open organization.

event_title: Andes Plane Disaster occurred, leading to an extraordinary survival story
description: On this day in 1972, a plane carrying 48 people disappeared over the Chilean Andes. Officials abandoned the search after eight days. The survivors, huddled at 12,000 feet, eventually resorted to cannibalism after exhausting airline food. An avalanche later killed nine more. Three strongest survivors walked out for help, and 72 days after the crash, 16 survivors were rescued in mid-December.

event_title: Introduction of the British Fifty Pence Piece
description: The Royal Mint introduced the British fifty pence piece. Intended to directly replace the ten-shilling note in the run-up to decimalisation, its bizarre, seven-sided shape was designed to prevent confusion with other round coins. In 1997, the coin shrank, reflecting its shrinking value.

Duke William II of Normandy's fleet invaded England, meeting the English army under Anglo-Saxon King Harold II near the present-day town of Battle in East Sussex. It was a decisive Norman victory, sealing William's conquest of England. Estimates suggest 2,000 invaders died along with about twice that number of Englishmen. William is said to have founded a monastery on the battle site, with the high altar of the abbey church at the spot where King Harold died.

A huge explosion shook the town of Senghenydd in Wales at 8 AM. More than 900 men were working underground in the local coal mine. Waves of fire and toxic gases swept through the mines, and rescuers were blocked by roof collapses and burning coal dust. By the time rescue attempts were abandoned three weeks later, 440 men and boys were tragically dead. Exactly 100 years later, the National Mining Memorial opened on the site of the Universal Colliery to commemorate those lost.

John Schrank attempted to kill ex-president Theodore Roosevelt. The murder attempt was apparently motivated by a dream in which William McKinley asked him to avenge his death. Schrank's bullet hit Roosevelt, but the politician was protected by a steel spectacles case and a 50-page copy of his speech. Roosevelt carried the bullet inside him until he died, and Schrank spent the rest of his life in an insane asylum.

event_title: Andy Green sets Land Speed Record
description: Andy Green set the Land Speed Record in Nevada's Black Rock Desert, reaching 1,228 km/h (763 mph) in the Thrust Supersonic Car (ThrustSSC) and becoming the first to break the sound barrier.
The Great Storm hit Britain, downing 15 million trees and bringing hurricane-force winds. It was the worst storm Britain had experienced since 1703.
World Sake Day marks the beginning of the annual rice harvest and sake production in Japan.
An unofficial 'National Holiday' from the USA, celebrating liqueurs.
For more than 30 years, today has been World Food Day, focusing on issues like global hunger, malnutrition, and sustainable food systems.
Oscar Wilde, son of a knighted surgeon and his artistic wife, was born in Dublin, Ireland. He went on to craft novels, plays, and poems.
Marks the beginning of the annual rice harvest in Japan, when sake production begins, running through to April.

A 7-meter (23-foot) tall vat holding 610,000 litres (135,000 imperial gallons) of beer ruptured at the Horse Shoe Brewery in London, causing a tidal wave of beer that flooded streets, destroyed two houses and a pub, and resulted in at least eight deaths. The disaster was declared an act of God.
Evel Knievel, the Guinness World Record holder for 'most bones ever broken in a lifetime' (433) and a famous daredevil known for impossible motorcycle leaps, was born.
Cravat Day is celebrated by order of the Croatian Parliament to honor the tie, which originated from Croatian mercenary soldiers who exported it to Europe.
Alaska Day commemorates the date in 1867 when the territory that is now Alaska passed from Russia to the United States.

Founded in 2010 and celebrated annually worldwide, this day started in memory of Mary Edith Keyburn, a G&T fan who passed away on this day in 2010, aged 95. In 2012, social media propelled the family celebration to a global event.
Mary Edith Keyburn, a G&T fan, passed away on this day in 2010, aged 95, with a gin and tonic by her side. Her passing inspired the creation of International Gin and Tonic Day.

The world's stock markets crashed, with the Dow Jones dropping by over 22%, the greatest percentage loss of all time. By the end of the month, the Hong Kong stock market had almost halved in value, Australia was down over 40%, and British stocks were worth less than three-quarters what they had been just two weeks earlier.
Marks the beginning of the annual rice harvest, when sake production begins in Japan, running all the way through to April.
One of the 20th century's architectural icons was unveiled to the world when the Queen opened the Sydney Opera House.
An all-American creation, National Mezcal Day is observed globally to show appreciation for the artisanal Mexican producers who craft this agave spirit.

event_title: Apple Day Launched in the UK
description: Launched in 1990 in the UK by the Dorset-based English charity Common Ground, Apple Day celebrates the fruit and highlights the loss of traditional varieties of apples, as well as the diversity of landscape, ecology, and culture.

event_title: Lord Nelson Died at the Battle of Trafalgar
description: On this day in 1805, the Battle of Trafalgar took place, where the British fleet, led by Lord Nelson, defeated the French and Spanish navies. Nelson tragically lost his life during the battle, his spine shattered by a bullet.

event_title: Harold Camping's Third Rapture Prediction Failed
description: Harold Camping, a doomsday predictor, had rescheduled his third prediction for the End of the World, or Rapture, for this day in 2011. Many of his followers gave up homes and jobs in preparation, but the event did not occur.
World Sake Day marks the beginning of the annual rice harvest in Japan, when sake production begins and runs through to April.

event_title: Sam Houston elected first president of the Republic of Texas
description: Sam Houston became the first elected president of the Republic of Texas, a nation that would exist for only a decade longer. The Republic of Texas sprang from a revolution in the Mexican state of Coahuila y Tejas.

event_title: Britain offered robotic heart surgery for the first time
description: Britain became the third country in Europe to offer robotic heart surgery, using the Da Vinci surgical robot. This system allows for surgery through small incisions between two ribs, leading to smaller scars, less operational stress, and faster recovery time.

Apple's iPod was unveiled by Steve Jobs, revolutionizing how music was enjoyed and impacting the music industry. The first iPod had a 5 GB hard drive, capable of holding 1,000 songs. Production of standalone music players ceased in 2017.
Montgomery's army engaged German forces at El Alamein, marking a turning point in World War II.

event_title: Boris Pasternak Awarded and Refused Nobel Prize
description: Boris Pasternak was awarded the Nobel Prize for Literature but was forced by Soviet authorities to renounce it, fearing he would be refused re-entry to the Soviet Union if he accepted.
The Smurfs, the little blue creatures, made their debut.

Ludwig Leichhardt, a German-born explorer and one of the best all-round scientists of his time, was born. He is known for covering great swathes of uncharted Australia, and for his mysterious disappearance during an expedition in 1848.

The United Nations was established with the goal of preventing dreadful conflicts from ever happening again, following the aftermath of World War II. It is now the world's largest international organization, with 193 member nations.
United Nations Resolution 2782 was passed, decreeing that United Nations Day should be observed and recommending it be a public holiday in member states.

event_title: Analogue TV Ended in the UK
description: The 24th October 2012 marked the end of analogue TV in the UK, over a century after the first proposals for an electric TV system and more than 80 years after the first British TV broadcasts. The spectrum cleared by switching off analogue signals became available for mobile technology.

Today is the one and only, official, World Pasta Day. And, no, that's not an internet invention. Today really IS World Pasta Day. And to commemorate it, the great and the good of the pasta-making world will be gathered to enjoy a riveting day based around the theme of "Pasta: the perfect choice for a tasty, healthy and sustainable diet".

Celebrating all forms of art, International Artist Day recognises the significance of art in history and culture all over the world. For thousands of years, art has captured revolutions and rebellions, love and war, the human and the other-worldly. Be it sculpture, painting, photography, music or architecture, art captures the essence of life, distilling a myriad of emotions into a single form.

Pablo Picasso was born this day in 1881. Picasso shares his birthday with International Artist Day, and we can think of nobody better to represent art in so many guises - be it cubism, surrealism, modernism, symbolism and so much more. In fact, it's rumoured that Picasso's first word was 'lapiz' (Spanish for 'pencil')!

Geoffrey Chaucer was crucial in establishing the legitimacy of vernacular Middle English when the dominant literary languages in England were French and Latin. Widely considered the greatest English poet of the Middle Ages, and most famously known for his Canterbury Tales, on this day in 1400 Chaucer became the first poet to be buried in Poet's Corner in Westminster Abbey. Canterbury Tales differs from other literature of the period in terms of its naturalism, its varied characters and the variety of stories the pilgrims tell.

The humble pumpkin is the star of the show each 26th of October. It's thought that the pumpkin became associated with Halloween in the 1800s when 'Jack-o'-lanterns' (carved pumpkins lit by candle from within) started appearing in the windows of American homes. Jack-o'-lanterns are said to originate from the old Irish folk tale of Stingy Jack, and the potato famine that saw many Irish immigrants make the journey to America.

The now-famous Gunfight at the O.K. Corral was a shootout between lawmen (Doc Holliday and brothers Virgil, Morgan and Wyatt Earp) and a group of outlaws called the Cowboys (Billy Claiborne, brothers Ike and Billy Clanton, and brothers Tom and Frank McLaury). It occurred at Tombstone, Arizona, USA. Some 30 shots were fired in about 30 seconds, leaving Billy Clanton and both of the McLaury brothers dead, with Virgil, Morgan and Doc Holliday wounded. Wyatt Earp was unscathed. The gunfight was recorded in a 1931 biography and made famous by the 1946 film, My Darling Clementine.
The artist William Hogarth, known for works like Gin Lane, Harlot's Progress, and Rake's Progress, died on this day. He is arguably the father of modern-day cartoons.

Before the first meeting of the Football Association in the Freemasons' Tavern in Great Queen Street, London, there were no nationally recognised football rules. The Football Association (now the oldest football association in the world) was formed to agree on a set of rules. Original suggestions for rules allowed players to handle the ball, while Blackheath FC were very keen to keep the practice of 'hacking', or kicking an opponent in the shins.
October 27 is National American Beer Day, a day to try a beer from one of over 7,000 breweries creating the definitive thirst-quencher in America.

event_title: The New York City Subway opened
description: The New York City Subway opened on this day in 1904 when at 2:35 pm Mayor George McClellan took the controls on the inaugural journey. It is one of the world's oldest public transit systems.

event_title: Independence Day in Saint Vincent and The Grenadines
description: Saint Vincent and the Grenadines celebrates its independence, gained from the UK in 1979.
World Amaro Day was established by Amaro Lucano in 2024 to celebrate Italy's tradition of bittersweet liqueurs.

event_title: The Volstead Act passed into law
description: On this day in 1919, the US Congress voted the Eighteenth Amendment, also known as the Volstead Act or the National Prohibition Act, into law.

event_title: National Chocolate Day in the US
description: October 28 is observed as National Chocolate Day in the US.
US lifestyle writer Colleen Paige took it upon herself to raise awareness about the number of cats that need to be rescued each year.

event_title: Anniversary of The Wall Street Crash (Black Tuesday)
description: The US stock market lost over $14 billion in a single day, an event that most consider marks the beginning of the Great Depression.

Candy corn, a sweet treat, has its own national day. It was created in the 1800s by George Renninger at the Wunderlee Candy Company. Originally named 'Chicken Feed', it was mass manufactured by the Goelitz Confectionery Company (now Jelly Belly) starting in 1898. By the 1950s, advertising made it synonymous with Halloween.

In Detroit and other parts of the US, October 30 is Devil's Night, a variation of the old English Mischief Night. It began after World War II with pranks and arson. Since the 1990s, Detroit has rebranded it as Angel's Night, with volunteers protecting against arson.
This day was created in 1999 to encourage people to plan their own funerals.

Traditionally, Halloween is the eve of the Western Christian feast of All Hallows' Day, which is dedicated to remembering the dead, including saints (the hallows) and departed believers. In many parts of the world, the Christian observances of All Hallows' Eve include lighting candles on the graves of the dead.

New York City's Greenwich Village has hosted the world's largest Halloween parade. It stretches over a mile (1.6 kilometres) and includes more than 60,000 costumed participants, circus performers, floats, live bands and giant puppets.
World Sake Day marks the beginning of the annual rice harvest, when sake production begins in Japan, running all the way through to April.

event_title: Day of the Dead (Día de los Muertos) festivities
description: Festivities are held on November 1st (All Saints' Day) and November 2nd (All Souls Day), a celebration which (almost) seamlessly blends Christian beliefs with activities so firmly pre-Christian the Aztecs would probably recognize them. Traditionally, today honours deceased children and tomorrow honours adults who have passed away.
In Catholicism, it's All Saints' Day, or All Hallows, or Hallowmas -- a day to commemorate everyone who has died and gone to heaven.
Today is about raising awareness of the positive impact a vegan lifestyle can have upon the planet, yourself and the animal kingdom.
The Vegan Society was founded in 1944.

event_title: Louise Wallis founded World Vegan Day
description: In 1994, Louise Wallis founded World Vegan Day in celebration.
The dominant red wine grape variety from the north of Greece steps into the global wine map with its very own day.
The Women's Royal Naval Service (Wrens) was created during the First World War to free up men for combat by having women do cooking, cleaning, telephony and intelligence.

event_title: Women allowed into the Royal Navy
description: On this day in 1993, women were allowed into the Royal Navy, integrating women sailors into the Navy and closing the separate Women's Royal Naval Service (Wrens).
On the Feast of All Saints in 1512, Pope Julius II inaugurated the Vatican's Sistine Chapel with a solemn Mass and revealed Michelangelo's magnificent artwork.

A celebratory day dedicated to the deviled egg, a dish made from hard-boiled eggs filled with a paste of egg yolk, mayonnaise, and mustard. The name 'devilled' was used in the nineteenth century for spicy or zesty foods.

event_title: Maiden Flight of the Spruce Goose (H-4 Hercules)
description: The H-4 Hercules, also known as the Spruce Goose, made its maiden and only flight on this day in 1947. Piloted by Howard Hughes Jr., the aircraft, boasting the largest wingspan of any ever built, flew just over one mile at an altitude of 70 feet for one minute above Long Beach, California.

event_title: BBC Television Service began regular broadcasts
description: The BBC Television Service, the world's first regular 'high-definition' broadcasting service, commenced its regular programming on this day in 1936 from the basement of London's Broadcasting House. It was later renamed BBC1 in 1964.
David Augustus Embury, author of 'The Fine Art of Mixing Drinks', was born on this day in 1886 in Pine Woods, upstate New York.

November 4th is dedicated to 'candy'. Candy can be dated back to between the 4th and 6th centuries BC when ancient Indians boiled sugarcane juice. It held a major place in cultural history globally, used as medicine before the Industrial Revolution, and became a childhood staple in America by the 18th century.

On this day in 1922, Egyptologist Howard Carter's team discovered the tomb of Tutankhamun in the Valley of the Kings, revealing the burial site of the young king, complete with artifacts including his death mask.

event_title: Publication of The Interpretation of Dreams
description: On this day in 1899, Austrian neurologist Sigmund Freud published 'The Interpretation of Dreams', a work that would later inspire Surrealists and transform the understanding of the human mind by suggesting dreams reflect subconscious desires and anxieties.

On this day in 2003, the Liverpudlian soap opera 'Brookside' aired its last ever episode. Known for being cutting-edge, it featured the first pre-watershed lesbian kiss, delved into domestic violence and drug addiction, and featured an out gay man in a non-judgemental fashion.

event_title: Guy Fawkes' Arrest and Gunpowder Plot
description: On November 5, 1605, Guy Fawkes was arrested while attempting to blow up the Houses of Parliament, the King, and install a Catholic queen. His co-conspirators were also apprehended and sentenced to be hung, drawn, and quartered.
On November 5, 1992, scientists confirmed the discovery of the world's oldest beer in Iran, dating back 5,000 years, evidenced by calcium oxalate (beerstone) found in an old jug.
Irish Coffee arrived in America on this day, a legacy attributed to Stanton Delaplane, a columnist for the San Francisco Chronicle.
A day dedicated to fried tortilla chips. Nachos were invented by Ignacio Anaya for his customers in 1941 at the Victory Club restaurant in Coahulia.
A day designated as 'Hot Scotch & Lemon Day', also known as 'Hot Toddy Day'.

event_title: Gustavus Adolphus the Great died in battle
description: Gustavus Adolphus the Great, who transformed Sweden into a major European player, died in battle on this day. Swedes commemorate his legacy.

event_title: The Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) opened
description: New York City's The Museum of Modern Art opened to the public. Affectionately known as MoMA, it is among the world's largest and most influential contemporary art museums, housing vast collections of books, catalogues, and works across various artistic mediums.

Marie Skłodowska-Curie, the first woman to become a professor at the University of Paris, the first woman to win a Nobel Prize, and the first person to win two Nobel Prizes, was born in Warsaw, Poland. She discovered radium and polonium, pioneered the study of the atom, and drove ambulances during World War I.
National Harvey Wallbanger Day is celebrated in America, and Harvey Wallbanger Cake is also a recognized item.
At 11:28pm, Asteroid 2005 YU55 passed less than the moon's distance (330,000 km/200,000 miles) from Earth. The next day, it passed even closer to the Moon.

On this day, protestors tore down the Berlin Wall, which had divided communist East Germany from capitalist West Germany since 1961. The wall was a potent symbol of Communist power, and its fall was a significant moment for Germany and the world, signaling the beginning of a new era as the Iron Curtain fell.
Stanton Delaplane and Jack Koeppler of San Francisco's Buena Vista cafe successfully recreated the Irish Coffee, which Stanton had discovered in Dublin airport. This led to its global phenomenon.
Apple's first iPod made its debut, transforming portable music listening and paving the way for the iPhone.
Bill Gates unveiled Windows 1.0, a revolutionary piece of software priced at $99, which included applications like notepad, clock, calendar, and paint, aiming to simplify PC usage beyond MS-DOS.

Douglas Mawson set off from Antarctica's Cape Denison to explore its eastern coast. The expedition faced extreme hardship, including the loss of a man, supplies, and dogs, with Mawson eventually surviving alone against incredible odds.
World War I hostilities formally ended at the 11th hour of the 11th day of the 11th month.

event_title: Last Casualty of World War I
description: Baltimore's Henry Gunther, the last casualty of World War I, died just one minute before the scheduled start of peace.
The minute's silence came into being as a way to commemorate the horrendous loss of life on both sides of World War I, and is now observed worldwide as a tribute to fallen soldiers.

November 11 is observed as Veterans Day in the US, Remembrance Day across the Commonwealth, and Armistice Day in the UK, France and Belgium, remembering those who fell during World War I and subsequent wars.

National Happy Hour Day is celebrated on November 12. The concept of 'happy hour' is said to have originated in America with the US Navy in the early 20th century, as a period for activities to overcome the monotony at sea. The Prohibition-era term 'Cocktail Hour' also influenced its promotion to entice customers into bars earlier.

World Tomatini Day is held annually on November 12, marking the start of a week of events celebrating the Tomatini cocktail. This signature cocktail was created in 2011 by Jimmy Barrat at La Petite Maison (LPM) restaurant in Dubai and is now served in all LPM restaurants globally.

On November 12, 1933, Hugh Gray reportedly photographed a mysterious creature in Loch Ness. While many believe it was a blurred image of an animal like a dog, otter, or seal, this photograph sparked a booming industry around the Loch Ness Monster. Subsequent searches by cryptozoologists have yielded little evidence, with one notable 'find' on March 31 being an April Fool's joke.
World Kindness Day, founded in 1998 by the World Kindness Movement, is observed globally to promote acts of kindness and build a kinder, more compassionate world.
Academic, journalist, novelist, and bard of American cocktails, Bernard DeVoto died, known for his work 'The Hour: A Cocktail Manifesto'.
Celebrated annually on 14th November, this day originated in America.
American libertarian, journalist, author and political satirist, P.J. O'Rourke celebrates his birthday.
India celebrates Children's Day to honour its youth. Jawaharlal Nehru, the first Prime Minister of India, was born on this day in 1889.

event_title: Christopher Columbus made the first recorded reference to tobacco
description: Christopher Columbus made the first recorded reference to tobacco after being gifted some dried tobacco leaves by a native population on a Bahamian island. His men later discovered the purpose of the leaves, and Columbus introduced tobacco to Europe.

event_title: King Camp Gillette received a patent for a new kind of razor
description: King Camp Gillette received a patent for a new kind of razor, which required two years of experimentation to produce hard, thin, and cheap steel for disposable blades. His company was later sold for over 50 billion dollars.

event_title: Founding of San Cristóbal de La Habana (Havana's Birthday)
description: Spanish colonists first said Mass on the spot where the city was founded as San Cristóbal de La Habana in a natural bay.

Also known as "Take a Hike Day", this American national day celebrates getting out in the wild and experiencing nature first-hand. Hiking, a hobby enjoyed by millions, offers benefits such as rushes of endorphins and serotonin and improvements to physical health.

event_title: Anniversary of the start of the coronavirus outbreak
description: The 17th of November 2019 marks the first case of the coronavirus, traced to a 55-year-old man in the Hubei Province, China.

event_title: International Poitín Day launched and established
description: International Poitín Day was both launched and established in 2015 at an event held at The Sun Tavern in London's Bethnal Green by Bán Poitín.

event_title: Mickey Mouse's birthday / Release of Steamboat Willie
description: On this day in 1928, Walt Disney released a cartoon called Steamboat Willie, featuring a character named Mickey Mouse. This is considered his birthday, though it was not his first appearance on the big screen. Disney's original design was for a character called Mortimer Mouse.

event_title: Amazon released its very first Kindle
description: Four and a half hours after that, the device sold out. And a technology that many had been talking about for years sprang fully-fledged into life. Jeff Bezos' baby was, of course, not the first e-reader. Project Gutenberg had been making digital books since 1971; books had been available as CD-Roms since the mid-80s; the first ebook readers had been released as early as 1998; and Sony had its own version out in 2006. It was, however, the Kindle that tipped the casual reader away from the notion that books have pages to be turned towards the idea that it might just be easier to pack all the reading you need for a holiday onto one small device.

an awareness day promoted by the WTO (that's the World TOILET Organisation, not the World Trade Organisation, and definitely not the World Toilet ASSOCIATION, which is a competing group). At the head of this fantastic organisation is one of two competing Mr Toilets, a Singaporean entrepreneur named Jack Sim, who, long before the awful "Let's Talk Bums" sewer-clogging campaign, was working to promote good toileting around the world. This World Toilet Day, Mr Toilet would like us to take to social media and inform the world that #wegiveashit. Why? Because 1.1 billion people around the world defecate in the open; 2.5 billion people do not have a clean toilet; and that every dollar spent on sanitation returns $5 in public health.

Since the 1960s, there have been plenty of women's days around the world, but not so many for the chaps. International Men's Day, which falls on this day every year, is an occasion to honour positive male role models, while raising money for men's health charities and the like. It's also a great chance for anyone who's been growing a 'tache for Movember to get out and about with like-minded chaps.

In 1908, Charles Gibson challenged bartender Charles Connelly at The Players Club, New York, to improve the classic Martini. Connelly created the Gibson Martini by swapping the signature olive garnish with a silver onion.

Astronomer Edwin Hubble, known for showing that Andromeda was a galaxy and that the universe was expanding, was born in Missouri on November 20, 1889. The Hubble Space Telescope, launched in 1990, is named after him.

On November 20, 2012, the last typewriter built in the UK rolled out of its North Wales factory, destined for the Science Museum in London, marking the end of over a century of technological history in the UK.

event_title: United Nations proclaimed World Television Day
description: Against considerable opposition, the United Nations proclaimed today World Television Day, a chance to highlight the role of communications, good and bad, in raising awareness of global issues.
National Cranberry Relish Day is observed, a staple of Thanksgiving and Christmas dinner, particularly popular in the US.
Lee Harvey Oswald assassinated the 35th president of the United States, John F. Kennedy, in Dallas, Texas. The act sent shockwaves around the world.

The first espresso machine patent was issued to Angelo Moriondo in Turin, Italy. His machine, called 'New steam machinery for the economic and instantaneous confection of coffee beverage, method 'A. Moriondo'', brewed coffee in bulk.

event_title: Cranberries served at the first Thanksgiving
description: Cranberries are believed to have been served at the very first Thanksgiving in Plymouth, Massachusetts.
November 23rd is celebrated as St George's Day (Giorgoba) in Georgia, one of two days of the year locals celebrate their patron saint.
The Palais Royale saloon in San Francisco installed the world's first jukebox, featuring four earpieces attached to a phonograph.

event_title: First episode of Doctor Who debuted
description: The first-ever episode of Doctor Who, 'An Unearthly Child', debuted with William Hartness as the Doctor.

National Sardines Day is observed to celebrate these surprisingly nutritious fishes, which are packed full of goodness such as vitamin B12, Omega 3 fatty acids, and other health-boosting vitamins and minerals. They are the second most consumed fish by humans and are very versatile.

event_title: Charles Darwin published 'On the Origin of Species'
description: English naturalist and geologist Charles Darwin published his defining work, 'On the Origin of Species'. The book sold out the same day, and its theory of evolution transformed biology, theology, medicine, and more.

Buy Nothing Day is observed, challenging people to spend absolutely nothing for an entire day. The initiative, presented by Adbusters, aims to raise awareness about overconsumption and its impact on global resources and labor practices.

Maize Day, celebrating corn, was inadvertently started in 2004 by artist Corrine Lightweaver and her family, who observed Thanksgiving from the standpoint of indigenous peoples. Corn itself was first domesticated by indigenous peoples of southern Mexico about ten thousand years ago.

Karl Friedrich Michael Vaillant, later Karl Benz, was born in Karlsruhe, Germany. He went on to design a 'horseless carriage', founded Mercedes-Benz, and invented an engine design still used today. He patented the world's first commercially available automobile, the three-wheeled Patent Motorwagen, in 1886.
Farmer and businessman Bernard Trevor Matthews died at the age of 79. He started a three hundred million Pound business and was known for his catchphrase 'They're bootiful'.

Quintus Horatius Flaccus, the Roman poet known for phrases like 'carpe diem' and 'nunc est bibendum', died. He was a prolific author of poems and rose through Roman society, associating with emperors, generals, and politicians despite being the son of a freed slave.

event_title: New Zealand women vote for the first time
description: New Zealand women cast their vote in a general election for the first time ever, having won the right with support from male politicians, decades before the US or the UK.

event_title: Pong launched, kick-starting the video game era
description: Pong, the devilishly simple game that kick-started the video game era, launched on this day in 1972.

Scots worldwide celebrate their patron saint, St Andrew, who intervened in a memorable battle against the Angles approximately a thousand years ago. Andrew is the patron saint of fishermen and Russia.

Movember, a global charity event raising funds for men's health, concludes on this day. Since its launch in Melbourne in 2003, over 5 million participants have raised more than 
300 million.

December 01 is observed as National Christmas Lights Day, celebrating the electric Christmas light, which was invented by Thomas Edison in 1880. These lights replaced candles on Christmas trees, reducing fires and starting a new trend in Christmas décor.

event_title: Rosa Parks' Refusal on Montgomery Bus
description: On December 01, 1955, African-American activist Rosa Parks refused to give up her seat in the 'coloured' section of a segregated bus in Montgomery, Alabama, leading to her arrest and becoming a key figure in the civil rights movement.
On December 01, 1933, US Prohibition ended when the House of Representatives passed the Twenty-first Amendment.

This day serves to raise awareness of the negative impact of man-made pollution and the threat of industrial accidents to life on earth. It was established in memory of those who lost their lives in the Bhopal gas disaster.

One of the world's worst industrial incidents occurred when 45 tons of methyl isocyanate gas escaped from a pesticide plant in Bhopal, Madhya Pradesh, India. Over half a million people were exposed, with tens of thousands dying in the aftermath.

A Seattle dentist named Barney Clark received the first permanent artificial heart, made of polyester, plastic and aluminium, and powered by an external pump. He survived for 112 days after the operation.
US Prohibition came to an end when the House of Representatives passed the Twenty-first Amendment.

event_title: Illinois became the 21st state in the United States
description: On this day, Illinois officially joined the United States as its 21st state. It developed into a major industrial and agricultural hub, with Chicago becoming a leading cultural, economic, and population center.

On this day, the first human heart transplant was performed by Dr. Christiaan Barnard at Groote Schuur hospital in Cape Town for patient Louis Washkansky. This procedure marked a significant medical breakthrough.
On this day, Joseph Conrad, the author of 'Heart of Darkness', was born in Poland. Christened Józef Teodor Konrad Korzeniowski, he chose to write in English, a language he learned in his twenties.

Cabernet Franc Day has been celebrated on December 4th since 2015. The grape variety itself is believed to have originated in the 12th century in the Spanish País Vasco (Basque Country) where it was cultivated by Roncesvalles priests.
Shawn Corey Carter, known as Jay-Z, was born on this day. He is an iconic rapper, entrepreneur, and musician.
Musician, composer, film director, and wit Frank Zappa died on this day in 1993.
US Prohibition came to an end on this day in 1933 when the House of Representatives passed the Twenty-first Amendment.
The "Noble Experiment" – US Prohibition – came to an end when the House of Representatives passed the Twenty-first Amendment to the United States Constitution, a date now celebrated as Repeal Day.

Saint Nicholas, also known as Santa Claus and Bishop of Myra in Turkey, died on this day more than 1,600 years ago. He is known for restoring burned children to health, saving falsely accused men from death, and innocent girls from slavery.

The Encyclopaedia Britannica, the world's biggest printed encyclopaedia, was first published on this day in 1768. The first three-volume set was published in Edinburgh, Scotland, by Andrew Bell, Colin MacFarquhar, and William Smellie.

event_title: Anniversary of the Dead Parrot Sketch
description: The eighth episode of Monty Python's Flying Circus aired on British television, featuring the iconic 'Dead Parrot' sketch with John Cleese and Michael Palin. The sketch involved a disgruntled customer trying to return a deceased Norwegian Blue parrot.
Apollo 17 launched, and Mission Commander Eugene Cerman became the last person to walk on the moon.

event_title: The pneumatic bicycle tyre was patented
description: Ayrshire vet John Boyd Dunlop received a patent for the pneumatic bicycle tyre, a groundbreaking invention that improved bicycle performance significantly.
The Noble Experiment – US Prohibition – came to an end when the House of Representatives passed the Twenty-first Amendment.

The legend states that the first brownie was created at Chicago's Palmer House Hotel in 1893, when Mrs Palmer requested a small, cake-like dessert for boxed lunches for ladies attending the Chicago World's Columbian Exposition. Modern-style chocolate brownie recipes began appearing in print at the turn of the twentieth century.

On this day every year, Bodhi Day, the Japanese celebrate the enlightenment of Buddha, the moment at which Gautama Siddhartha, while sitting under a bodhi tree, found the solution to the problems he was confronting and achieved Nirvana. In Japan, the festival is known as "rohatsu", or "eighth day of the twelfth month".

event_title: Pretend To Be A Time Traveller Day
description: This day encourages people to act as a visitor from a utopian future, channel the spirit of a visitor from the past, or imagine a dystopian future.

A day to celebrate pastries. The humble pastry has its roots in ancient and classical times, with Egyptians, Romans, and Greeks using filo-type pastry. Shortcrust and puff versions became familiar in the Mediaeval period, and in the 17th century, pastry became extremely fashionable.

Britain's Coronation Street, the world's longest-running soap opera, debuted on this day in 1960. It features life in the fictional town of Weatherfield and has included guest appearances from Prince Charles to Sir Ian McKellen.

Lucius Beebe, restaurant critic, bon viveur, photographer, drinks geek, and author of The Stork Club Bar Book, was born on this day in 1902. He was known for his wardrobe, wit, and appetite for food and drink.
In 1888, a government act asked local authorities to take responsibility for roads, effectively getting rid of most toll roads in the UK, which were considered an inequitable form of taxation.
On this day in 2003, the M6 toll motorway opened in the UK, offering an alternative to congested routes around Birmingham.

On this day in 1952, a toxic cloud of smog, enriched with sulphuric acid, hydrochloric acid, smoke, and fluorides, finally cleared from London after hovering for four days. At least four thousand people died, leading to the Clean Air Act of 1956.
Victor Bergeron, known as Trader Vic, a bar legend, was born in downtown San Francisco. He was instrumental in taking the 'Tiki' nightlife genre to a new and global level.

event_title: Winston Churchill awarded Nobel Prize in Literature
description: Winston Churchill was awarded the Nobel laureate for literature. His speeches were inspirational and his books were significant.
The US Prohibition, known as the Noble Experiment, came to an end when the House of Representatives passed the Twenty-first Amendment.
A national day to celebrate the revolutionary effect of apps on our lives and the tech industry.
Established by the UN General Assembly to encourage sustainable development in mountains.
US Prohibition came to an end when the House of Representatives passed the Twenty-first Amendment.

event_title: National Poinsettia Day and Joel Roberts Poinsett's Passing
description: December 12th was chosen to mark National Poinsettia Day, commemorating the day of Joel Roberts Poinsett's passing in 1851. Poinsett, the first U.S Ambassador to Mexico and a keen botanist, sent cuttings of the plant home in 1828. The plant, originally known as Cuetlaxochitl by the Aztecs, was used for decoration, dye, and medicine.
Frank Sinatra, an enduring musical icon and successful actor, was born on this day in 1915. He was known for his singing and acting, and was a central figure in the Rat Pack.
The Noble Experiment – US Prohibition – came to an end on this day in 1933 when the House of Representatives passed the Twenty-first Amendment.

Today is National Cocoa Day, a day to celebrate chocolate's key ingredient. Cocoa is a highly concentrated powder made from the seeds of the Theobroma Cacao, widely cultivated in the Amazon Basin around 4,000 years ago and used by the Mayans and Aztecs.

Today in St Lucia, the country celebrates its independence. Britain returned St Lucia to the St Lucians in 1979, after ruling it alternately with the French from the 17th century and then controlling it from the early 19th century.

On this day in 1951, American star Joan Bennett's husband/producer, Walter Wanger, shot her agent, Jennings Lang, after suspecting they were having an affair. Wanger served a four-month sentence for the incident.

event_title: The South Pole was conquered by Roald Amundsen
description: Norwegian explorer Roald Amundsen became the first human being to reach the South Pole, beating Robert Falcon Scott's expedition. Amundsen used traditional dogsleds and survival skills learned from Eskimos.

event_title: Eugene Cernan became the last man on the Moon
description: Eugene Cernan, the eleventh person to walk on the Moon, became the last man on the Moon as he was the last to re-enter the Lunar Module as Commander of Apollo 17, the final Apollo lunar landing.
Mariner 2, a small probe, successfully came within reach of Venus, transmitting data back to Earth. It continues to orbit the sun after its systems died.
Jerry Thomas, the godfather of modern bartending and author of the first known cocktail book, died in New York in 1885, aged 55, from apoplexy.
The classic film 'Gone With The Wind', starring Clark Gable as Rhett Butler, premiered on this day in 1939. During filming, Gable eloped with Carole Lombard.
US Prohibition, known as The Noble Experiment, came to an end on this day in 1933 when the House of Representatives passed the Twenty-first Amendment.

As an argument about tax and monopolies escalated, on this day in 1773 it culminated in a boat-based riot. As the captains and crew of three British ships lay sleeping, more than a hundred men dressed as Native Americans, complete with tomahawks, crept into Boston Harbor. Merchants and tea house owners in Massachusetts were increasingly aggravated about the Tea Act, which whacked huge taxes on imported tea, so on 16 December 1773 to stop the taxed, imported tea making it onto the quayside, they divided into three groups and stormed the ships, braking open the chests of tea the ships were carrying, and scattering tea leaves, worth over a million dollars in today's money, into the sea. The Boston Tea Party, was one of many acts that would lead to American independence.

event_title: First Powered Flight by Wright Brothers
description: Orville Wright took to the skies, ascending to about first storey height and flying for 12 seconds over less than 40 metres. Later the same day, Wilbur Wright flew for 59 seconds over almost 300 metres. The Wright brothers are credited with making the first powered flight.
Keith Richards, English musician, songwriter, and founding member of the Rolling Stones, was born in Dartford, Kent, England.

event_title: Hellé Nice breaks world land-speed record for women
description: Hellé Nice, French model, dancer, and racing-car driver, broke the world land-speed record for women by achieving 197.708 kmh (122.8 mph) at Montlhéry in her lapis-blue Bugatti.

event_title: 13th Amendment abolishes slavery in the USA
description: The 13th Amendment to the United States Constitution, officially abolishing slavery, was ratified.

event_title: Look for an Evergreen Day founded
description: Look for an Evergreen Day was founded by the National Arborist Association to celebrate the natural beauty of evergreen trees all year round.
The first decorated Christmas tree was recorded in Rigia, Latvia.
Édith Giovanna Gassion, the popular French singer known as Édith Piaf, was born.
Zanzibar celebrates the anniversary of its independence, though it is now part of Tanzania.
Indian forces invaded Goa, leading to the Portuguese surrender, and Goa became the smallest state in India.

In 1803, the United States concluded the biggest property deal in history, the Louisiana Purchase. For a mere $15 million, the US acquired 2,144,000 square kilometres (828,000 square miles) of land from France and doubled in size. On this day, both Louisiana and La Nouvelle Orléans became part of the United States.

event_title: Anniversary of Cardiff being proclaimed Capital of Wales
description: Cardiff was officially made a city in 1905, and 50 years later, it was proclaimed the capital of Wales, beating the historic town of Caernarfon.

Depending on the hemisphere, this day marks either the shortest day (Winter Solstice in the Northern Hemisphere) or the longest day (Summer Solstice in the Southern Hemisphere) of the year. It usually occurs on 21 December, but can be between 20 and 22 December.
British TV's most enduring bad guys, the Daleks, made their debut on this day. They appeared in their first series, doing battle with the Thals and the Ninth Doctor on their home planet, Skaro.
The classic coming-of-age film 'The Graduate', starring Dustin Hoffman as Benjamin Braddock, was released on this day.
A 3.6 magnitude earthquake occurred in Cumbria, felt across much of northern England, Scotland, Wales, and the Isle of Man. It caused no injuries or damage.

The original bore, the Centre Tube, of the Lincoln Tunnel, named after Abraham Lincoln, opened. Approximately 1 ½ miles / 2.4 km long, it runs under the Hudson River, connecting Weehawken, New Jersey, to Midtown Manhattan, New York City.

Srinivasa Ramanujan, an extraordinary mathematician, was born. Despite no formal training, his contributions to mathematical analysis, number theory, infinite series, and continuing fractions opened new areas of the discipline.
Due to Srinivasa Ramanujan's pioneering discoveries, his home country dedicated this day to him, and it is now celebrated globally as Mathematics Day.

More than two months after a plane carrying rugby players crashed in the snowy Andes on October 13, 1972, two men walked out of the mountains, leading rescuers to the remains of the plane and 14 other survivors.
Giacomo Puccini, born into a musical family in Tuscany, composed masterpieces such as La Boheme, Tosca, and Madame Butterfly. He died of throat cancer while writing Turandot.

Festivus, an alternative ritual established by Frank Constanza in a Seinfeld episode, reached the public domain. It is based on the real-life family tradition of one of the show's writers, Dan O'Keeffe, and features an aluminium pole, an "Airing of the Grievances," and "Feats of Strengths."

event_title: Night Of The Radishes (Noche de Rábanos)
description: In Oaxaca City, Mexico, thousands gather in the plaza to admire an exhibition of radishes that are grown especially and carved into extraordinary sculptures, ranging from nativity scenes to exact models of the city's buildings.

event_title: Apollo 8 mission: First men to orbit the Moon
description: The three astronauts of the Apollo 8 mission spent Christmas Eve 1968 circling the Moon no fewer than ten times, and captured the iconic Earthrise photo.
Christmas Eve has unofficially been declared National Eggnog Day across the USA, where Eggnog is traditionally served over the festive period.
On Christmas Eve in 1914, an unofficial Christmas truce occurred at Ypres. Soldiers from both sides decorated trenches, sang carols, and met in no-man's-land to play football and exchange gifts.

Allegedly, the day Jesus was born in the stable of Bethlehem. Early Christians likely chose this date to align with solstice festivals such as pagan Yule and Roman Saturnalia, and conceivably a later Roman celebration of the sun god, Sol Invictus.

Anniversary of the death of singer, actress, and cabaret star Eartha Kitt. Born on a cotton plantation in the segregated South, she rose to global fame and political activism, notably making the First Lady cry with trenchant anti-war statements during the Vietnam War.
St Stephen, an early martyr, was stoned to death in Jerusalem for praising Jesus in front of a sceptical audience.

event_title: Good King Wenceslas's Act of Charity
description: St Stephen's Day is the day on which Good King Wenceslas went to help a poor peasant with his firewood.
A tradition of giving alms to the poor, Wenceslas-style, likely gave Boxing Day its name.
Wren Day is a long-standing tradition in some Celtic parts of Europe, where people hunt real or fake wrens.
The tradition of giving visiting tradespeople a box of gifts on the day after Christmas.
A mysterious, thick fog smelling of coal tar blanketed London, cutting visibility and disrupting travel. It was caused by pollution from new-fangled factories of the Industrial Revolution.
J.M. Barrie's story 'Peter Pan' first appeared as a play in a London theater.
'Star Wars' premiered in London's West End, with long queues and high ticket prices, sparking 'Star Wars fever'.
US Prohibition, known as the Noble Experiment, ended when the House of Representatives passed the Twenty-first Amendment.

This day commemorates the supposed massacre of innocent children by Herod. In Spain, it's known as Día de los Santos Inocentes, a day similar to April Fool's Day, featuring pranks, made-up stories in media, bonfires, and local traditions like flour battles, egg throwing, burning giant paper dolls, and the Dance of the Fools.
One of the dates observed for National Chocolate Day in the USA, a day to celebrate chocolate.

Piero the Unfortunate (also known as Piero the Unlucky), the oldest son of Lorenzo the Magnificent and a member of the Medici dynasty, passed away by drowning in a river after his boat capsized while he was escaping a battle he had lost. He was known for his spoiled, petulant, and arrogant personality.
The day was named after the sound of a clock, the sound of time moving forward by Thomas and Ruth Roy, who created this day and many more and even founded a holiday company called Wellcat Holidays.
Physics genius Richard Feynman explained to a rapt audience how the Encyclopedia Britannica really could be written on the head of a pin.

The last battle between the indigenous Americans and U.S. troops was back in 1890 on 29th December. The conflict in South Dakota was more of a massacre than a battle - it was lightningly quick, and the majority of Chief Big Foot's 350-strong Sioux army were massacred by the 7th Cavalry.

The race known as 'Hell on High Water' took a radical turn for the worse, with conditions some competitors compared to a hurricane. Waves reached up to 20 meters high; winds reached as much as 150 kph; more than half the competitors turned for home. Six men died, two bodies were never recovered, five boats sank, and 55 sailors had to be rescued.

event_title: Equal Pay Act came into force in the UK
description: Making it illegal to pay a woman less than a man for doing the same job, or give her fewer benefits.

On December 30, 1896, José Rizal, the national hero of the Philippines, was executed by Spanish troops at the age of 35. A polymath, he was an artist, poet, writer, fencer, doctor specializing in ophthalmology, and a campaigner for Filipino nationhood, freedom, and education. Rizal Day is celebrated annually in his honor.

The anniversary of the death of Rasputin, the 'Mad Monk' known for his influence over Russian Emperor Nicolas II and his wife. His death was mysterious, involving multiple attempts to kill him, including being shot, poisoned, rolled in a carpet, and thrown into an icy river.

On December 30, 1813, during the War of 1812, British troops reduced Buffalo, New York, to a smouldering ruin. This act was in retaliation for US forces burning a Canadian town earlier that month. American militia defending the town largely fled when faced with British soldiers and Native American warriors.

event_title: Founding of the East India Company
description: The East India Company was founded on this very day in 1600.
